The columns of QF are simple flows, so Lemma 13 and Proposition 12 imply the columns of UF are simple flows as well; therefore all entries of UF lie in {−1,0,+1}. Since U contains I_s, the matrix UF contains F as a submatrix, so every entry of F is 0 or ±1. But F=Z^{-1}=adj(Z)/det(Z), so the entries of F are the signed (s−1)-minors of Z; hence det(Z′)=±1. Now delete any column i from Q and U, obtaining Q_i and U_i. The preceding paragraph shows Q_i is weakly unimodular, while U_i is totally unimodular and contains I_{s−1}. Also Q_i†Q_i=U_i†U_i. By induction Q_i is totally unimodular. This holds for every deleted column, giving all minors of Q of size at most s−1; the s by s minors are controlled by weak unimodularity. Hence Q is totally unimodular.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 and Corollary 2, reconstruction of regular matroids and 3-connected graphs from flow lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a regular matroid M, let M• be the minor obtained by contracting all co-loops of M. Theorem 1 states: if M and N are regular matroids, then their integer flow lattices Λ(M) and Λ(N) are isometric if and only if M• and N• are isomorphic. Corollary 2 states: if G and H are 3-connected finite graphs, then Λ(G) and Λ(H), the lattices of integer flows of the graphs, are isometric if and only if G and H are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward direction of the easy implication, assume M•≅N•. Choose corresponding bases and coordinatize both co-loop-free minors by the same totally unimodular matrix [I_r L]. If M has p co-loops and N has q co-loops, then M and N can be represented by block matrices with leading identity blocks I_p and I_q for the co-loops and the same [I_r L] block for the rest. Their fundamental flow-basis matrices are [0_{p by s}; −L; I_s] and [0_{q by s}; −L; I_s], which have identical Gram matrices; hence the flow lattices are isometric. Conversely, let ψ:Λ(M)→Λ(N) be an isometry. Choose a fundamental basis β_1,...,β_s of Λ(M) and let U be its coordinate matrix, so U is totally unimodular and contains I_s. Let Q be the coordinate matrix of ψ(β_1),...,ψ(β_s) in a fixed representation of N. The Gram matrices agree, Q†Q=U†U, and Lemma 5 makes Q weakly unimodular. Lemma 16 then implies Q is totally unimodular. By Corollary 15, after permuting rows, the nonzero rows of U♯ and Q♯ are the same matrix X(A), where A=U†U=Q†Q; zero rows correspond to co-loops. Because X(A) contains an identity block, write the nonzero part as [K; I_s] in absolute value. Camion’s lemma allows row and column sign changes so that the nonzero parts of U and Q become identical, say [−L; I_s]. Thus M and N admit representations with the same non-co-loop block [I_r L], while their only difference is the number of co-loops. Therefore M•≅N•. Corollary 2 follows from Whitney’s theorem that 3-connected graphs are isomorphic exactly when their graphic matroids are isomorphic, together with the fact that a 3-connected graph has no cut-edges, so its graphic matroid has no co-loops.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 17 and 18, cut-lattice versions by duality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a regular matroid M represented by A, the lattice of integer cuts is Γ(M)=Row(A)∩Z^E with the induced Euclidean inner product. Let M◦ denote the minor obtained from M by deleting all loops. Corollary 17 states: for regular matroids M and N, Γ(M) and Γ(N) are isometric if and only if M◦ and N◦ are isomorphic. Corollary 18 states: for regular matroids M and N, Λ(M) and Γ(N) are isometric if and only if M• is isomorphic to (N◦)*, equivalently to (N*)•.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper observes that Λ(M) is isometric to Γ(M*) for regular matroids, because if M is represented in coordinates by [I_r L], then the dual M* is represented by [−L† I_s], and the corresponding flow and cut lattices match. Loops of M correspond to co-loops of M*, and contraction/deletion are interchanged by duality. Applying Theorem 1 to the dual matroids gives Corollary 17, and applying the same duality once to compare a flow lattice to a cut lattice gives Corollary 18.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 19, intrinsic characterization of lattices that are regular-matroid flow lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a lattice basis B, call B g-nonnegative, g-positive, or g-feasible when its Gram matrix has that property. A symmetric integer matrix A with positive diagonal is g-feasible if there is a totally unimodular matrix U with U†U=A; it is g-positive if it is g-nonnegative and g_A({i})>0 for every singleton {i}. Proposition 19 states that for an integral lattice Λ, the following are equivalent: (a) Λ has a g-feasible basis; (b) Λ has a basis that is both g-feasible and g-positive; (c) Λ is isometric to Λ(M) for some regular matroid M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a)⇒(b), choose a g-feasible basis with Gram matrix A, and choose a totally unimodular matrix U whose columns have Gram matrix A, equivalently a suitable signing of X(A) after deleting zero rows. Since A has full rank, U has an invertible s by s submatrix Z. Total unimodularity gives det(Z)=±1, so F=Z^{-1} is integral with determinant ±1. Then Q=UF is weakly unimodular and contains I_s, so Lemma 3 makes Q totally unimodular. The columns of Q are another basis of the same lattice, and because Q contains I_s, Proposition 14 implies its Gram matrix is g-positive as well as g-feasible. For (b)⇒(c), take a g-feasible g-positive Gram matrix A and a totally unimodular signing U of X(A) with U†U=A. Since g-positivity supplies unit rows, after row and column sign changes U can be arranged to contain I_s; writing U=[−L; I_s] constructs a regular matroid represented by [I_r L], whose fundamental flow basis has Gram matrix A. Hence Λ is isometric to Λ(M). The implication (c)⇒(b) follows by taking any fundamental basis of Λ(M): Lemma 9 gives a signed-circuit basis, and Proposition 14 shows its Gram matrix is g-feasible and g-positive.

proof_techniques.name: Metric indecomposability detects signed circuits
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines, for an arbitrary integral lattice, simple elements as nonzero elements α for which every decomposition α=β+γ into nonzero lattice elements has β·γ<0. In regular-matroid flow lattices, Proposition 12 proves that these metric simple elements are exactly the simple flows, i.e. signed circuits. The proof uses only the coordinate restrictions of a signed circuit and circuit minimality in one direction, and Tutte’s consistent decomposition in the other direction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The irreducibility condition in the original problem is exactly the negation of the existence of a decomposition with nonnegative cross term, so the paper’s simple-element criterion is directly analogous. A possible route is to identify some vertices with circuit-like minimal objects under an auxiliary representation, or to prove an analogue of Proposition 12 for the tree lattice.

proof_techniques.name: Conformal decomposition by minimal support
proof_techniques.description: Tutte’s decomposition starts by proving that every nonzero flow has a signed circuit contained in its support and with compatible coordinate signs. The key choice is a minimal-support counterexample, then a circuit inside its support, then a coordinate of minimal absolute value on that circuit; subtracting the largest compatible multiple reduces support without reversing signs. Iterating gives a decomposition of any flow into signed circuits that all lie inside the original support and point in the same coordinate directions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a lattice with a preferred graph basis, a similar minimal-support reduction could turn a hypothetical reducible vertex or vector into smaller graph-supported pieces. This may help convert algebraic decomposability into a combinatorial statement about connected supports or minimal subtrees.

proof_techniques.name: Coordinate sign obstruction for decompositions of {−1,0,+1}-vectors
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Proposition 12, if a signed circuit α is written as α=β+γ, then each coordinate of α is 0 or ±1, so integer coordinates force β(e)γ(e)≤0 for every e. Circuit minimality then ensures β and γ overlap somewhere, making at least one coordinate product negative and hence the whole Euclidean inner product negative.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a vertex of the given tree lattice can be embedded or represented as a minimal {−1,0,+1}-supported vector in a positive definite coordinate lattice, this argument would prove its irreducibility. Even without such an embedding, the pattern suggests proving coordinatewise nonpositivity plus unavoidable overlap.

proof_techniques.name: Fundamental circuit bases from matroid bases
proof_techniques.description: Given a regular matroid representation [I_r L], the matrix [−L; I_s] has columns forming a Z-basis of the flow lattice. Each column is the signed circuit associated with adding one non-base element to the chosen base. This provides a basis all of whose elements are metrically simple, and the simple flows span the whole flow lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the vertex basis of the tree lattice can be compared to a fundamental circuit or cocircuit basis, then irreducibility of some or all basis elements may follow from the paper’s signed-circuit argument. This is especially suggestive because the tree form has a graph-indexed Gram matrix.

proof_techniques.name: Recovering support data from the Gram matrix
proof_techniques.description: The paper reconstructs the nonzero row supports of a totally unimodular column matrix U from A=U†U. Pairwise intersections are recovered from |a_ij|, triple sign conditions determine when higher intersections vanish, and an inclusion-exclusion transform g_A counts rows with any prescribed support. Total unimodularity is used critically to rule out a J_t−I_t submatrix whose determinant would be ±(t−1).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original lattice is specified only by its Gram matrix in the vertex basis. If one tries to realize that Gram matrix inside a standard coordinate lattice or as a cut/flow lattice, the g_A support-reconstruction method gives concrete constraints on possible supports and may force the existence of a metric simple basis vector.

proof_techniques.name: Row and column sign normalization
proof_techniques.description: Camion’s lemma says that two totally unimodular signings of the same support matrix differ only by row and column sign switches. The paper combines this with Gram-matrix support reconstruction to upgrade equality of Gram matrices into equality of the underlying matroid representation, up to irrelevant zero rows.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When signs of off-diagonal pairings are fixed by a tree, sign-switching can be a useful normalization device. It may allow a decomposition or embedding problem to be reduced to a canonical all-negative-on-edges form.

proof_techniques.name: Weak-to-total unimodularity via an identity submatrix
proof_techniques.description: Several arguments exploit the fact that a weakly unimodular matrix containing an identity block is automatically totally unimodular. Lemma 16 further shows that if a weakly unimodular matrix has the same Gram matrix as a totally unimodular matrix containing an identity block, then it is totally unimodular; the proof uses induction, lattice isometries, and preservation of simple elements.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed coordinate matrix for the tree lattice has maximal minors controlled and contains a visible identity block, these lemmas suggest a way to obtain strong total-unimodularity conclusions. Such conclusions can convert metric information into graph or matroid structure.

proof_techniques.name: Flow-cut duality
proof_techniques.description: The paper notes that the lattice of integer flows of a regular matroid is isometric to the lattice of integer cuts of its dual. Consequently, all flow-lattice reconstruction results have cut-lattice versions, with co-loops replaced by loops. This is particularly natural for graphic matroids, where cut lattices are closely related to incidence matrices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The Gram matrix of a graph cut basis has diagonal degree terms and negative adjacency terms, resembling the tree-indexed bilinear form in the original problem. The cut-lattice viewpoint may therefore be more directly adaptable than the flow-lattice viewpoint.

proof_techniques.name: Discarding zero rows and co-loops
proof_techniques.description: In the reconstruction theorem, zero rows of a flow-basis matrix correspond exactly to co-loops of the matroid: elements that occur in no flow and do not affect the flow lattice. The proof separates the common nonzero support matrix from arbitrary leading zero rows, showing that the lattice determines only the co-loop-free minor.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If some vertices or coordinates in an auxiliary model contribute no essential decomposability information, it may be useful to isolate and discard them, focusing on the part detected by the bilinear form.

other_useful_info: The paper cites Bacher, de la Harpe, and Nagnibeda, and Biggs, for foundational material on graph flow and cut lattices. It cites Tutte’s work on chain-groups for the conformal decomposition theorem used as Proposition 11. Whitney’s theorem on 2-isomorphism is used to derive the graph corollary for 3-connected graphs. The paper’s examples identify the root lattice A_n with Λ(U_{1,n+1}) and with Γ(U_{n,n+1}), and identify the body-centered cubic lattice with Λ(K_4) and Γ(K_4); these examples may be useful reference points for recognizing familiar positive definite lattices as regular-matroid flow or cut lattices.
why_relevant: Gives structural results for graphic and regular-matroid flow lattices, a major source of graph-lattice techniques used by Greene. Its circuit/flow viewpoint can help translate decomposability of lattice vectors into connectivity properties of graphs.

title: The Lens Space Realization Problem
overall_summary: Greene proves the lens space realization theorem: every lens space obtained by positive integer surgery from a knot in S3 is realized, at the level of the relevant homology class and knot Floer data, by a Berge knot. The proof combines Heegaard Floer correction terms, Donaldson diagonalization, and a detailed combinatorial classification of changemaker lattices whose orthogonal complements are linear plumbing lattices. For the original problem, the most useful material is the lattice-theoretic core in Sections 3 and 4: irreducible elements in graph lattices are characterized by connected subsets with connected complements; vertex-basis elements in linear lattices are irreducible; changemaker standard-basis elements are proved irreducible by coordinate sign arguments; and intersection graphs of irreducible interval representatives satisfy strong forbidden-configuration constraints. Although the paper does not treat arbitrary weighted trees with one bad vertex directly, it supplies several portable methods for proving irreducibility of distinguished generators in positive-definite graph/plumbing lattices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.1 observation: minimal-norm irreducible basis and pairing-graph decomposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a positive-definite integral lattice, meaning a finitely generated free abelian group equipped with a positive-definite symmetric bilinear pairing ⟨ , ⟩ taking integer values. For v in L write |v| = ⟨v,v⟩. A nonzero vector v is reducible if v = x + y for nonzero x,y in L with ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0, and irreducible otherwise. Then L has a basis S = {v1,...,vn} consisting entirely of irreducible vectors, obtained by choosing v1 of minimal positive norm and then choosing vi of minimal norm among vectors not in the span of v1,...,v_{i-1}. If S is an irreducible basis and its pairing graph has vertices S and edges between pairs with nonzero pairing, then the spans of the connected components of this graph are indecomposable orthogonal summands of L. The decomposition into indecomposable summands is unique up to reordering, by Eichler’s uniqueness theorem as cited in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the irreducible-basis construction, suppose vi is chosen of minimal norm outside the span W of the previous vectors and vi = x + y with x,y nonzero and ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0. Positive definiteness gives |vi| = |x| + |y| + 2⟨x,y⟩, so |x| < |vi| and |y| < |vi|. By minimality of vi, both x and y must lie in W, forcing vi in W, a contradiction. Thus every selected vi is irreducible. For the pairing-graph decomposition, different graph components are orthogonal by definition, so their spans form an orthogonal direct sum. If the span Lk of one connected component decomposed nontrivially as L′ ⊕ L″, then every irreducible basis vector in that component must lie wholly in one summand, since a vector with nonzero components in both summands would split orthogonally and hence be reducible. Connectedness of the component then forces all its basis vectors into the same summand, contradicting nontriviality. The cited Eichler theorem gives uniqueness of the resulting indecomposable decomposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.2 construction: graph lattices and norm formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = (V,E) be a finite loopless undirected graph, allowing parallel edges, and write e(v,w) for the number of edges between distinct vertices v,w. Define Γ(G) to be the free abelian group generated by symbols [v], v in V, with symmetric bilinear form given by ⟨[v],[v]⟩ = d(v), the degree of v, and ⟨[v],[w]⟩ = -e(v,w) for v ≠ w. For x = Σ_v x_v [v], one has |x| = Σ_{edges e=(v,w)} (x_v - x_w)^2. Hence Γ(G) is positive semidefinite, and its nullspace is generated by the sums [V_i] over connected components V_i of G. The graph lattice associated to G is the quotient Γ(G) = Γ(G)/Z(G), where Z(G) is this nullspace; it is positive definite. If G is connected and r is a chosen root vertex, then Γ(G) is identified with the span of {[v] : v ≠ r}, giving a vertex basis.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Expanding the defining form gives Σ_v d(v)x_v^2 - 2Σ_{edges (v,w)} x_v x_w, which is exactly Σ_{edges (v,w)}(x_v - x_w)^2. This expression is nonnegative and vanishes precisely when x_v is constant on each connected component. Thus the radical consists of the component-sums [V_i]. After quotienting by this radical the form is positive definite. In the connected case, the relation [V] = 0 in the quotient lets every class be written uniquely using all vertex generators except a fixed root r.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a connected finite loopless undirected graph with chosen root r, and let Γ(G) be its graph lattice with vertex basis {[v] : v in V minus {r}}. For a subset T of V, write [T] for the class Σ_{v in T}[v] in Γ(G), understanding that [T] = -[V minus T] if r lies in T. Then the irreducible elements of Γ(G) are exactly the vectors ±[T] for which both induced subgraphs G|T and G|(V minus T) are connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First show every irreducible vector has the form ±[T]. Write a nonzero x in the rooted representative as x = Σ_{v ≠ r} c_v [v]. Replacing x by -x if necessary, assume c = max c_v ≥ 1, and let T = {v : c_v = c}. Then T does not contain the root. A direct calculation gives ⟨[T], x - [T]⟩ = Σ_{v outside T} (c - 1 - c_v)e(v,T), which is nonnegative because c_v ≤ c-1 outside T. Since x is irreducible, the decomposition x = [T] + (x - [T]) forces x = [T]. Now determine when [T] is irreducible. For y = Σ y_v[v], with the rooted convention y_r = 0, the paper uses the identity ⟨y, y - [T]⟩ = Σ_C Σ_{(u,v) in E(C)}(y_u - y_v)^2 + Σ_{(u,v) in E(T,V minus T)}(y_u - y_v)(y_u - y_v - 1), where C ranges over connected components of G|T and G|(V minus T). Each summand is nonnegative for integer y_u - y_v. If G|(V minus T) is disconnected, choose a component C not containing r and put y = -[C]; then y and [T] - y are nonzero, orthogonal, and sum to [T], so [T] is reducible. If G|T is disconnected, choose a component C of G|T and put y = [C], again splitting [T] orthogonally. Conversely, if both induced subgraphs are connected and [T] = y + z with ⟨y,z⟩ ≥ 0, then ⟨y,y-[T]⟩ = -⟨y,z⟩ ≤ 0, while the displayed formula is nonnegative, so all summands vanish. Connectivity forces y to be constant on T and on V minus T, with value 0 on the root side and value either 0 or 1 on T; hence y is 0 or [T]. Thus no nontrivial reducible decomposition exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a connected graph with no cut-edge, with graph lattice Γ(G) rooted at r. Suppose a class [T] in Γ(G) satisfies [T] = y + z for y,z in Γ(G) with ⟨y,z⟩ = -1. Then exactly one of the following structural alternatives occurs. First, G|T contains a cut-edge e such that, writing the vertex sets of the two components of G|T minus e as T1 and T2, the unordered pair {y,z} equals {[T1],[T2]}. Second, G|(V minus T) contains a cut-edge e such that, writing the vertex sets of the two components of G|(V minus T) minus e as T1 and T2 with r in T2, the unordered pair {y,z} equals {[T1 union T], -[T1]}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the same identity as in Proposition 3.3. Since [T] = y + z and ⟨y,z⟩ = -1, one has ⟨y,y-[T]⟩ = 1. The summands (y_u-y_v)(y_u-y_v-1) across the cut between T and its complement are always 0 or at least 2 for integer differences, while the internal edge summands are squares. Therefore exactly one internal edge, either inside G|T or inside G|(V minus T), contributes 1, and every other term vanishes. This unique edge must be a cut-edge of the relevant induced subgraph. If the edge lies in G|T, then y is constant on each of the two components T1,T2 and on V minus T. Since G itself has no cut-edge, each Ti has some edge to V minus T, forcing the constants on T1 and T2 to differ from the outside constant by at most one and to differ from each other by exactly one; after using the root normalization, the only possibility is {y,z} = {[T1],[T2]}. If the edge lies in G|(V minus T), the same constant-value analysis, with the component T2 containing the root normalized to value 0, gives {y,z} = {[T1 union T], -[T1]}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = direct sum over k of L_k be a sum of linear lattices, where each L_k has a vertex basis x1,...,xn with pairings ⟨xi,xi⟩ = a_i ≥ 2, ⟨xi,x_{i+1}⟩ = -1, and all other off-diagonal pairings zero. In such a linear summand, an interval means a consecutive set {xi,...,xj}, and [T] is the sum of the basis vectors in the interval. Then: (1) the irreducible vectors in L are exactly ±[T], where T is an interval in one summand; in particular every vertex-basis element xi is irreducible; (2) each linear summand L_k is indecomposable; (3) if two intervals T and T′ overlap but do not share a common endpoint, then [T minus T′] ± [T′ minus T] is reducible; (4) [T] is unbreakable, meaning it is not expressible as x+y with |x|,|y| ≥ 3 and ⟨x,y⟩ = -1, if and only if T contains at most one vertex of norm at least 3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Represent each linear lattice as a graph lattice by taking a path of vertices x1,...,xn, adding a root r, and adding enough edges from r to xi so that the graph degree of xi is a_i. Proposition 3.3 then says irreducibles correspond to connected subsets with connected complement. In this graph model those subsets are exactly intervals in a single path summand, proving (1). The pairing graph of the interval singleton basis in each summand is connected and the basis vectors are irreducible, so the indecomposability argument from Section 3.1 proves (2). For (3), if T and T′ overlap without common endpoint, their symmetric difference is a union of two distant intervals T1 and T2. Thus [T minus T′] ± [T′ minus T] is a sum of two nonzero orthogonal interval classes up to signs, hence reducible. For (4), if T contains two vertices of norm at least 3, split T into consecutive subintervals T1,T2 each containing one such vertex; then [T]=[T1]+[T2], both norms are at least 3, and ⟨[T1],[T2]⟩=-1, so [T] is breakable. Conversely, if [T] is breakable, Proposition 3.4 applies in the graph model. The case where the cut-edge lies in the complement would yield one summand of norm 2, incompatible with breakability. Thus the cut-edge lies inside T, splitting T into two intervals of norms at least 3, and each such interval must contain a vertex of norm at least 3. Hence T contains at least two high-norm vertices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T = {T1,...,Tk} be a collection of intervals in a linear lattice such that the interval classes [Ti] are linearly independent. Define a graph G(T) whose vertices are the intervals Ti and whose edges join pairs of intervals that abut, meaning that they are consecutive or share a common endpoint. If C is a cycle in G(T), then all intervals in V(C) abut pairwise at a common end: there is an index j such that every Ti in V(C) has left endpoint x_{j+1} or right endpoint x_j. In particular, V(C) induces a complete subgraph of G(T).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the number of edges in the subgraph induced by the intervals of the cycle. If the cycle is induced and no three intervals abut at a common end, orient the cyclic list T1,...,Tk and assign signs ε_i = ±1 according to whether Ti lies to the right of Ti−1 or shares the appropriate common endpoint. For each basis vertex x_j, the contributions of intervals containing x_j cancel in pairs because the next interval around the cycle containing x_j has the opposite sign. Hence Σ_i ε_i[Ti] = 0, contradicting linear independence. Thus the base case is a triangle with all three intervals abutting at a common end. If the induced subgraph has a chord, split the cycle into two smaller cycles along the chord and apply induction to each; both force the same common end for the chord intervals, hence for all intervals in the original cycle.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Subsection 3.4 construction: changemaker lattice standard basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ = (σ0,...,σn) in Z^{n+1} be a changemaker, meaning 1 = σ0 ≤ σ1 ≤ ... ≤ σn and every integer between 0 and σ0+...+σn is a subset sum of the σi; equivalently σi ≤ σ0+...+σ_{i-1}+1 for every i ≥ 1. In the Euclidean lattice Z^{n+1} with orthonormal basis e0,...,en, let L = (σ)⊥ be the orthogonal complement of σ. For each 1 ≤ j ≤ n, define v_j as follows. If σ_j = 1 + Σ_{i=0}^{j-1} σ_i, set v_j = -e_j + 2e0 + Σ_{i=1}^{j-1} e_i and call it tight. Otherwise choose, among all subsets A of {0,...,j-1} with σ_j = Σ_{i in A} σ_i, the subset maximal in binary order, and set v_j = -e_j + Σ_{i in A} e_i. In the latter case v_j is called gappy if A is not a set of consecutive integers and just right if A is consecutive. Then S = {v1,...,vn} is a basis of L, called the standard basis.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each v_j is orthogonal to σ by construction. The vectors are triangular with coefficient -1 on e_j and no e_i with i > j, so they are linearly independent. To see that they span L, take any w in L and successively eliminate its e_n, e_{n-1},...,e_1 coordinates by adding suitable integer multiples of v_n, v_{n-1},...,v_1. The resulting vector lies in L and is supported only on e0, hence is zero because σ0 = 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the standard basis S = {v1,...,vn} of a changemaker lattice L = (σ)⊥ as above, write v_{j,k} = ⟨v_j,e_k⟩. The following hold. (1) v_{j,j} = -1 for all j, and v_{j,j-1} = 1 unless j = 1 and v_{1,0} = 2. (2) For any pair vi,vj in S, one has ⟨vi,vj⟩ ≥ -1. (3) If k is a gappy index for a gappy vector v_j, meaning k lies in the chosen subset A but k+1 does not lie in A union {j}, then |v_{k+1}| ≥ 3. (4) If z in L has |z| ≥ 3, has exactly one negative coordinate, namely z_j = -1, then j is the maximum index in supp(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Part (1) is immediate from the triangular construction and the maximal choice of A: except in the tight one-dimensional case, the largest possible previous index j-1 must appear. Part (2) follows because each v_j has coefficient -1 in its own new coordinate and nonnegative coefficients in earlier coordinates, so two different standard-basis vectors can have at most one negative contribution to their inner product. For (3), if v_{k+1} had norm 2, then v_{k+1} would allow replacing the occurrence of e_k in v_j by a lexicographically larger subset representation of σ_j, contradicting maximality of A. For (4), if some k > j lay in supp(z) with positive coefficient, then 0 = ⟨z,σ⟩ would be strictly greater than σ_k - σ_j, which is nonnegative since σ is nondecreasing; this contradiction proves that no support occurs beyond j.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every standard-basis element v_j of a changemaker lattice L = (σ)⊥ is irreducible in L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume v_j = x + y with x,y in L nonzero and ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0, and write coordinates in the orthonormal e_i basis. If v_j is not tight, then every coordinate of v_j is 0, 1, or -1. If any coordinate product x_i y_i were positive, then the corresponding coordinate of v_j would have absolute value at least 2, impossible. Since ⟨x,y⟩ is the sum of these products and is nonnegative, all coordinate products must be zero. Because v_j has only one negative coordinate, one of x and y has all coordinates nonnegative. A nonzero vector in (σ)⊥ cannot have all coordinates nonnegative because all σ_i are positive, so one summand is zero, contradiction. If v_j is tight, the only coordinate where both summands can have the same positive sign is e0, forcing x0 = y0 = 1. There can be at most one coordinate k with x_k y_k = -1; otherwise the inner product would be negative. If no such coordinate exists, the previous nonnegative-coordinate argument applies. If such k exists, assume x_k = 1 and y_k = -1. Then y has its unique negative coordinate at k, while x has its unique negative coordinate at j. Since tight v_j has nonzero coordinates for all indices up to j, this cancellation coordinate k must satisfy k > j. But x has norm at least 3 and unique negative coordinate at j, so Lemma 3.12(4) says j is the maximum index in supp(x), contradicting k > j. Thus v_j is irreducible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.14 and 3.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = (σ)⊥ be a changemaker lattice with standard basis S. Suppose v_t is tight. Then the paper proves three additional irreducibility tests: (1) if v_j is also tight and j ≠ t, then v_j - v_t is irreducible; (2) if j > t and v_j = -e_j + e_{j-1} + e_t, then v_j + v_t is irreducible; (3) if v_{t+1} = -e_{t+1} + e_t + ... + e0, then v_{t+1} - v_t is irreducible. Separately, if v_j is not tight, then v_j is unbreakable: there do not exist x,y in L with v_j = x+y, |x|,|y| ≥ 3, and ⟨x,y⟩ = -1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proofs are coordinate-sign refinements of Lemma 3.13. For v_j - v_t with two tight vectors, one may assume j > t; the vector has a coefficient 2 at e_t, positive coefficients between t+1 and j-1, and coefficient -1 at e_j. A reducible decomposition with nonnegative pairing would force both summands to have e_t coefficient 1 and a single cancellation coordinate k. Lemma 3.12(4), applied to the summand with unique negative coordinate, forces incompatible inequalities among k,t,j. The cases v_j+v_t and v_{t+1}-v_t are similar: the possible coordinate products are so constrained that one summand would have a unique negative coordinate whose index is not maximal, or would pair with σ with the wrong sign. For unbreakability of a non-tight v_j, a breakable decomposition v_j=x+y with ⟨x,y⟩=-1 and |x|,|y|≥3 has exactly one coordinate where x_i y_i=-1 and all other coordinate products zero. Lemma 3.12(4) then forces the negative-coordinate indices to be maximal in contradictory ways, and the maximality built into the standard-basis choice gives v_j strictly smaller than one summand in the binary order, impossible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2 and Corollary 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume a changemaker lattice L = (σ)⊥ with standard basis S is isomorphic to a linear lattice or a direct sum of linear lattices. By Lemma 3.13 and Corollary 3.5, each v_i in S can be written as ε_i[T_i], where ε_i is ±1 and T_i is an interval in a linear summand. If v_t is tight, then: (1) no other standard-basis vector is tight; (2) if j > t+1 and v_j = -e_j + e_{j-1} + e_t, then the intervals T_t and T_j are consecutive; (3) if v_{t+1} = -e_{t+1} + e_t + ... + e0, then t = 1 and T_1 and T_2 are consecutive. Consequently, using Lemma 3.15, a standard basis contains at most one breakable vector, and if it exists it is tight.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), if v_j and v_t were both tight, then Lemma 3.14 says v_j - v_t is irreducible. But the pairings of two tight vectors force their interval representatives to overlap transversely in the linear-lattice model, and Corollary 3.5(3) says the corresponding interval difference is reducible, a contradiction. For (2), the coordinate pairing gives ⟨v_t,v_j⟩=-1 and |v_j|=3. The interval alternatives are either that T_t and T_j are consecutive, or that the unique high-norm vertex of T_j lies inside T_t with transverse overlap. The latter would make ε_j v_j - ε_t v_t equal a reducible symmetric-difference vector, while Lemma 3.14 says v_j+v_t is irreducible; hence only consecutiveness remains. For (3), the same argument applies to v_{t+1}-v_t, using Lemma 3.14 and excluding the transverse-overlap alternative. Finally, Lemma 3.15 says every non-tight standard-basis vector is unbreakable, and (1) says there is at most one tight vector, so at most one standard-basis vector can be breakable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4 and Corollary 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same setting where a changemaker lattice with standard basis S is isomorphic to a sum of linear lattices, let v_i = ε_i[T_i] and v_j = ε_j[T_j] be unbreakable standard-basis elements with |v_i|,|v_j| ≥ 3. Then |⟨v_i,v_j⟩| ≤ 1. Equality holds if and only if the intervals T_i and T_j are consecutive, and in that case ε_i ε_j = -⟨v_i,v_j⟩. As a consequence, if T_i and T_j are distinct unbreakable intervals of norm at least 3, then their unique high-norm vertices z_i and z_j are distinct.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The main point is to prove ⟨[T_i],[T_j]⟩ ≤ 0. If this interval pairing were positive, unbreakability implies that T_i and T_j share the same unique vertex of degree at least 3. If one vector is tight, direct coordinate bounds show that the changemaker pairing has absolute value at most d-3 while the interval pairing has size at least d-2, impossible. If neither vector is tight and the signs ε_i,ε_j agree, then positive pairing forces either one interval nearly to contain the other or a transverse overlap with almost identical supports; in the first case one obtains a lexicographically larger allowed standard-basis vector such as v_j - v_i, and in the second case v_j - v_i is irreducible by the same coordinate argument as Lemma 3.13 but reducible by Corollary 3.5(3). If the signs are opposite, the only remaining positive interval-pairing case has norm 3 and transverse overlap; then v_j+v_i is coordinate-irreducible but corresponds to a reducible symmetric-difference vector. These contradictions force nonpositive interval pairing. The statement about equality follows because for unbreakable intervals of norms at least 3, the only way to get pairing -1 is for the intervals to be consecutive, with the sign relation determined by v_i = ε_i[T_i]. The corollary follows immediately: if two such intervals shared the same high-norm vertex, their interval pairing would be positive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.8, 4.10, and 4.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of a changemaker lattice L = (σ)⊥ isomorphic to a sum of linear lattices, remove the possible single breakable standard-basis vector and call the remaining set S_bar. Write each v_i in S_bar as ε_i[T_i], with T_i an interval in a linear summand. Define the intersection graph G(S_bar) with vertices S_bar and edges between v_i,v_j when the intervals T_i,T_j abut. Then: (1) G(S_bar) is claw-free, meaning it has no induced K_{1,3}; (2) G(S_bar) contains no heavy triple, where a heavy triple is a set of three distinct norm-at-least-3 vertices in the same connected component such that none separates the other two in the graph; (3) every cycle in G(S_bar) has length 3 and contains a unique vector of norm 2. Moreover, if (v_i,v_j,v_k) is such a cycle with i < j < k and v_k is not gappy, then all v_l with l ≤ i have norm 2, except that if a breakable vector v_t was removed the conclusion is for t < l ≤ i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Claw-freeness is purely interval-theoretic: if one interval abuts three others, at least two of those abut it at the same end, and therefore they abut each other, so the claw is not induced. For the heavy-triple exclusion, place the relevant intervals in the same path of the linear lattice. Each unbreakable interval of norm at least 3 contains a unique high-norm vertex. If z_i lies between z_j and z_k along the path, then any unbreakable interval avoiding z_i lies entirely to one side of it; hence v_i separates v_j from v_k in the intersection graph, contradicting the definition of heavy triple. For cycles, Lemma 3.8 first implies that the intervals in any cycle induce a complete subgraph, all abutting pairwise at a common end. Such a complete subgraph cannot contain three norm-at-least-3 vertices because that would be a heavy triangle. Norm-2 standard-basis vectors themselves induce only paths, and two norm-2 vertices in the same cycle would have forms v_{i+1}=-e_{i+1}+e_i and v_i=-e_i+e_{i-1}; any third interval abutting both would contradict maximality of the standard-basis representation by allowing replacement by v_j-v_i or v_j-v_{i+1}. Thus a cycle has exactly two high-norm vertices and one norm-2 vertex, so it is a triangle. The final initial-segment norm-2 assertion follows by inspecting the support of the largest-index non-gappy vector in such a triangle and observing that any earlier high-norm vector would create a forbidden heavy triple.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = (σ)⊥ be a changemaker lattice with standard basis S, isomorphic to a linear lattice or a sum of linear lattices. Suppose v_s is chosen with s smallest such that |v_s| ≥ 3, and suppose v_s is not tight. Then v_s is just right, and |v_s| is either s or s+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If v_s were gappy, then by Lemma 3.12(3) a gappy index k for v_s would force |v_{k+1}| ≥ 3 with k+1 < s, contradicting the minimal choice of s. Thus v_s is just right. A just-right vector has the form -e_s + e_{s-1} + ... + e_k. If |v_s| < s, then k lies between 2 and s-2. The vectors v_{k-1}, v_{k+1}, and v_s all abut v_k in the intersection graph while not all abutting one another, giving an induced claw. This contradicts Lemma 4.8. Therefore the consecutive support has length s or s+1, i.e. |v_s| is s or s+1.

proof_techniques.name: Minimal-norm irreducibility induction
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the elementary fact that in a positive-definite lattice a vector chosen with minimal norm outside a fixed sublattice cannot split as x+y with ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0. Such a splitting would make both x and y have strictly smaller norm than the chosen vector, forcing them into the previous span and contradicting linear independence. This gives an irreducible basis in every positive-definite lattice and supplies a way to prove irreducibility by contradiction using norm minimality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a positive-definite weighted tree lattice, one can try choosing vertices or combinations with minimal norm or minimal support and showing that any reducible splitting would produce smaller-norm lattice elements confined to a previously controlled sublattice. This is especially relevant if an induction on deleting leaves or arms is set up.

proof_techniques.name: Graph-laplacian energy formula
proof_techniques.description: For graph lattices with diagonal entries equal to vertex degrees and off-diagonal entries minus edge multiplicities, Greene uses the identity |Σ x_v[v]| = Σ_{edges (v,w)}(x_v-x_w)^2. This turns lattice inequalities into combinatorial statements about integer functions on vertices. The formula also identifies the radical as functions constant on connected components and makes connectivity conditions visible in the quadratic form.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original tree form differs from the graph-laplacian form by diagonal corrections w(v)-d(v). Since the hypothesis includes a vertex with w(v)<d(v), comparing the given form to the graph-laplacian energy may expose vectors of small or nonpositive norm under an assumption that all vertices are reducible.

proof_techniques.name: Connected-subset criterion for irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.3 proves irreducibility in graph lattices by showing that any irreducible must be ±[T], and that [T] is irreducible exactly when both T and its complement induce connected subgraphs. The key calculation is the nonnegative sum formula for ⟨y,y-[T]⟩, whose vanishing forces y to be constant on connected components. Disconnected induced subgraphs give explicit orthogonal decompositions, while connectedness prevents them.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A weighted tree proof may imitate this by analyzing a putative vertex decomposition u=a+b through the connected components of the support of a or b after deleting u. The bad-vertex condition might be used to rule out all possible component-wise splittings unless some vertex is irreducible.

proof_techniques.name: Linear-lattice interval model
proof_techniques.description: Linear plumbing lattices are modeled as graph lattices of a path plus a root. This converts irreducible elements into interval sums and makes vertex-basis irreducibility immediate. Breakability is also interval-theoretic: an interval class is breakable exactly when the interval contains at least two vertices of norm at least 3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any tree can be decomposed into path arms from a chosen vertex. The interval model suggests proving irreducibility first along arms or using interval sums as canonical pieces in any reducible decomposition of a vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Coordinate-sign obstruction in Euclidean embeddings
proof_techniques.description: For changemaker lattices L=(σ)⊥ inside a standard Euclidean lattice, standard-basis vectors have one negative coordinate and controlled nonnegative coordinates. Greene proves these vectors irreducible by observing that a reducible splitting with nonnegative pairing severely restricts coordinate products; one summand must have all coordinates nonnegative, or a unique negative coordinate whose index violates a support-maximality lemma. This is a powerful local sign-pattern argument independent of the global classification.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree lattice can be embedded into a diagonal lattice, or if local vertex vectors can be represented with one negative coordinate and positive tails, this sign argument can be ported to show specific vertices cannot split reducibly.

proof_techniques.name: Comparing two irreducible bases via intervals
proof_techniques.description: When a changemaker lattice is also a sum of linear lattices, every changemaker standard-basis vector is irreducible and hence must be an interval sum in the linear-lattice vertex basis. Greene studies the resulting collection of intervals, using signs, overlaps, and endpoint relations to derive strong restrictions. This comparison is the engine behind the classification of standard bases.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A possible strategy for the original problem is to assume no vertex is irreducible, choose another irreducible basis by minimal norm, and compare the original vertex generating set to irreducible pieces. Forbidden interval or support configurations may then contradict the presence of a bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Intersection graph forbidden configurations
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes interval representatives of irreducible standard-basis vectors in an intersection graph whose edges mean the intervals abut. The graph is claw-free, has no heavy triples, and has only triangular cycles with a unique norm-2 vertex. These restrictions are derived from interval combinatorics plus irreducibility and unbreakability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Weighted trees with a high-valence or bad vertex naturally create claw-like local configurations. If reducibility of all vertices forces interval representatives around that vertex, the paper’s claw-free and heavy-triple principles suggest a route to contradiction.

proof_techniques.name: Expansion and reduction of standard bases
proof_techniques.description: Greene’s later sections classify standard bases by repeatedly applying expansions, operations that add a new vector of the form -e_m+e_{m-1}+...+e_k under norm-2 chain conditions. Expansions preserve the linear/changemaker structure and reduce complicated bases to small initial configurations. Although the detailed classification is lens-space-specific, the method is a disciplined induction on adding or deleting leaves in a plumbing-like lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, an induction that deletes leaves or contracts norm-2 chains may mirror the expansion-reduction method. One could try to reduce to a small bad-vertex configuration where irreducibility of a vertex is direct.

other_useful_info: Terminology from the paper that may be useful: a vector is breakable if it splits as x+y with |x|,|y| ≥ 3 and ⟨x,y⟩ = -1; otherwise it is unbreakable. In linear lattices, breakability of an interval is exactly the presence of two high-norm vertices in that interval. The paper cites Milnor-Husemoller for uniqueness of indecomposable lattice decompositions, Gerstein for isomorphism classification of linear lattices, and Lisca’s work for expansion-style combinatorial analysis of lattice embeddings.
why_relevant: Introduces changemaker lattices and the combinatorial analysis of standard bases in positive-definite lattices. These methods are central to later proofs detecting irreducible elements in plumbing and graph lattices.

title: Lattices, Graphs, and Conway Mutation
overall_summary: The paper proves that, for connected graphs, the lattice-theoretic d-invariant of the cut or flow lattice determines the graph up to 2-isomorphism, and applies this to show that reduced alternating link diagrams with the same branched-cover Heegaard Floer homology are Conway mutants. The core combinatorial-lattice techniques are: realizing graph Laplacian-type forms as cut lattices inside a standard diagonal lattice, classifying short characteristic covectors of cut lattices by graph orientations, gluing complementary lattices using discriminant or d-invariant data, applying Elkies's theorem to force a glued unimodular positive definite lattice to be standard, and then using support-counting to prove rigidity of embeddings into standard lattices. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the graph-lattice dictionary: incidence vectors ∂*v have diagonal degree and off-diagonal negative adjacency, exactly the unweighted Laplacian pattern, and the paper develops methods for detecting when a lattice with graph-like pairings must come from such an incidence construction. The short-covector inequalities and support-recovery arguments are also plausible tools for proving that a graph-shaped positive definite lattice cannot have all basis vertices decomposable, especially when a vertex weight violates the degree-equality pattern of a cut lattice.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.1 construction (cut and flow lattices and incidence pairings)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = (V,E) be a finite loopless undirected graph, possibly with parallel edges, and fix an arbitrary orientation O0 of every edge. Let C1(G;Z) be the free abelian group generated by E, equipped with the standard positive definite inner product making the edges orthonormal, and let C0(G;Z) be the free abelian group generated by V, with vertices orthonormal. If an oriented edge e points from w to v, set ∂e = v − w, and let ∂* : C0(G;Q) → C1(G;Q) be the adjoint, so ∂*v = Σ_e <∂e,v> e. The cut lattice is C(G) = im(∂*) ∩ C1(G;Z), and the flow lattice is F(G) = ker(∂) ∩ C1(G;Z). Over Q there is an orthogonal splitting C1(G;Q) = im(∂*) ⊕ ker(∂). From the definitions, for vertices u,v one has <∂*u,∂*v> = deg(u) if u = v, and <∂*u,∂*v> = −m(u,v) if u ≠ v, where m(u,v) is the number of edges joining u and v. Changing the initial orientation O0 only changes signs of edge basis vectors, so it preserves the isomorphism types of C(G) and F(G).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Adjointness gives <∂*x,y> = <x,∂y>, hence im(∂*) is orthogonal to ker(∂), and finite-dimensional linear algebra gives im(∂*) = ker(∂)^⊥ over Q. The formula for ∂*v follows by inspecting each oriented edge: an edge entering v contributes +e, an edge leaving v contributes −e, and a nonincident edge contributes 0. Thus the norm of ∂*v counts incident edges, while an edge joining two distinct vertices u and v contributes opposite signs to ∂*u and ∂*v, hence contributes −1 to their pairing. Reversing an edge orientation replaces the corresponding basis vector e by −e in all incidence vectors, an isometry of the standard edge lattice.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Short-covector inequality (implicit in Section 2.4 and used in Proposition 3.3)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Λ be a positive definite integral lattice, let Char(Λ) be its characteristic covectors, and let χ ∈ Char(Λ) be short in its class modulo 2Λ, meaning |χ| ≤ |χ + 2y| for every y ∈ Λ in the same class. Then for every x ∈ Λ one has |<χ,x>| ≤ |x|. In the proof of Proposition 3.3 this is applied to Λ = C(G) and x = Σ_{v∈T} ∂*v for arbitrary subsets T of vertices, giving |<χ,Σ_{v∈T} ∂*v>| ≤ |Σ_{v∈T} ∂*v|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since χ is short, both |χ + 2x| ≥ |χ| and |χ − 2x| ≥ |χ| hold. Expanding gives |χ + 2x| − |χ| = 4<χ,x> + 4|x| ≥ 0, hence <χ,x> ≥ −|x|. Similarly |χ − 2x| − |χ| = −4<χ,x> + 4|x| ≥ 0, hence <χ,x> ≤ |x|. Combining the two inequalities gives the stated absolute-value bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.6 (Elkies)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Λ be a rank n integral, unimodular, positive definite lattice. If every characteristic covector χ ∈ Char(Λ) satisfies |χ| ≥ n, then Λ is isomorphic to the standard diagonal lattice Z^n; equivalently, Λ admits an orthonormal basis.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem from Elkies and does not prove it. Its role in the paper is decisive: after gluing two positive definite lattices to obtain an integral unimodular lattice whose d-invariant vanishes, the definition of d implies that all characteristic covectors have norm at least the rank, so Elkies's theorem forces the glued lattice to be Z^n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4 (lattice gluing from discriminant forms)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an integral lattice Λ, write Λ* for its dual, Λbar = Λ*/Λ for its discriminant group, and bΛ(xbar,ybar) = <x,y> mod 1 for its discriminant bilinear form. Suppose Λ1 and Λ2 are complementary primitive sublattices of an integral unimodular lattice Λ, meaning they are orthogonal, their ranks add to rk(Λ), and the restriction maps Λ* → Λi* are surjective. Then there is a natural isomorphism of discriminant forms φ : (Λ1bar,b1) → (Λ2bar,−b2). Conversely, if Λ1 and Λ2 are integral lattices and φ : (Λ1bar,b1) → (Λ2bar,−b2) is an isomorphism of discriminant forms, then the glue lattice Λ1 ⊕_φ Λ2 = {x + y ∈ Λ1* ⊕ Λ2* : φ(x mod Λ1) = y mod Λ2} is integral and unimodular, and contains Λ1 and Λ2 as complementary primitive sublattices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the forward direction, the isomorphism is described as follows: for a class xbar ∈ Λ1*/Λ1, choose ybar ∈ Λ2*/Λ2 such that x + y lies in Λ; then set φ(xbar) = ybar. Complementarity, primitivity, and unimodularity ensure this is well-defined and bijective. Since x + y belongs to the integral unimodular lattice Λ and Λ1 is orthogonal to Λ2, the discriminant pairings satisfy b1(xbar,xbar′) + b2(ybar,ybar′) = 0 in Q/Z, giving the sign reversal. Conversely, the displayed set is an overlattice of Λ1 ⊕ Λ2. The condition b2(φ(x),φ(x′)) = −b1(x,x′) implies that all pairings in the overlattice are integral. The quotient of the glue lattice by Λ1 ⊕ Λ2 is the graph of φ inside Λ1bar ⊕ Λ2bar, a subgroup of square-root order in the full discriminant group, so the glued lattice has determinant 1. The coordinate projections onto Λi* are surjective by construction, giving primitivity of Λi in the glue lattice.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5 and Proposition 2.7 (characteristic-coset and d-invariant gluing)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an integral lattice Λ, define Char(Λ) = {χ ∈ Λ* : <χ,y> ≡ |y| mod 2 for all y ∈ Λ}, define C(Λ) = Char(Λ) mod 2Λ, and define ρ([χ]) = (|χ| − σ(Λ))/4 mod 2, where σ(Λ) is the signature. If Λ1 and Λ2 are complementary primitive sublattices of an integral unimodular lattice Λ, then there is a natural isomorphism (C(Λ1),ρ1) → (C(Λ2),−ρ2). Conversely, an isomorphism of these ρ-invariants gives, via the induced discriminant-form isomorphism, an integral unimodular glue lattice containing Λ1 and Λ2 as complementary primitive sublattices. In the positive definite case, where dΛ([χ]) = min{(|χ′| − rk(Λ))/4 : χ′ ∈ [χ]}, Proposition 2.7 says more: if Λ1 and Λ2 are complementary primitive sublattices of Z^n and the restriction Short(Z^n) → Short(Λ1) is surjective, then (C(Λ1),d1) is naturally isomorphic to (C(Λ2),−d2). Conversely, if positive definite integral lattices Λ1 and Λ2 have an isomorphism (C(Λ1),d1) → (C(Λ2),−d2), then the corresponding glue lattice is isomorphic to Z^n, and the restrictions Short(Z^n) → Short(Λi) are surjective for i = 1,2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 2.5, restrict characteristic covectors of the unimodular lattice Λ to the primitive complementary sublattices. A key algebraic point is the identity 2Λ ∩ (2Λ1 + Λ2*) = 2Λ1 + 2Λ2, after identifying the orthogonal complement of Λ1 with Λ2*. This ensures that if two characteristic covectors restrict to the same class in C(Λ1), then their restrictions to Λ2 define the same class in C(Λ2), so the map on characteristic cosets is well-defined. Van der Blij's theorem says the ρ-invariant of a unimodular lattice vanishes, and applying it to χ1 + χ2 ∈ Char(Λ) gives ρ1([χ1]) + ρ2([χ2]) = 0 mod 2. The converse uses Lemma 2.3, which recovers the discriminant form from ρ by the identity <x,y> = (|χ + 2x + 2y| + |χ| − |χ + 2x| − |χ + 2y|)/8 modulo 1, and then applies the discriminant-form gluing lemma. For Proposition 2.7, set ni = rk(Λi). In the forward direction, choose χ1 ∈ Short(Λ1), and by surjectivity lift it to χ1 + χ2 ∈ Short(Z^n) with χ2 ∈ Short(Λ2). Since every short characteristic covector of Z^n has norm n, one obtains 0 = (|χ1 + χ2| − n)/4 = (|χ1| − n1)/4 + (|χ2| − n2)/4 = d1([χ1]) + d2([χ2]). This proves the d-invariants are opposite. Conversely, if the d-invariants are opposite, the glue lattice is integral and unimodular by the previous gluing results. For any short χ = χ1 + χ2 in the glue lattice, the same displayed equality and the d-invariant hypothesis give |χ| = rk(Λ1) + rk(Λ2). Elkies's theorem then identifies the glue lattice with Z^n. Finally, given χ1 ∈ Short(Λ1), choose χ2 ∈ Short(Λ2) in the corresponding class; the equality again gives χ1 + χ2 ∈ Short(Z^n), proving surjectivity of the short-covector restrictions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.8 with Lemmas 2.9–2.11 (rigid embeddings)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Λ be a lattice with a chosen basis BΛ. For i = 1,2, let Zi be a standard positive definite lattice with an orthonormal basis Bi, and let ιi : Λ → Zi be an embedding. Assume that the first embedding has 0/1 coordinates on the chosen bases, i.e. <ι1(x),e> ∈ {0,1} for every x ∈ BΛ and e ∈ B1, and that every e ∈ B1 appears in the support of some ι1(x). Assume also that the restriction maps Zi* → Λ* induce surjections Short(Zi) → Short(Λ) for i = 1,2. Then there exists an isometric embedding ι : Z1 → Z2 such that ι2 = ι ∘ ι1. In particular, under these hypotheses the 0/1 coordinate-support pattern of the first embedding forces the second embedding to contain a relabeled copy of it.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For z ∈ Zi define supp+(z), supp−(z), and supp(z) using the orthonormal basis Bi. For Y ⊂ BΛ define S(Y) to be the set of short covectors χ of Λ satisfying <χ,y> = |y| for all y ∈ Y, and define Si(Y) analogously in Zi. Surjectivity gives S(Y) = ri(Si(Y)). For x ∈ BΛ define M(x,Y) and m(x,Y) as the maximum and minimum of <χ,x> over χ ∈ S(Y), and set D(x,Y) = (M − m)/2. Because ι1 has 0/1 coordinates, the short covectors of Z1 maximizing or minimizing are obtained by choosing signs on basis vectors, and one gets D(x,Y) = |supp(ι1(x)) − ⋃_{y∈Y} supp(ι1(y))|. Lemma 2.9 compares this formula with the corresponding extrema in Z2 for Y = empty set: D(x,empty) equals both Σ_f |<ι2(x),f>| and |ι2(x)| = Σ_f <ι2(x),f>^2, forcing all coordinates <ι2(x),f> to be 0 or ±1 and giving |supp(ι1(x))| = |supp(ι2(x))|. Lemma 2.10 uses Y = {y} to show <x,y> = |supp(ι1(x)) ∩ supp(ι1(y))| = |supp(ι2(x)) ∩ supp(ι2(y))| for all basis elements x,y. Lemma 2.11 then observes that the pairing <ι2(x),ι2(y)> is a sum of ±1 terms over the common support, but the sum equals the number of common support coordinates; therefore every such term is +1. Thus, for each coordinate f, all nonzero coefficients <ι2(x),f> have the same sign as x varies, and flipping signs of basis elements f gives an orthonormal basis B2′ in which all coefficients of all ι2(x) are 0 or 1. With 0/1 coordinates on both sides, the same D(x,Y) formula holds for i = 1 and i = 2. Inclusion-exclusion applied to D recovers the cardinality of each atom, meaning the set of coordinates that lie in exactly a specified subcollection X of the supports supp(ιi(x)) and in none of the others. The hypothesis that every coordinate of B1 appears in some support makes these atoms partition B1. Choose arbitrary bijections between corresponding atoms in B1 and B2′ and extend linearly; the resulting isometric embedding Z1 → Z2 carries each support supp(ι1(x)) onto supp(ι2(x)), hence satisfies ι2 = ι ∘ ι1 on the basis BΛ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1 (fundamental cut and cycle bases)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite loopless graph with a chosen orientation O, and let F be a maximal spanning forest. For each forest edge ei ∈ E(F), removing ei from F separates the relevant tree into components K1 and K2; orient the fundamental cut cut(F,ei) from K1 to K2, and define xi = Σ_{ej∈cut(F,ei)} εj ej ∈ C(G), where εj = ±1 according as the cut orientation agrees or disagrees with O. For each nonforest edge ei ∈ E(G) − E(F), the graph F ∪ ei contains a unique fundamental cycle cyc(F,ei); orient it cyclically so that ei keeps its orientation from O, and define xi = Σ_{ej∈cyc(F,ei)} εj ej ∈ F(G). Let BC be the set of xi associated to forest edges, and BF the set of xi associated to nonforest edges. Then C(G) and F(G) are complementary primitive sublattices of C1(G;Z), BC is a basis for C(G), and BF is a basis for F(G).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let C′ and F′ be the spans of BC and BF. If ei and ej are both forest edges, then <xi,ej> = δij, because the fundamental cut of ei contains ei and no other forest edge in the same distinguished way. Similarly, if ei and ej are both nonforest edges, then <xi,ej> = δij for the fundamental cycle basis. Therefore BC and BF are linearly independent, and the relevant edge subsets evaluate as dual bases, proving primitivity of C′ and F′ in the edge lattice. Orthogonality of cuts and flows gives rk C(G) + rk F(G) ≤ rk C1(G;Z) = |E|, while |BC| + |BF| = |E|, so the rational spans must already be im(∂*) and ker(∂). Any element x ∈ C(G) can be written over Q as Σ_{ei∈E(F)} qi xi; pairing with the forest edge ei gives qi = <x,ei> ∈ Z, so x lies in C′. The same argument with nonforest edges shows F′ = F(G). Hence the displayed sets are bases, and the sublattices are primitive and complementary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 (spanning forest with 0/1 cut and flow bases)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every finite loopless graph G there exists a maximal spanning forest F and an orientation O1 of G such that, for the fundamental cut basis BC from Proposition 3.1, every coefficient satisfies <xi,ej> ∈ {0,1} for all xi ∈ BC and all edges ej. For the same forest F there is also an orientation O2 of G such that, for the fundamental cycle basis BF, every coefficient satisfies <xi,ej> ∈ {0,1} for all xi ∈ BF and all edges ej.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper constructs F by a layered root-set procedure. A root set contains one vertex in each connected component. Starting with G1 = G, choose a root set R1. Having chosen Ri in Gi, remove Ri to get Gi+1, choose a root set Ri+1 in Gi+1 so that every vertex of Ri+1 has a unique chosen neighbor in Ri, and include one distinguished edge from each such vertex to its chosen neighbor. Inductively, the resulting distinguished edges form a forest F, and reverse induction shows it is maximal. If an edge joins vi ∈ Ri to vj ∈ Rj with i < j, orient it from vi to vj; call this orientation O1. Then every edge in every fundamental cut of a forest edge points consistently with the cut orientation, so the cut-basis coefficients are all 0 or 1. To obtain O2, reverse the orientation of every nonforest edge and keep the forest-edge orientations; then every edge in every fundamental cycle of a nonforest edge points consistently with the cycle orientation, so the flow-basis coefficients are all 0 or 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.3 (short covectors of cut lattices)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix an initial orientation O0 of a finite loopless graph G. The short characteristic covectors of the full edge lattice C1(G;Z) are exactly the covectors χO with edge coefficients ±1, equivalently orientations O of G: χO pairs with an edge basis vector as +1 if O agrees with O0 on that edge and −1 otherwise. The restriction of χO to the cut lattice C(G) is called an orientation covector, and it satisfies <χO,∂*v> = indegO(v) − outdegO(v). Proposition 3.3 states that Short(C(G)) consists precisely of these orientation covectors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One direction uses the general fact that if C(G) and F(G) are complementary primitive sublattices of the standard edge lattice, then the restriction of a short characteristic covector of the standard lattice to C(G) is short; hence every orientation covector is short. Conversely, let χ ∈ Short(C(G)). The short-covector inequality gives |<χ,Σ_{v∈T} ∂*v>| ≤ |Σ_{v∈T} ∂*v| for every subset T ⊂ V. Define integer target indegrees l(v) by the parity-compatible formula l(v) = (deg(v) + <χ,∂*v>)/2 for the sign convention <χO,∂*v> = indeg − outdeg; equivalently, the paper uses the opposite sign and then the globally reversed orientation. The inequalities imply l(V) = |E| and l(T) ≤ e(T) for every T ⊂ V, where e(T) is the number of edges with at least one endpoint in T. Indeed, the norm of Σ_{v∈T} ∂*v counts edges crossing the cut between T and its complement, and the inequality with the appropriate sign rearranges exactly to l(T) ≤ number of edges incident to T. Build a bipartite graph whose left part has l(v) copies of each vertex v and whose right part is E; a copy of v is adjacent to an edge e if v is an endpoint of e. The inequalities l(T) ≤ e(T), together with l(V) = |E|, are precisely Hall-type conditions ensuring a perfect matching from all vertex-copies to edges. Direct each edge toward the endpoint copy matched to it. Then indeg(v) = l(v), so indeg(v) − outdeg(v) = <χ,∂*v> for every v, and since the ∂*v generate C(G), χ is the orientation covector of this orientation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.4 (cut/flow short-covector surjectivity and opposite d-invariants)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every finite loopless graph G, the restriction maps from the standard edge lattice C1(G;Z) to the cut and flow lattices induce surjections Short(C1(G;Z)) → Short(C(G)) and Short(C1(G;Z)) → Short(F(G)). Moreover, the inclusion C(G) ⊕ F(G) ⊂ C1(G;Z), with C(G) and F(G) complementary primitive sublattices, induces a natural isomorphism of positive definite lattice d-invariants (C(C(G)),dC) → (C(F(G)),−dF).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 3.3 proves surjectivity onto Short(C(G)) by realizing every short covector of the cut lattice as the restriction of an orientation covector of the full edge lattice. Since C(G) and F(G) are complementary primitive sublattices of the standard lattice by Proposition 3.1, the observation preceding Proposition 2.7 implies that surjectivity of the short-covector restriction to one complementary summand is equivalent to surjectivity to the other. Proposition 2.7 then applies to the complementary primitive sublattices C(G) and F(G) inside C1(G;Z) ≅ Z^{|E|}, giving the d-invariant isomorphism with opposite signs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.8 (graph lattices and 2-isomorphism)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G and G′ be connected finite loopless graphs. Let F(G) and C(G) denote their flow and cut lattices. Write Λ ≃ Λ′ if Λ ⊕ Z^k ≅ Λ′ or Λ ≅ Λ′ ⊕ Z^k for some k. Let T(G) be the set of edges contained in some cycle; a 2-isomorphism between connected graphs is a cycle-preserving bijection T(G) → T(G′). Then the following are equivalent: (1) G and G′ are 2-isomorphic; (2) F(G) ≅ F(G′); (3) C(G) ≃ C(G′); (4) the d-invariants of F(G) and F(G′) are isomorphic; and (5) the d-invariants of C(G) and C(G′) are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implications from graph 2-isomorphism to lattice isomorphism are based on Proposition 3.7, which shows that switches preserve flow lattices and cut lattices, while contracting a cut-edge preserves the flow lattice and changes the cut lattice by adding or removing a Z summand. The implications from lattice isomorphism to d-invariant isomorphism are formal, and the equivalence of the cut and flow d-invariants follows from Corollary 3.4. The main work is proving that an isomorphism of cut-lattice d-invariants implies 2-isomorphism. First contract all cut-edges, since this preserves 2-isomorphism type, reducing to the case where both graphs are 2-edge-connected. An isomorphism (C(C(G)),dC) ≅ (C(C(G′)),dC′), composed with the cut-flow opposition for G′, gives an isomorphism (C(C(G)),dC) → (C(F(G′)),−dF′). Proposition 2.7 glues C(G) and F(G′) along this data to a standard lattice Z2 and gives surjectivity of short-covector restrictions. Now apply the rigid embedding Proposition 2.8 twice: once to the embedding of C(G) into the standard edge lattice C1(G;Z), and once to the embedding of F(G′) into C1(G′;Z). Lemma 3.2 supplies the required 0/1 bases, and 2-edge-connectivity ensures every edge appears in some cut for G and in some cycle for G′, satisfying the support condition. Repeating the construction with G and G′ interchanged gives inequalities forcing the ranks of the edge lattices and glued standard lattices to match, so the embeddings into the glued lattices are actually isomorphisms. Their composite gives an isometry f : C1(G;Z) → C1(G′;Z) carrying the edge orthonormal basis bijectively to the edge orthonormal basis and carrying F(G) isomorphically onto F(G′). If C is a cycle in G, its signed edge sum is a flow vector with coefficients 0,±1; applying f gives a 0,±1 flow in G′, hence an Eulerian subgraph, decomposable into directed cycles. Applying the same argument to f^{-1} shows that a cycle cannot map to a proper union of cycles; therefore the induced edge bijection preserves cycles. This is the desired 2-isomorphism.

proof_techniques.name: Incidence realization of graph-like negative pairings
proof_techniques.description: The paper represents a graph by the adjoint incidence vectors ∂*v inside the standard edge lattice. These vectors have norm equal to vertex degree and pair as minus the number of edges between distinct vertices. This converts graph connectivity and adjacency into lattice pairings, while retaining access to the ambient diagonal lattice C1(G;Z).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original tree lattice has the same off-diagonal pattern as these incidence vectors, but with arbitrary vertex weights on the diagonal. Comparing a weighted tree lattice to the incidence model highlights that the condition w(v) < d(v) is exactly a failure of the cut-lattice degree diagonal at v; a proof by contradiction might show that absence of irreducible vertices forces such a cut-lattice-like degree pattern.

proof_techniques.name: Short characteristic covectors give subset inequalities
proof_techniques.description: A short characteristic covector χ satisfies |<χ,x>| ≤ |x| for every lattice element x. In the cut-lattice setting, choosing x to be a sum of incidence vectors over a vertex subset turns this into an inequality controlled by the number of cut edges around the subset. The paper uses this to turn lattice minimality into graph-theoretic Hall inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a positive definite weighted tree lattice, one can try to choose extremal or short characteristic covectors and apply the same inequality to sums of vertices over subtrees. The bad vertex condition w(v) < d(v) may produce a subset inequality that is incompatible with a hypothetical decomposition pattern for every vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Hall-matching conversion from lattice inequalities to orientations
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 3.3, after deriving inequalities l(T) ≤ e(T) for every vertex subset T, the paper builds a bipartite graph between prescribed vertex-copies and edges. Hall's theorem gives a perfect matching, which is then interpreted as an orientation with prescribed indegrees, proving that every short covector of a cut lattice comes from an orientation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem can be reduced to prescribed local counts around vertices, Hall's theorem may convert lattice inequalities into a global orientation or flow on the tree. Since trees have especially simple cuts, this technique may produce a sharp contradiction at the unique bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Gluing plus Elkies to force a standard diagonal lattice
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly glues two positive definite lattices with opposite d-invariants to obtain an integral unimodular positive definite lattice. The d-invariant equality forces all characteristic covectors in the glued lattice to have norm at least the rank, and Elkies's theorem then identifies the glued lattice with Z^n. This turns abstract lattice data into coordinate-support data in a standard diagonal lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a complement or auxiliary lattice can be naturally attached to the weighted tree lattice, a gluing argument could place the problem inside a standard diagonal lattice. Irreducibility of a vertex might then be tested via coordinate supports or projections.

proof_techniques.name: Support recovery from short-covector extrema
proof_techniques.description: The rigid embedding argument defines D(x,Y) as half the difference between the maximum and minimum of <χ,x> over short covectors satisfying constraints on Y. In a 0/1 embedding, D(x,Y) exactly counts coordinates in supp(x) outside the union of supports of Y. Inclusion-exclusion then recovers all support atoms and forces any other embedding with the same short-covector behavior to be a coordinate relabeling.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a graph-shaped lattice, decomposability of basis vectors can often be studied through possible coordinate-support decompositions in an ambient diagonal lattice. This support-counting method suggests a way to prove that a basis vertex with certain degree/weight imbalance cannot be decomposed in the required nonnegative-pairing way.

proof_techniques.name: Spanning-forest sign normalization
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.2 chooses a maximal spanning forest and orientations so that all fundamental cut-basis or flow-basis coefficients are 0 or 1. This removes sign ambiguity and lets the paper apply support-cardinality arguments rather than signed cancellation arguments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, there is already a canonical spanning forest: the tree itself. Orienting edges away from a root or toward a distinguished bad vertex may similarly normalize signs and make decompositions of vertex sums easier to control.

other_useful_info: Section 3.3 identifies Short(C1(G;Z)) with all orientations of G; under this identification, the value on ∂*v is indeg(v) − outdeg(v). For a connected graph, the incidence vectors satisfy Σ_{v∈V} ∂*v = 0, so deleting one vertex gives a standard reduced graph-Laplacian presentation of the cut lattice with diagonal degrees and off-diagonal negative edge multiplicities. The paper cites Bacher-de la Harpe-Nagnibeda [BdlHN97] for earlier cut/flow lattice results, Hakimi [Hak65] and Schrijver [Sch03] for the orientation/degree-sequence theorem underlying Proposition 3.3, and Elkies [Elk95] for the diagonal-lattice characterization.
why_relevant: Develops the graph-lattice theory on which later irreducibility arguments are based. The paper contains structural results about positive-definite graph lattices, obtuse bases, and how lattice data encodes graph connectivity.

title: Deficiency Symmetries of Surgeries in S3
overall_summary: The paper proves a symmetry theorem comparing the Heegaard Floer correction-term deficiencies of rational negative surgery S^3_{-p/q}(C) on a knot C with those of a related integral negative surgery S^3_{-n}(C), where p=nq-r. It then uses this symmetry, Ozsvath-Szabo's characteristic-covector calculus for negative-definite linear plumbings, and Donaldson diagonalisation to prove a rational-surgery changemaker theorem generalising several results of Greene. The most relevant material for a purely lattice-theoretic problem is not the knot Floer input itself, but the combinatorial machinery: push-down operations on weighted linear graphs, classification of maximising characteristic covectors by no-full-tank conditions, and the way sharpness plus Donaldson forces plumbing rows in a lattice embedding to have entries 0, ±1 with controlled overlaps. The proof of the changemaker theorem also contains reusable row-vector arguments in which the existence of many ±1 characteristic covectors forces strong constraints on supports and subset-sum, changemaker structure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C be a knot in S^3 and let p,q>0 be coprime. Write p=nq-r with 0<r<q. For a Spin^c structure t on S^3_{-p/q}(C), define the deficiency D_C^{p/q}(t)=d(S^3_{-p/q}(C),t)-d(S^3_{-p/q}(U),t), using the standard identification with the corresponding surgery on the unknot U; define D_C^n analogously for -n surgery. Then there is a function r: Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C)) -> Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C)) such that D_C^{p/q}(t)=D_C^n(r(t)) for every t. All fibres of r have size q except one exceptional fibre, lying over a Spin^c structure on S^3_{-n}(C) that minimises D_C^n; in the construction this exceptional fibre has size q-r. Consequently conjugation on Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C)), under which D_C^n is invariant, lifts to a function on Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C)) under which D_C^{p/q} is invariant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has two independent ingredients whose equality forces the map. First, using the Ni-Wu formula for rational-surgery deficiencies in terms of the monotone sequences V_i and H_i from knot Floer homology, the paper proves the exact sum identity sum_{Spin^c rational} D_C^{p/q} = q sum_{Spin^c integral} D_C^n - r min D_C^n. Second, the paper analyses the negative-definite linear plumbing W' for S^3_{-p/q}(U) with continued fraction p/q=[a_1,...,a_ell], a_1=n. Ozsvath-Szabo's algorithm gives a set K of maximising characteristic covectors with no full tanks. This set is modified to a set K' by pushing down those K whose restriction to G-v_1 is left-full; each push-down preserves the square and hence remains a maximiser. For each representative K' one obtains, from the factorisation S^3 -> S^3_{-n}(C) -> S^3_{-p/q}(C) and the negative-definite cobordism inequality, an inequality D_C^n(v_{K'}) <= D_C^{p/q}(w_{K'}), where v_{K'} is determined by the first coordinate of K' and w_{K'} is the rational Spin^c structure. Counting first coordinates in K' gives q representatives over each integral Spin^c structure except the central minimising one, where there are q-r. Summing all these inequalities gives the same right-hand side as the Ni-Wu sum identity, so every individual inequality is equality. The desired map r sends the rational Spin^c structure represented by K' to the integral Spin^c structure determined by its first coordinate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Y=S^3_{-p/q}(C) for a knot C in S^3 and coprime p,q>0. Write p/q=[a_1,...,a_ell] in Hirzebruch-Jung form with a_i>=2 for i>=2, and set n=a_1. Let W be the trace of the corresponding negative-definite integral surgery diagram with linear chain weights -a_i, where the first component is tied to C as in Figure 1 of the paper. Suppose that -Y bounds a sharp, simply connected, negative-definite smooth 4-manifold X, that H_2(X) is free, and that Q_X is its intersection form. If the deficiency d(Y,i)-d(S^3_{-p/q}(U),i) vanishes for one Spin^c structure i when n is odd, or for q-r+1 Spin^c structures when n is even in the non-integral notation p=nq-r, then there exists an integral matrix A such that -A A^t = Q_X direct_sum Q_W. If q != 1, A can be chosen so that its last ell rows, corresponding to the W summand, have the staircase changemaker form displayed in the paper: the first of these rows is of the form (sigma_s,...,sigma_1,1,0,...,0), the rows corresponding to vertices v_i for i=2,...,ell have exactly a_i nonzero entries, all equal to ±1, adjacent such rows overlap in exactly one column with opposite signs, non-adjacent such rows do not overlap, and row v_2 overlaps the first row in the single displayed 1-column with opposite sign. The nonnegative integers sigma_1,...,sigma_s form a changemaker set, meaning every integer from 0 to their total sum is a subset sum. If q=1, the last row can be chosen in the form (sigma'_s,...,sigma'_1,sigma'_0), where sigma'_0,...,sigma'_s form a changemaker set.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof begins by applying the gluing and Donaldson setup summarised in Proposition 4.2.
