Since X and W glue to a closed negative-definite 4-manifold, Donaldson identifies its intersection lattice with the standard negative diagonal lattice, yielding an integral matrix A with -A A^t=Q_X direct_sum Q_W. Let the last ell rows of A be x,y_2,...,y_ell, corresponding to the vertices v_1,v_2,...,v_ell of the surgery chain. Lemma 4.3 says that whenever K is a maximising characteristic covector for a Spin^c structure with zero deficiency, K is obtained by pairing one of the rows with a vector alpha all of whose entries are ±1. For i>=2 the paper chooses explicit maximising covectors K with <K,v_i>=a_i and first coordinate among the values guaranteed by Corollary 4.1 to have zero deficiency. Pairing with alpha gives -sum_j alpha_j y_{i,j}=a_i=sum_j y_{i,j}^2, and each summand y_{i,j}^2+alpha_j y_{i,j} is nonnegative; hence every entry y_{i,j} is 0 or ±1, with exactly a_i nonzero entries. To control overlaps, the paper chooses further maximisers with prescribed coordinates at two vertices. For non-adjacent rows y_i and y_j, a maximiser with large values at both forces an alpha that is -1 on the support of y_i and also realises the maximum for y_j; using y_i dot y_j=0 then shows their supports are disjoint. For adjacent rows, a maximiser with coordinates a_i and a_{i+1}-2 forces exactly one shared support column, and the entries there have opposite signs because y_i dot y_{i+1}=-1 in the positive Euclidean row convention. The first row x is then analysed by comparing maximal possible first coordinates among families of zero-deficiency maximisers with <K,v_i>=a_i, a_i-2, or -a_i. For i>=3 these comparisons, together with x dot y_i=0, force x to vanish on the support of y_i. For i=2 they force x to have exactly one nonzero entry on the support of y_2, equal to -1 after signs, giving the displayed single overlap. Finally, the possible first coordinates for zero-deficiency maximisers with <K,v_2>=-a_2 form an entire parity interval. Writing these coordinates as 1+sum sigma_i - 2 sum chi_i sigma_i, with chi_i in {0,1}, shows that the subset sums of the sigma_i realise every integer between 0 and sum sigma_i; this is exactly the changemaker condition. The cases 0<p<q and q=1 are handled by simplifying the same argument: for n=1 all relevant rational deficiencies vanish once one does, and when q=1 there are no rows y_i, so only the changemaker argument for x remains.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1 and Proposition 2.2 (Ni-Wu formula as used in the paper)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a knot C in S^3, the knot Floer complex gives nonnegative integer sequences V_i and H_i associated to the maps v_i^+ and h_i^+. These satisfy V_0=H_0, the sequence V_i is non-increasing in i, and the sequence H_i is non-decreasing in i. For coprime p,q>0 and for the standard labelling t_i of Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C)), the deficiency is D_C^{p/q}(t_i)=2 max{ V_floor(i/q), H_floor((i-p)/q) }.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The monotonicity and equality V_0=H_0 are quoted from Ni and Wu, as is the deficiency formula. In this paper these facts are used as a finite combinatorial formula: after writing rational Spin^c labels as t_{iq+j}, the floor functions split the sum over rational Spin^c structures into q-r copies of the integral expression and r shifted copies. The monotonicity of V_i and H_i is then the only property needed to identify the contribution of the shifted copies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C be a knot in S^3 and let p,q>0 be coprime with p=nq-r and 0<r<q. Then sum_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C))} D_C^{p/q}(t) = q sum_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C))} D_C^n(t) - r min_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C))} D_C^n(t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For integral -n surgery, Proposition 2.2 gives sum D_C^n = 2 sum_{i=0}^{n-1} max{V_i,H_{i-n}}. For rational -p/q surgery, write labels as t_{iq+j}, with 0<=j<q and 0<=i<=n-1-delta(j), where delta(j)=floor((j+r)/q). Thus delta(j)=0 for q-r values of j and delta(j)=1 for r values. The delta=0 terms exactly reproduce the integral sum. For delta=1 one must compare sum_{i=0}^{n-2} max{V_i,H_{i-n+1}} with the integral sum. Choose the largest index i' at which max{V_i,H_{i-n}} is minimal, and split into the two cases V_{i'}>=H_{i'-n} and H_{i'-n}>=V_{i'}. Monotonicity of V and H shows that the shifted sum equals the integral max-sum minus m, where 2m is the minimal integral deficiency. Multiplying by 2 and accounting for the r shifted j-values gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For integral -n surgery on a knot C, using the standard labels t_i on Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C)), if n is even then t_{n/2} realises the minimal deficiency D_C^n, while if n is odd then t_{(n-1)/2} and t_{(n+1)/2} realise the minimal deficiency.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The label t_i evaluates modulo 2n as n+2i, so conjugation sends t_i to t_{n-i}; deficiencies are invariant under conjugation. If t_i is a minimiser with i not equal to 0, then its conjugate t_{n-i} is also a minimiser. Assuming i<=n-i, monotonicity of V and H implies every t_j with i<=j<=n-i also has deficiency at most the same minimum, hence equal to it; therefore the central label or labels are minimisers. If t_0 is a minimiser, then D_C^n(t_0)=2 max{V_0,H_{-n}}=2V_0 because V_0=H_0>=H_{-n}. Since V_i<=V_0 and H_{i-n}<=H_0=V_0, t_0 actually has maximal deficiency; thus if it is also minimal, all deficiencies are equal and the central labels are again minimisers.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be the negative-definite linear graph with vertices v_1,...,v_ell, weights w(v_i)=-a_i, and p/q=[a_1,...,a_ell] with a_i>=2 for i>=2. A characteristic covector K has coordinates k_i=<K,v_i> congruent to -a_i modulo 2. Suppose K satisfies -a_i+2 <= k_i <= a_i for every i. A vertex v_i is a peak if k_i=a_i. K has a full tank if there exist peaks v_i and v_j with i<j and k_m=a_m-2 for all i<m<j. Then the Spin^c structures on S^3_{-p/q}(U), equivalently on the boundary of the plumbing, are represented by exactly those such K with no full tanks; the paper denotes this collection by K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Ozsvath-Szabo's algorithm says that characteristic covectors satisfying the initial inequalities represent ker U in HF^+ precisely when they initiate maximising paths under push-downs. Pushing down at a peak v_i means replacing K by K+2 PD[v_i], which changes the i-coordinate from a_i to -a_i and increases adjacent coordinates by 2. If K has a full tank between peaks v_i and v_j, pushing down successively from v_i toward v_j produces an overshoot at v_j, ending with coordinate a_j+2; hence the path is non-maximising. Conversely, assume K has no full tank. The proof inducts on the number of peaks appearing in K and its push-downs. If there is no peak, the path is already terminal and maximising. If there is a peak at v_i, push it down. If neither neighbour becomes a peak, the number of peaks decreases and no full tank is created. If a peak propagates to one side, continue pushing in that direction; the absence of full tanks guarantees this propagation stops before overshooting. If peaks propagate in both directions, push both ways, repeating as needed when a_i=2, until the number of peaks drops. The induction then gives a maximising path. Since the boundary lens space is an L-space, these maximising covectors give all Spin^c structures without repetition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With G, Q, W' and K as above, where Q is the weighted adjacency matrix of the negative-definite plumbing W' with boundary S^3_{-p/q}(U), each Spin^c structure t on S^3_{-p/q}(U) has a unique representative K in the no-full-tank set K such that [K]=t, and this K is a maximiser for t, meaning K Q^{-1} K^t is maximal among characteristic covectors restricting to t. Moreover d(S^3_{-p/q}(U),t) = (K Q^{-1} K^t + b_2(W'))/4.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 3.3 identifies the no-full-tank initial covectors with Ozsvath-Szabo's maximising representatives for ker U. Because S^3_{-p/q}(U) is a lens space and hence an L-space, ker U contains one generator for each Spin^c structure, so there is no repetition. The correction-term formula is the standard Ozsvath-Szabo formula for a sharp negative-definite plumbing: for each boundary Spin^c structure, the d-invariant is the maximum of (K^2+b_2)/4 over extensions, and the representative K in K attains that maximum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the no-full-tank set of characteristic covectors for the linear plumbing associated to p/q=[a_1,...,a_ell], and write a_1=n and p=nq-r with 0<r<q. Restrict a covector K in K to its first coordinate <K,v_1>. If this first coordinate is not n, then for each allowed first-coordinate value the restriction map has exactly q preimages in K. If the first coordinate is n, then there are exactly q-r elements of K with that first coordinate.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a first coordinate not equal to n. The remaining coordinates must form a characteristic covector on the subchain G-v_1 satisfying the same initial inequalities, initiating a maximising path, and having no full tanks. By Lemma 3.3 this number is the number of Spin^c structures on the lens space corresponding to the continued fraction [a_2,...,a_ell]=q/r, namely q. There are n-1 first-coordinate values other than n, accounting for (n-1)q elements. Since the total number of elements of K is |Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(U))|=p=nq-r, the remaining first-coordinate-n elements number q-r.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the rational-surgery setup, let F be any complete set of representatives of the equivalence classes of characteristic covectors for the plumbing graph G, and assume each K_i in F is a maximiser for its boundary Spin^c structure. Let s_i be the Spin^c structure on the full trace W(C) with c1(s_i)=K_i. Let w_i=s_i restricted to S^3_{-p/q}(C), and let v_i=s_i restricted to S^3_{-n}(C) through the natural factorisation of the trace S^3 -> S^3_{-n}(C) -> S^3_{-p/q}(C). Then 0 <= D_C^n(v_i) <= D_C^{p/q}(w_i) for every i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Ozsvath-Szabo inequality for a negative-definite cobordism W from Y_1 to Y_2 gives (c1(s)^2+b_2(W))/4 <= d(Y_2,s|Y_2)-d(Y_1,s|Y_1). Apply this first to the second-stage cobordism W_2(C) from S^3_{-n}(C) to S^3_{-p/q}(C). For the unknot cobordism W_2(U), equality holds for the corresponding maximiser by the correction-term calculation for the sharp plumbing; since the intersection form is independent of C, subtracting the unknot equality from the inequality for C gives D_C^n(v_i) <= D_C^{p/q}(w_i). Applying the same inequality to the first-stage cobordism W_1(C) from S^3 to S^3_{-n}(C), and comparing with the unknot case, gives 0 <= D_C^n(v_i).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7 with Corollaries 3.8 and 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a characteristic covector for the negative-definite linear graph G, not necessarily in the no-full-tank set. If <K,v_i>=a_i, so v_i is a peak, and K'=K+2 PD[v_i] is the push-down at v_i, then (K')^2=K^2, where square is computed using Q^{-1}. Consequently, any push-down of a maximising representative K in K is also a maximiser for the same boundary Spin^c structure, and the set M of all maximisers is closed under push-downs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the basis dual to the vertices, PD[v_i] is the i-th row of the intersection matrix Q, so PD[v_i] Q^{-1}=e_i. Therefore (K+2 PD[v_i]) Q^{-1} (K+2 PD[v_i])^t = K Q^{-1} K^t + 4 e_i K^t + 4 e_i PD[v_i]^t. Here e_i K^t=<K,v_i>=a_i, and e_i PD[v_i]^t=Q_{ii}=-a_i. The two extra terms cancel, giving equal squares. Since push-down does not change the boundary Spin^c class and preserves square, it preserves the property of maximising that square within the class.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any knot C in S^3 and p=nq-r with 0<r<q, the deficiencies satisfy sum_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C))} D_C^{p/q}(t) >= q sum_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C))} D_C^n(t) - r min_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C))} D_C^n(t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof constructs a new complete family K' of maximising characteristic covectors from the no-full-tank family K. If K in K has first coordinate j with -1<=j<n and the restriction of K to G-v_1 is left-full, let v_k be the first peak in that restriction and replace K by K+2 sum_{i=1}^k PD[v_i]. This push-down is a maximiser by Lemma 3.7 and increases the first coordinate from j to j+2. All other K are left unchanged. For a fixed first-coordinate value j, exactly r elements are nudged upward: by Lemma 3.5 there are q restrictions on G-v_1 and q-r of them are not left-full when the first coordinate is n, so the left-full count is r. Thus K' has q elements over each integral first-coordinate value except the central value, namely -1 when n is odd or 0 when n is even, where it has q-r. Applying Lemma 3.6 to every member of K' and summing gives the stated inequality; Lemma 2.4 identifies the exceptional central value as one with minimal integral deficiency.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With the notation used to prove Theorem 1.1, let t be a Spin^c structure on S^3_{-p/q}(C), let K be a maximiser representing t, and let K' be the corresponding representative in the adjusted maximiser family K'. Then D_C^{p/q}(t) is minimal if the first coordinate <K',v_1> is 0 or ±1. If n is even and there are q-r+1 choices of t at which D_C^{p/q} is minimal, this minimality also extends to representatives with first coordinate ±2. In either case, if D_C^{p/q}(t)=0 for at least one t, then the minimal deficiency is zero.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 1.1 identifies D_C^{p/q}(t) with the integral deficiency D_C^n of the Spin^c structure determined by the first coordinate of K'. Lemma 2.4 says the central integral Spin^c structures, represented by first coordinates 0 and ±1 according to parity conventions, minimise D_C^n. When n is even, the first-coordinate-0 fibre has only q-r representatives, so having q-r+1 rational minima forces at least one additional adjacent fibre to be minimal; the same monotonicity argument used in Lemma 2.4 shows that first coordinates ±2 are the next possible ones. The final assertion uses the non-negativity 0<=D_C^n from Lemma 3.6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Y be a closed 3-manifold obtained by integral surgery on an ell-component link L with negative-definite linking matrix Q and trace W. Let Y' be obtained by integral surgery on a possibly different link L' with the same linking matrix Q, and suppose the trace W' is sharp. Let X be a sharp negative-definite smooth 4-manifold with boundary -Y, with H_1(X)=0 and H_2(X) free. For a fixed t in Spin^c(Y), there is a bijection between Spin^c structures s on X union_Y W restricting to t and pairs (s_X,s_W) in Spin^c(X) x Spin^c(W) with both restrictions equal to t, and under this bijection c1(s)^2=c1(s_X)^2+c1(s_W)^2. Moreover max_{s|Y=t} c1(s)^2 + b_2(X union_Y W) = 4 d(Y',t) - 4 d(Y,t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This proposition is quoted as a standard algebraic package from Greene's work. The bijection is the natural gluing statement for Spin^c structures, using that c1 commutes with restriction maps and that the relevant restriction maps are surjective; the paper notes that an odd-determinant hypothesis used elsewhere is unnecessary because H_1(X) and H_1(W) are torsion-free. The square identity follows from the orthogonal splitting of the intersection form under gluing. The max formula comes from sharpness of X and W' together with the Ozsvath-Szabo correction-term inequality: W and W' have the same intersection form, so the maximisation over the glued closed manifold measures exactly the difference between the d-invariants of Y' and Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Proposition 4.2, additionally in the situation where X union_Y W is a closed simply connected negative-definite 4-manifold so Donaldson's theorem identifies its lattice with the standard negative diagonal lattice. Let u_1,...,u_ell be the basis of H_2(W) coming from the surgery link, with images in H_2(X union_Y W). If K is a maximising characteristic covector for a boundary Spin^c structure t=[K] and d(Y',t)-d(Y,t)=0, then there exists a vector alpha in {±1}^{b_2(X)+ell} such that K=(<alpha,u_1>,...,<alpha,u_ell>) in the basis dual to u_1,...,u_ell.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 4.2 and the zero-deficiency assumption, there is a Spin^c structure s' on X union_Y W with c1(s')^2=-b_2(X union_Y W). Decompose s' into its restrictions to X and W. Since X is sharp and K is a maximiser over the same boundary Spin^c structure, replace the W-component of s' by a Spin^c structure s_W with c1(s_W)=K without changing the total square; the resulting closed-manifold characteristic covector alpha still satisfies alpha^2=-b_2. Donaldson diagonalises the closed negative-definite lattice as (Z^N,-id). A characteristic covector in this lattice has all entries odd, and the equality alpha^2=-N forces every entry to have absolute value 1. The restriction of alpha to H_2(W), computed by pairing alpha with the images of u_i, is precisely K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the special case q=2, so p/q=[n,2], the conclusion of Theorem 1.2 holds under a weaker even-n vanishing assumption: if n is even, it is enough that d(Y,t)-d(S^3_{-p/q}(U),t)=0 for one Spin^c structure t.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For q=2 the second plumbing row y can be put in the form (1,1,0,...,0). The relation x dot y=-1 gives x_1+x_2=-1; choose signs and ordering with x_2<=x_1, which implies x_1>=0. Let S be the set of maximisers K with <K,v_2>=0 and zero deficiency, and let j_max be the maximum first coordinate among them. The assumed single zero deficiency gives j_max>=0. If j_max>0 then enough adjacent first-coordinate values occur to satisfy the original q-r+1 condition, so Theorem 1.2 applies. If j_max=0, the same row-maximisation argument used in the proof of Theorem 1.2 gives j_max=x_1-x_2+sum_{i>=3}|x_i|. Substituting x_2=-1-x_1 yields 0=2x_1+1+sum_{i>=3}|x_i|, impossible because all terms on the right are nonnegative and 1 is positive. Therefore j_max>0 and the theorem applies.

proof_techniques.name: Push-down calculus for characteristic covectors
proof_techniques.description: For a negative-definite plumbing graph, a characteristic covector K can be modified by pushing down at a peak v_i, replacing K by K+2 PD[v_i]. In the linear graph used in the paper, this changes a coordinate equal to a_i into -a_i and increases adjacent coordinates by 2. The paper uses this as a rewriting system: starting from covectors in the initial Ozsvath-Szabo range, paths of push-downs either terminate in the final allowed range, giving maximisers, or overshoot, giving non-maximisers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is a statement about a definite tree lattice. A sign-reversed version of this push-down operation is a natural way to move along vertices of a tree while preserving or controlling a quadratic quantity, so it may help build normal forms or detect configurations forced by the inequality w(v)<d(v).

proof_techniques.name: No-full-tank criterion
proof_techniques.description: In the linear plumbing, a full tank is a pair of peaks with every intermediate coordinate as high as possible without being a peak. The paper proves that full tanks are exactly the local obstruction, within the initial range, to initiating a maximising push-down path: a full tank propagates a push-down into an overshoot, while absence of full tanks permits an induction on peaks.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original tree is not necessarily linear, this suggests looking for path obstructions between large pairings on vertices. A proof by contradiction that every vertex is reducible might produce saturated paths analogous to full tanks, which could then be ruled out by positive definiteness.

proof_techniques.name: Square-preserving peak push-down
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.7 is a purely algebraic computation: if a characteristic covector K pairs with a vertex v_i as <K,v_i>=a_i, then K+2 PD[v_i] has the same square because the linear and quadratic correction terms cancel. This lets the paper move among many representatives of the same boundary Spin^c structure without losing the maximising property.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a graph lattice, decomposability or irreducibility questions often involve comparing norms and pairings after adding multiples of vertices. The cancellation identity gives a template for finding transformations that preserve extremality and may expose forced irreducible vertices.

proof_techniques.name: Counting representatives by deleting a vertex
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.5 counts maximising covectors with a fixed first coordinate by identifying the remaining choices with maximising covectors on the subchain G-v_1. Since the subchain represents the continued fraction q/r, the count is q for ordinary first-coordinate values; the exceptional value is then obtained by subtracting from the total p.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a general weighted tree, deleting a vertex and comparing the lattice or determinant of the remaining forest could be a useful inductive counting device. The hypothesis w(v)<d(v) singles out a vertex where such deletion-and-counting arguments might become imbalanced.

proof_techniques.name: Summing inequalities until equality forces pointwise equality
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 1.1 obtains individual inequalities D_C^n(v_i)<=D_C^{p/q}(w_i) from cobordism theory, then sums them over a carefully chosen family of maximisers. A separate exact formula for the total sum shows the summed inequality is actually an equality, forcing every individual inequality in the family to be equality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a lattice-only proof, one might set up local inequalities associated to putative decompositions of vertices and then sum over all vertices or over a normal-form family. If positive definiteness gives an exact global identity, equality forcing could isolate a vertex where decomposition is impossible.

proof_techniques.name: Donaldson diagonalisation plus zero deficiency gives ±1 test vectors
proof_techniques.description: After gluing a sharp negative-definite filling X to a surgery trace W, Donaldson's theorem identifies the closed intersection lattice with the standard negative diagonal lattice. If a boundary Spin^c structure has zero deficiency, the corresponding characteristic covector in the closed lattice has square -N; since characteristic covectors in -I have odd entries, all entries must be ±1. Pairing this ±1 vector with the W rows realises the desired maximising characteristic covector.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original tree lattice can be embedded into a standard definite lattice, or if one can otherwise produce enough sign vectors testing the rows, the paper's argument shows how ±1 pairings can force strong restrictions on vertex vectors. This is especially relevant for proving that some vertex cannot be decomposed with nonnegative cross-pairing.

proof_techniques.name: Row-support forcing from ±1 pairings
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 1.2, a row y_i with norm a_i is tested against a sign vector alpha satisfying -<alpha,y_i>=a_i. Since a_i=sum y_{i,j}^2, the equality sum_j(y_{i,j}^2+alpha_j y_{i,j})=0 and nonnegativity of each summand force y_{i,j} in {0,±1}. Similar tests with two rows force non-adjacent rows to have disjoint supports and adjacent rows to overlap exactly once with opposite signs.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is one of the most directly portable lattice arguments. If vertices of the tree are represented as vectors in a standard positive or negative lattice, finding sign vectors that maximise pairings can force vertices to be sums of unit coordinate vectors with controlled overlaps, a structure that may make irreducibility of at least one vertex transparent.

proof_techniques.name: Changemaker extraction from a full interval of pairings
proof_techniques.description: Once the first row x has been shown to have a special form with entries sigma_i and one unit overlap, the paper considers all possible first coordinates j of zero-deficiency maximisers. These j form a complete parity interval and can be written as j=1+sum sigma_i-2 sum chi_i sigma_i. Therefore the subset sums sum chi_i sigma_i realise every integer from 0 to sum sigma_i, exactly the changemaker condition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of the original problem reduces the tree lattice to a standard-coordinate model, interval-realisation of pairings may similarly imply subset-sum constraints. Such constraints can be used to find extremal or indecomposable coordinate-support vertices.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes that in the gluing proposition a determinant-odd assumption used in earlier work is not necessary; torsion-freeness of H_1(X) and H_1(W) suffices for the needed Spin^c restriction surjectivity. The Ozsvath-Szabo plumbed-manifold algorithm used here is cited as [7], Greene's earlier changemaker and lattice-embedding arguments as [2] and [3], and Donaldson diagonalisation as [1]. Section 4.4 remarks that the asymmetry in Theorem 1.2 between odd n and even n might be removable by choosing a different map r, but the paper does not do so; it does prove the q=2 simplification in Proposition 4.4.
why_relevant: Generalizes Greene's changemaker theorems to rational surgeries. It gives lattice-embedding and changemaker machinery that is often used to convert definite plumbing problems into finite combinatorial lattice problems.

title: Alternating knots with unknotting number one
overall_summary: McCoy proves that for an alternating knot, unknotting number one is equivalent to its branched double cover arising by half-integer surgery on a knot in S^3, and also equivalent to the existence of an unknotting crossing in any reduced alternating diagram. The proof is driven by lattice theory: Goeritz lattices of reduced alternating diagrams are treated as graph lattices, then compared with Greene-style change-maker lattices obtained from Heegaard Floer and Donaldson-theoretic obstructions. For the original problem, the most useful content is Section 2, which develops irreducibility in positive-definite integral lattices, especially a graph-lattice characterization of irreducible vectors and methods for certifying irreducibility using decompositions and sign/support arguments. Later parts of the paper also provide reusable estimates: if a graph lattice is embedded in a change-maker lattice, sums of vertices have tightly bounded coordinates because any attempted decomposition has non-positive cross-term.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition in Section 2: irreducible element and indecomposable lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A positive-definite integral lattice L is a finitely generated free abelian group with a positive-definite, symmetric, integer-valued bilinear form, written x·y. Its norm is ∥v∥^2=v·v. The lattice L is indecomposable if it cannot be written as an orthogonal direct sum L=L_1⊕L_2 with L_1 and L_2 nonzero sublattices. An element v∈L is irreducible if there do not exist nonzero x,y∈L such that v=x+y and x·y≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is definitional in the paper. The exact irreducibility condition is used throughout: to prove a vector is irreducible one rules out decompositions with nonnegative cross-term, and to exploit irreducibility one produces a natural decomposition v=a+b and shows a·b≥0, forcing one summand to vanish.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1 together with equations (2.1) and (2.2): non-positive cross-term for sums of vertices in graph lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops, allowing multiple edges. For vertices u,v let e(u,v) be the number of edges between them, and let d(v)=∑_{u∈V} e(u,v). Let Λ(G) be the quotient of the free abelian group on V by the subgroup generated by [V]=∑_{v∈V}v, equipped with the form v·v=d(v) and u·v=−e(u,v) for u≠v. For R⊆V write [R]=∑_{v∈R}v. If x=[R] is a sum of vertices and z∈Λ(G) is arbitrary, then (x−z)·z≤0. More explicitly, if z is represented by ∑_{v∈V} b_v v and b_{v,u}=b_v−b_u, then ([R]−z)·z=∑_{u∈R,v∈V∖R} b_{v,u}(1−b_{v,u})e(v,u) − (1/2)∑_{u,v∈R} b_{v,u}^2 e(v,u) − (1/2)∑_{u,v∈V∖R} b_{v,u}^2 e(v,u), and every summand on the right is non-positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For y=∑ c_v v one first computes v·y=∑_{u∈V}(c_v−c_u)e(v,u). Multiplying by the coefficients b_v of x=∑ b_v v and symmetrizing gives x·y=(1/2)∑_{u,v∈V}(c_v−c_u)(b_v−b_u)e(v,u). Put y=[R]−z, so c_v=1−b_v for v∈R and c_v=−b_v for v∉R. Substitution yields the displayed formula. Since b_{v,u} is an integer, b_{v,u}(1−b_{v,u})≤0, and the square terms are also non-positive; hence ([R]−z)·z≤0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2: irreducible vectors in graph lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops, and let Λ(G) be its graph lattice as in Lemma 2.1. For a nonzero element x∈Λ(G), x is irreducible, meaning x cannot be written as a+b with nonzero a,b and a·b≥0, if and only if x=[R] for some subset R⊆V such that both induced subgraphs G[R] and G[V∖R] are connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward direction, write x=∑ a_v v and use the relation [V]=0 to choose the integer coefficients with min_v a_v=0. Let a=max_v a_v≥1 and let R={v:a_v=a}. A direct calculation of (x−[R])·[R] shows that internal edges of R cancel and each edge from R to V∖R contributes (a−1)−a_u≥0, because a_u≤a−1 outside R. Thus (x−[R])·[R]≥0. Since [R] is nonzero and x is irreducible, the decomposition x=[R]+(x−[R]) forces x=[R]. If G[R] were disconnected, say R=S∪T with e(S,T)=0, then [R]=[S]+[T] and [S]·[T]=0 would contradict irreducibility; applying the same argument to −x=[V∖R] shows G[V∖R] is connected. Conversely, assume x=[R] and both R and V∖R induce connected subgraphs. If [R]=y+([R]−y) with ([R]−y)·y≥0, Lemma 2.1 gives equality. In the formula from Lemma 2.1, equality forces all square terms to vanish, so the coefficients of y are constant on R and constant on V∖R. The edge terms across the cut force the two constants to differ by either 0 or 1. Therefore y is either 0 or [R] in the quotient, so no nontrivial decomposition with nonnegative cross-term exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3: 2-connected graphs, irreducible vertices, and indecomposable graph lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops, and let Λ(G) be its graph lattice. The following are equivalent: (i) G is 2-connected, i.e. deleting any vertex does not disconnect G; (ii) every vertex v∈V, viewed as an element of Λ(G), is irreducible; (iii) Λ(G) is indecomposable as an orthogonal direct sum of nonzero sublattices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of (i) and (ii) is Lemma 2.2 applied to R={v}: the singleton is connected, and the complement is connected exactly when v is not a cut vertex. For (ii)⇒(iii), suppose Λ(G)=L_1⊕L_2. Since each vertex is irreducible, its two orthogonal projections cannot both be nonzero, so every vertex lies entirely in L_1 or L_2. Let R_i be the vertices lying in L_i. Then [R_1]+[R_2]=[V]=0 and [R_1]·[R_2]=0, hence [R_1]^2=0. Positive-definiteness forces [R_1]=0, and connectedness implies the partition is trivial; if L_1 is nonzero, all vertices lie in L_1 and L_2=0. For (iii)⇒(i), if G has a cut vertex v, then G∖{v} has at least two nonempty components; grouping these into two nonempty parts gives two nonzero orthogonal sublattices whose direct sum is Λ(G), contradicting indecomposability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Implicit corollary used in Lemma 2.4: norm-one vectors and cut-edges in graph lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite connected graph with no self-loops and graph lattice Λ(G). If G has no cut-edge, then Λ(G) contains no vector of norm 1. More precisely, a cut-edge separating a subset R from V∖R gives [R]^2=1, and conversely a norm-one vector in Λ(G) would correspond, via Lemma 2.2, to a subset R with boundary size d(R)=1, hence to a cut-edge.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The identity [R]^2=d(R), where d(R)=e(R,V∖R), follows directly from the graph-lattice form or from the edge-energy formula. A vector of norm 1 in a positive-definite integral lattice is automatically irreducible: if z=a+b with a,b nonzero and a·b≥0, then 1=z^2=a^2+b^2+2a·b≥2. Lemma 2.2 then gives z=[R] with both sides connected, and z^2=d(R)=1. A boundary of size one is exactly a cut-edge.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4: a decomposition v=x+y with x·y=−1 detects a cut-edge after deleting v
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite connected graph with no self-loops, assume G is 2-connected and contains no cut-edges, and let Λ(G) be its graph lattice. Suppose a vertex v∈V admits a decomposition v=x+y in Λ(G) with x,y∈Λ(G) and x·y=−1. Then G∖{v} contains a cut-edge e. If R and S are the vertex sets of the two components of (G∖{v})∖{e}, then {x,y}={[R]+v,[S]+v}. Moreover, if u_1∈R and u_2∈S are the endpoints of e, then they are the unique vertices different from v satisfying x·u_1=1 and y·u_2=1; every vertex w∉{v,u_1,u_2} satisfies w·x≤0 and w·y≤0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First prove x and y are irreducible. If x=z+w with z·w≥0, then because x·y=−1, at least one of z·y or w·y is nonnegative; assume w·y≥0. Then w·(y+z)≥0, and v=w+(y+z). Since G is 2-connected, v is irreducible by Lemma 2.3, so either w=0 or y+z=0. In the latter case w=v and y=−z; then x·y=−1 and z·w≥0 imply z·(v+z)=1 with v·z≥0, hence ∥z∥^2≤1. As G has no cut-edges, Λ(G) has no norm-one vectors, so z=0. Thus x is irreducible, and similarly y is. By Lemma 2.2, x=[R′] and y=[S′] for subsets with connected induced sides. The equality v=[R′]+[S′] in the quotient forces R′∪S′=V and R′∩S′={v}; write R=R′∖{v} and S=S′∖{v}. Thus x=v+[R] and y=v+[S], with R∪S=V∖{v}. The irreducibility conditions imply R, S, R∪{v}, and S∪{v} induce connected subgraphs. Since [R]+[S]=−v, one computes x·y=[R]·[S]=−e(R,S)=−1, so there is exactly one edge between R and S. That edge is a cut-edge of G∖{v}; the final uniqueness and sign assertions follow by computing pairings of x and y with vertices in R and S, where only the endpoints of the unique R–S edge pair positively.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5: Brown's subset-sum characterization of change-maker sequences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ_1≤σ_2≤⋯≤σ_s be nonnegative integers. Every integer k with 0≤k≤σ_1+⋯+σ_s can be expressed as k=∑_{i∈A}σ_i for some subset A⊆{1,…,s} if and only if the sequence satisfies the change-maker inequalities σ_1≤1 and σ_i≤σ_1+⋯+σ_{i−1}+1 for every 1<i≤s.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as a theorem of J. L. Brown and does not reprove it. McCoy uses it as the combinatorial engine allowing one to manufacture lattice vectors with prescribed σ-pairing zero: an integer bounded by a partial sum of the σ_i can be represented as a subset sum of earlier coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.6: representing σ_s by earlier coefficients, with control of the index 0
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=⟨ρ,σ⟩^⊥⊆ℤ^{r+2} be a change-maker lattice, where ρ=e_{−1}−e_0 and σ=e_0+σ_1e_1+⋯+σ_re_r, and assume σ_i≥1 for i≥1, so σ_1=1. Write σ_0=1 for the coefficient of e_0. For any s>1, there is a subset A⊆{0,1,…,s−1} such that 1∈A and σ_s=∑_{i∈A}σ_i. If the sequence σ is slack, meaning no index t>1 satisfies σ_t=1+σ_1+⋯+σ_{t−1}, then A may be chosen with 0∉A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states that this follows by induction on s. One inductive reconstruction is as follows. If σ_s is tight at s, take A={0,1,…,s−1}. If σ_s is not tight, the change-maker inequality gives σ_s≤σ_1+⋯+σ_{s−1}, so Brown's proposition gives a subset B⊆{1,…,s−1} summing to σ_s. If 1∈B, take A=B, which already avoids 0. If 1∉B, let j be the smallest element of B. By induction, σ_j can be expressed as a sum over A_j⊆{0,…,j−1} containing 1; replacing j in B by A_j produces a subset summing to σ_s, and minimality of j avoids overlap with B∖{j}. If the whole sequence is slack, every initial segment is slack, so the inductive representation of σ_j can be chosen without 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.7: standard vectors form a basis of a change-maker lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=⟨ρ,σ⟩^⊥⊆ℤ^{r+2} be a change-maker lattice with ρ=e_{−1}−e_0 and σ=e_0+σ_1e_1+⋯+σ_re_r, and assume σ_i≥1 for i≥1. For each s=1,…,r define a standard vector v_s as follows. If σ_s=1+σ_1+⋯+σ_{s−1}, set v_s=−e_s+e_{−1}+e_0+e_1+⋯+e_{s−1}. Otherwise choose A_s⊆{1,…,s−2} with σ_s−σ_{s−1}=∑_{i∈A_s}σ_i and set v_s=−e_s+e_{s−1}+∑_{i∈A_s}e_i. Then S={v_1,…,v_r} is a basis for L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The vectors are triangular with respect to the ordered basis e_1,…,e_r: v_k has coefficient −1 on e_k, and no v_j with j<k has e_k in its support. Hence they are linearly independent. To show they span, take any v∈L and let k=max supp(v) among indices ≥1. Adding (v·e_k)v_k kills the e_k coefficient and does not create any larger indexed coefficient. Iterating reduces to a vector whose support is contained in {−1,0}. Orthogonality to ρ then makes it a(e_{−1}+e_0), and orthogonality to σ forces a=0. Thus every element of L is an integer combination of the standard vectors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.9: irreducible vectors in change-maker lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=⟨ρ,σ⟩^⊥⊆ℤ^{r+2} be a change-maker lattice with ρ=e_{−1}−e_0 and σ=e_0+σ_1e_1+⋯+σ_re_r, and assume σ_i≥1 for all i≥1. If v=−e_k+∑_{i∈A}e_i lies in L with A⊂{−1,0,1,…,k−1}, then v is irreducible in L. In particular, every standard basis element from Lemma 2.7 is irreducible. Additionally, if σ_k is tight, then the vector v=−e_k+e_{k−1}+⋯+e_2+2e_1 is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First observe that any nonzero y∈L has both positive and negative support: orthogonality to ρ ties the e_{−1} and e_0 coefficients, and since σ has positive coefficients on e_0,e_1,…,e_r, a vector with all coefficients of one sign cannot be orthogonal to σ unless it is zero. For v=−e_k+∑_{i∈A}e_i, suppose v=x+y with x,y∈L and x·y≥0. In each coordinate, x_i+y_i is −1, 0, or 1, so x_i y_i≤0. Thus x·y=∑ x_i y_i≤0, and the assumed inequality forces every coordinate product to be zero. At the k-coordinate, one summand has coefficient −1; assume it is x. Then y has no negative coefficient anywhere, so y=0 by the sign-support observation. This proves irreducibility of all such 0/1-support vectors. For the additional tight vector, write y_i=v_i−x_i and compute x·y=−x_{−1}^2−x_0^2+(2−x_1)x_1+∑_{i=2}^{k−1}(1−x_i)x_i−(x_k+1)x_k−∑_{i=k+1}^r x_i^2. All terms except possibly (2−x_1)x_1 are non-positive, and that term is at most 1. Hence x·y≥0 implies x_1y_1 is 0 or 1. If x_1y_1=0, all coordinate products vanish and one summand has no negative support, so it is zero. If x_1y_1=1, then x_1=y_1=1 and there can be at most one coordinate with negative product. Since both nonzero summands must have negative support, after swapping summands one has supp−(y)={k} and supp−(x)={l} with l>k, while supp+(x)⊆{1,…,k−1}. Orthogonality σ·x=0 would express σ_l as a sum of coefficients strictly before k, which is less than σ_k because σ_k is tight; this contradicts monotonicity σ_l≥σ_k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.10: indecomposable change-maker lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=⟨ρ,σ⟩^⊥⊆ℤ^{r+2} be a change-maker lattice with ρ=e_{−1}−e_0 and σ=e_0+σ_1e_1+⋯+σ_re_r. The following are equivalent: (i) L is indecomposable; (ii) σ_s≥1 for every s≥1; (iii) L contains no vector of norm 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If some σ_s=0, then e_s lies in L and gives a norm-one summand, so L is decomposable and contains a norm-one vector. Conversely, because the ambient lattice has orthonormal basis, any norm-one vector in L must be ±e_i; the orthogonality conditions allow this only for an index i≥1 with σ_i=0. This proves the equivalence of (ii) and (iii) and the implication (i)⇒(ii). For (ii)⇒(i), take the standard basis v_1,…,v_r. By Lemma 2.9 all v_i are irreducible. If L=L_1⊕L_2, each v_i lies entirely in one summand. Suppose v_1∈L_1 and choose the least k with v_k∈L_2, if any exists. Since σ_k>0, the standard vector v_k has a smallest support index m<k. If m>0, then v_k·v_m=−1; if m≤0, then m=−1 and v_k·v_1=1. Either case contradicts orthogonality of L_1 and L_2. Thus no such k exists and the decomposition is trivial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2: a change-maker test vector gives cross-term −1 or 0
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Section 5, let D be a reduced alternating diagram whose Goeritz lattice Λ_D is identified with a change-maker lattice L=⟨ρ,σ⟩^⊥⊆ℤ^{r+2}; let V_D be the images in L of the vertices of the corresponding white graph. If x is either a single element of V_D or a sum of elements of V_D, and if z=−e_g+∑_{i∈A}e_i lies in L with A⊆supp+(x):={i:x·e_i>0} and g∉supp+(x), then (x−z)·z is either −1 or 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because x is a vertex or a sum of vertices in a graph lattice, Lemma 2.1 gives (x−z)·z≤0. Directly in the ambient orthonormal lattice, (x−z)·z=−x·e_g−1+∑_{i∈A}(x·e_i−1). The hypotheses give x·e_g≤0 and x·e_i≥1 for i∈A, so the right-hand side is at least −1. It is integral, hence it must be −1 or 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3: coefficient bounds for sums of vertices in a graph lattice embedded as a change-maker lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same Section 5 setting, let x be a single vertex of V_D or a sum of vertices of V_D. Then: (i) |x·e_0|≤1; (ii) |x·e_1|≤2; (iii) if x is irreducible and x·e_0≠0, then |x·e_1|≤1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because the total sum of all graph vertices is zero in the graph lattice, −x is again a sum of vertices, so signs can be chosen conveniently. For (i), assume x·e_0≥0. Choose the least g>0 with x·e_g≤0. By the change-maker condition, write σ_g−1 as a subset sum of earlier σ_i and form z=−e_g+e_0+e_{−1}+∑_{i∈A}e_i∈L. Lemma 2.1 gives (x−z)·z≤0, while minimality of g and x·e_0=x·e_{−1} give the lower bound (x−z)·z≥−1−x·e_g+(x·e_0−1)+(x·e_{−1}−1)≥2x·e_0−3. Thus x·e_0≤3/2, hence x·e_0≤1. Applying the same argument to −x gives |x·e_0|≤1. For (ii), assume x·e_1≥0 and choose the least g>1 with x·e_g≤0. Build z=−e_g+e_1+∑_{i∈A}e_i∈L with z·e_1=ε∈{1,2}. Lemma 2.1 yields 0≥(x−z)·z≥−1+ε(x·e_1−ε), so x·e_1≤ε+1/ε≤5/2 and integrality gives x·e_1≤2; again apply this to −x. For (iii), assume x is irreducible and x·e_0=1. Let g>0 be least with x·e_g≤0. If g=1, take z=−e_1+e_0+e_{−1}, which is irreducible by Lemma 2.9. If x≠z, irreducibility of x forces (x−z)·z≤−1, and the computation (x−z)·z=−(x·e_1+1), together with minimality, gives −1≤x·e_1≤0; if x=z then x·e_1=−1. If g>1, choose z so that z·e_1=1, using either e_0+e_{−1} or e_1 according to whether 1 belongs to the subset-sum representation. If x≠z, irreducibility gives (x−z)·z≤−1, while the same coordinate estimate gives x·e_1≤1; minimality gives x·e_1≥1. Thus x·e_1=1. The case x·e_0=−1 follows by replacing x with −x.

proof_techniques.name: Edge-wise energy expansion for graph lattices
proof_techniques.description: The paper rewrites the graph-lattice pairing as a sum over ordered pairs of vertices, equivalently as an edge-energy expression involving coefficient differences. When one summand is [R], the expression for ([R]−z)·z splits into contributions from edges inside R, inside V∖R, and across the cut; each contribution is non-positive for integral coefficients. This converts an algebraic condition about decompositions into local inequalities along edges.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree lattice, an analogous expansion for a proposed decomposition of a vertex or of a connected subtree sum may expose sign restrictions branch-by-branch. The key transferable idea is to express cross-terms using coefficient differences and then force each local term to have a sign.

proof_techniques.name: Extremal coefficient or top-level-set extraction
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Lemma 2.2, an arbitrary lattice element x is represented with minimum coefficient 0, and the subset R on which the coefficient is maximal is isolated. The decomposition x=[R]+(x−[R]) has nonnegative cross-term, so irreducibility of x forces x=[R]. Connectivity of R and its complement is then forced by excluding decompositions [R]=[S]+[T] with zero cross-term.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a vertex in the weighted tree is assumed reducible or if one studies a minimal counterexample, choosing coefficient maxima/minima in a decomposition may identify a connected branch or level set whose pairing has controlled sign. This can turn irreducibility questions into combinatorics of connected subtrees.

proof_techniques.name: Graph-theoretic characterization of irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: For graph lattices, McCoy proves that irreducible elements are exactly the sums [R] where both R and V∖R induce connected subgraphs. As a special case, every graph vertex is irreducible exactly when the graph has no cut vertex, and graph-lattice indecomposability is equivalent to 2-connectedness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original lattice is a weighted tree lattice rather than a graph Laplacian quotient, this suggests looking for a graph-theoretic meaning of a reducible vertex: reducibility should correspond to splitting the tree into branches with controlled interaction. The theorem provides a model for translating algebraic irreducibility into connectivity.

proof_techniques.name: Norm-one obstruction
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the fact that graph lattices with no cut-edges have no norm-one vectors, since a norm-one vector would be irreducible and hence a cut [R] with exactly one boundary edge. More generally, in any positive-definite integral lattice a norm-one vector cannot decompose as a+b with a,b nonzero and a·b≥0 because the norm would be at least 2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree has any vertex of weight 1, that vertex is immediately irreducible by the same norm-one argument. More broadly, ruling out or locating small-norm vectors can sharply constrain possible decompositions of vertices.

proof_techniques.name: Reducible vertex localizes to a cut
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.4 analyzes a decomposition v=x+y with x·y=−1 in a graph lattice. Under 2-connectedness and absence of cut-edges, it first proves x and y are themselves irreducible, then uses the classification of irreducibles to show that x and y are sums over the two sides of a unique edge in G∖{v}. Thus an algebraic decomposition of one vertex identifies a very specific graph cut.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a tree lattice, every vertex removal already separates branches. A parallel argument might show that any reducible decomposition of a vertex must align with a partition of incident branches; this could be used to find a vertex whose decomposition is impossible under positive-definiteness and the bad-weight hypothesis.

proof_techniques.name: Irreducibility from sparse support in an orthonormal ambient lattice
proof_techniques.description: For change-maker lattices inside ℤ^{r+2}, McCoy proves that vectors of the form −e_k plus a 0/1 sum of earlier basis vectors are irreducible. The proof is coordinate-wise: if v=x+y and x·y≥0, then every coordinate product x_i y_i is non-positive, so all must vanish; one summand is then forced to have no negative coordinate, impossible for a nonzero vector in the change-maker lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree lattice can be embedded into a diagonal lattice, or if candidate vertices can be expressed in a similarly sparse coordinate form, this gives a direct certificate of irreducibility that avoids global case analysis.

proof_techniques.name: Irreducible generating set plus nonzero-pairing graph implies indecomposability
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 2.10, standard basis vectors of a change-maker lattice are irreducible. If the lattice decomposed orthogonally, each irreducible basis vector would lie in one summand; the triangular support structure guarantees nonzero pairings linking each new basis element to an earlier one, forcing all basis elements into the same summand.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, if one can construct enough irreducible elements supported near vertices or branches, nonzero adjacency pairings may propagate them through the connected tree. This can rule out orthogonal decompositions or help isolate where irreducible vertices must occur.

proof_techniques.name: Constructing test vectors from subset-sum conditions
proof_techniques.description: The change-maker condition, via Brown's theorem and Lemma 2.6, lets the paper build lattice vectors z with a prescribed negative coordinate and positive coordinates chosen from a desired support. These z are then inserted into the graph-lattice inequality (x−z)·z≤0 to force sharp coefficient bounds, often narrowing a cross-term to only −1 or 0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem may admit analogous test vectors built from paths or branches in the tree. If such vectors can be chosen to match the sign pattern of a suspected decomposition, the same strategy can turn positivity into rigid coefficient bounds.

proof_techniques.name: Equality-case analysis in non-positive decompositions
proof_techniques.description: Several arguments use an inequality such as (x−z)·z≤0 and then force equality by an independent lower bound or irreducibility. Equality in the edge-wise formula is very strong: coefficient differences must vanish along internal edges, and cross-edge differences must be 0 or 1. This converts a global algebraic equality into exact local coefficient constraints.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When positive-definiteness or minimality forces a cross-term to be nonnegative while a structural inequality forces it to be nonpositive, the equality case may determine the coefficients of a decomposition on every branch of the tree.

other_useful_info: Section 2.1 is explicitly based on Greene, The lens space realization problem, Section 3.2. Change-maker lattices are drawn from Greene's work on lens spaces, cabling, and unknotting number one. The paper repeatedly uses the identities [R]^2=d(R) in a graph lattice and [V]=0 in the quotient, and also uses that if x is irreducible then −x is irreducible. The later knot-theoretic parts identify Goeritz lattices of reduced alternating diagrams with graph lattices and then with change-maker lattices; those topological applications are probably less directly relevant than the lattice lemmas themselves for the original weighted-tree irreducibility problem.
why_relevant: Uses the exact irreducibility definition from the problem for positive-definite integral lattices. Its graph-lattice and changemaker-lattice lemmas give concrete techniques for proving that specified vertex-like lattice elements are irreducible.

title: Non-integer surgery and branched double covers of alternating knots
overall_summary: The paper proves that when the branched double cover of an alternating link is obtained by non-integer surgery of slope p/q with |p/q| > 1, the surgery is realized diagrammatically by a rational tangle replacement in an alternating diagram. The core method combines Heegaard Floer correction-term surgery obstructions, Donaldson-type lattice embedding input, and a detailed structural analysis of Goeritz graph lattices that are isomorphic to p/q-changemaker lattices. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the purely lattice-theoretic material: graph-lattice irreducibility criteria, the behavior of irreducible elements under decompositions, and changemaker-lattice arguments certifying irreducibility from coordinate patterns. Especially useful are Lemmas 3.1–3.4 and 6.5–6.8, which give reusable mechanisms for proving that certain elements cannot split as a sum with nonnegative inner product.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 graph-lattice setup and identities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = (V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops. For disjoint subsets R,S ⊆ V, let e(R,S) be the number of edges between R and S, and let d(R) = e(R,V \ R). Let Λbar(G) be the free abelian group generated by V, with symmetric bilinear form defined on vertices by v·w = d(v) if v = w and v·w = −e(v,w) if v ≠ w. For R ⊆ V, write [R] = ∑_{v∈R} v. Then v·[R] = −e(v,R) if v ∉ R, and v·[R] = e(v,V \ R) if v ∈ R. In particular, [V]·x = 0 for all x ∈ Λbar(G), so the form descends to the quotient graph lattice Λ(G) = Λbar(G) / Z[V]. Since G is connected, the descended form on Λ(G) is positive definite.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The displayed formula for v·[R] is obtained by summing v·u over all u ∈ R: if v ∉ R only off-diagonal edge terms occur, while if v ∈ R the diagonal d(v) is reduced by the number of edges from v to other vertices of R, leaving the number of edges from v to V \ R. The identity [V]·x = 0 follows by taking R = V. Positive definiteness is the usual Laplacian energy calculation: for a representative ∑ a_v v, the square is a sum over edges of terms of the form (a_u − a_v)^2, whose kernel consists exactly of constant functions when G is connected; quotienting by Z[V] removes this kernel.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the graph lattice Λ(G) of a finite connected graph G as above, if x = [R] is the class of a sum of vertices over some subset R ⊆ V, then for every z ∈ Λ(G) one has (x − z)·z ≤ 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a recalled result from McCoy [12]. A useful way to see the inequality is via the edge-energy formula. Choose an integer representative z = ∑ n_v v and let x be represented by the indicator function ε_v of R. For each oriented edge ab, the contribution to (x − z)·z is ((ε_a − ε_b) − (n_a − n_b))(n_a − n_b). If the edge is internal to R or to V \ R, then ε_a − ε_b = 0 and the contribution is −(n_a − n_b)^2 ≤ 0. If the edge crosses the cut, then ε_a − ε_b = ±1, and the contribution is (1 − m)m or (−1 − m)m for an integer m = n_a − n_b, both of which are nonpositive. Summing over all edges gives the claimed inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Λ(G) be the graph lattice of a finite connected graph G. A nonzero vector x ∈ Λ(G) is irreducible, meaning that there do not exist nonzero y,z ∈ Λ(G) with x = y + z and y·z ≥ 0, if and only if x = [R] for some subset R ⊆ V such that the subgraph induced by R and the subgraph induced by V \ R are both connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls this from McCoy [12]. The forward direction is based on the fact that if either R or V \ R is disconnected, then [R], or equivalently −[V \ R], can be split as a sum over disconnected components with dot product 0, giving a reducible decomposition. Conversely, if R and V \ R are connected, then for any decomposition [R] = z + ([R] − z), Lemma 3.1 gives ([R] − z)·z ≤ 0. If equality occurs, the edgewise proof of Lemma 3.1 forces z to be constant on each of the two connected induced subgraphs and to have only the possible cut jump compatible with [R]; in the quotient by [V], this means z is either 0 or [R]. Hence no nontrivial decomposition with nonnegative cross product exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite connected graph G, the following are equivalent: (i) G is 2-connected, i.e. G cannot be disconnected by deleting a single vertex; (ii) every vertex v ∈ V, viewed as an element of the graph lattice Λ(G), is irreducible; (iii) the lattice Λ(G) is indecomposable, i.e. it cannot be written as an orthogonal direct sum Λ(G) = L1 ⊕ L2 with L1 and L2 nonzero sublattices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls this from McCoy [12]. The equivalence of (i) and (ii) follows directly from Lemma 3.2: the vertex v is represented by [{v}], whose support is connected, and its complement is connected exactly when deleting v does not disconnect G. The equivalence with indecomposability expresses the same cut-vertex obstruction at the lattice level: a cut vertex produces an orthogonal splitting associated to the components separated by that vertex, while any nontrivial orthogonal splitting of the graph lattice detects such a separation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a 2-connected finite graph with no cut-edges, and let Λ(G) be its graph lattice. Suppose there is a vertex v ∈ V and elements x,y ∈ Λ(G) such that v = x + y and x·y = −1. Then there is a cut edge e in the graph G \ {v}. If R and S are the vertex sets of the two components of (G \ {v}) \ {e}, then {x,y} = {[R] + v, [S] + v}. Furthermore, there are unique vertices u1,u2 ≠ v with x·u1 = 1 and y·u2 = 1, and every vertex w ∉ {v,u1,u2} satisfies w·x ≤ 0 and w·y ≤ 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a recalled result from McCoy [12]. Its role is to convert a nearly reducible decomposition of a graph-lattice vertex, namely v = x + y with x·y = −1, into an explicit graph separation: deleting v creates a graph in which a single cut edge e separates two vertex sets R and S, and the summands are the corresponding cut vectors [R] + v and [S] + v. The uniqueness of u1 and u2 identifies the endpoints of the relevant separation by the only positive pairings of x and y with other vertices; all other vertex pairings are nonpositive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be a reduced alternating link diagram, let Γ_D be its white graph for the chessboard coloring with all crossing incidence numbers −1, and identify the white Goeritz lattice Λ_D with the graph lattice of Γ_D. Suppose Γ_D is 2-connected and has a vertex v that can be written in Λ_D as v = x + y with x·y = −1. Then there are unique vertices u1,u2 ≠ v satisfying u1·x > 0 and u2·y > 0. Moreover, there is a flype from D to another reduced alternating diagram D′ and an isomorphism Λ_{D′} ≅ Λ_D such that the vertices of Γ_{D′} correspond to the vertices of Γ_D after replacing v,u1,u2 by x,y,u1+u2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites McCoy [12] for the proof. Algebraically, Lemma 3.4 identifies a cut edge in Γ_D \ {v} and determines the two summands x and y from the separated vertex sets. Diagrammatically, this cut-edge configuration is exactly the white-graph trace of a flypable alternating tangle. Performing the flype changes the white graph by replacing the old regions v,u1,u2 with regions represented in the lattice by x,y,u1+u2, while preserving the Goeritz lattice up to the stated isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 cut-set flype observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be an alternating diagram with white graph Γ_D. Suppose two white regions v and w form a cut set in Γ_D and that there is an edge between v and w. If G1 is a component of Γ_D \ {v,w} adjacent in the plane to an edge between v and w, then a flype rotates the tangle corresponding to G1. If D′ is the result, then Λ_{D′} is naturally isomorphic to Λ_D by replacing each vertex z ∈ G1 with −z and replacing v and w with v + [G1] and w + [G1], respectively.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper explains this directly from the planar flype picture. The key algebraic identity is that for every z ∈ G1 one has (v + w)·z = −z·[G1]. This identity makes the stated replacements preserve all pairings among the white-region vectors after the tangle corresponding to G1 is rotated. Thus the flype has a transparent lattice description in terms of adding the component sum [G1] to the two boundary regions and negating the interior region vectors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 6.2 and Section 6.1 setup for p/q-changemaker lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For q ≥ 2 and p/q > 1, write p/q = n − r/q with 0 < r < q, and write the negative continued fraction p/q = [a0,a1,...,al]_- with a0 = n and ai ≥ 2 for i ≥ 1. Define m0 = 0 and mk = ∑_{i=1}^k ai − k for 1 ≤ k ≤ l, and put s = ml. Let f1,...,ft,e0,...,es be an orthonormal basis for Z^{t+s+1}. Let w0 = e0 + σ1 f1 + ... + σt ft, where ∥w0∥^2 = n and the nondecreasing sequence σ1,...,σt satisfies the changemaker condition 0 ≤ σ1 ≤ 1 and σ_{i−1} ≤ σ_i ≤ σ1 + ... + σ_{i−1} + 1 for 1 < i ≤ t. For 1 ≤ k ≤ l, set wk = −e_{m_{k−1}} + e_{m_{k−1}+1} + ... + e_{mk}. The lattice L = ⟨w0,...,wl⟩^⊥ ⊆ Z^{t+s+1} is called a p/q-changemaker lattice. Its fractional part is LF = ⟨w1,...,wl⟩^⊥ ∩ ⟨e0,...,es⟩, and its integer part is LI = ⟨w0⟩^⊥ ∩ ⟨e0,f1,...,ft⟩. For x ∈ L write x0 = x·e0; one decomposes x as x = xI + xF − x0 e0 with xI ∈ LI and xF ∈ LF, and for x,y ∈ L one has x·y = xI·yI − x0 y0 + xF·yF. The fractional part LF has a basis v0,...,vm constructed from the indices not among m0,...,ml, with Gram matrix satisfying vi·vj = ∥vi∥^2 if i = j, vi·vj = −1 if |i−j| = 1, and vi·vj = 0 if |i−j| > 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is mostly definition and direct coordinate calculation. The vectors wk have the continued-fraction intersection pattern wk·wk = ak, wk·w_{k+1} = −1, and wk·wj = 0 for |k−j| > 1. The decomposition x = xI + xF − x0 e0 separates the f-coordinate part and the e-coordinate part while correcting for the shared e0-coordinate; because the two coordinate blocks otherwise have disjoint support, the dot-product formula follows immediately. The basis v0,...,vm of LF is built so that consecutive basis vectors overlap in exactly one e-coordinate with opposite signs, giving pairing −1, while nonconsecutive vectors have disjoint support.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.4 (Brown complete-sequence criterion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ = (σ1,...,σs) be a nondecreasing sequence of integers. There is a subset A ⊆ {1,...,s} with k = ∑_{i∈A} σ_i for every integer k satisfying 0 ≤ k ≤ σ1 + ... + σs if and only if σ satisfies the changemaker condition 0 ≤ σ1 ≤ 1 and σ_{i−1} ≤ σ_i ≤ σ1 + ... + σ_{i−1} + 1 for all 1 < i ≤ s.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem of Brown [1]. It is used as a black-box subset-sum device: whenever a coefficient σ_g is bounded by the changemaker condition, one can choose a subset of earlier coefficients summing to σ_g or to σ_g − 1, depending on the required test vector. These subset choices are the source of many vectors of the form −f_g plus a controlled sum of earlier f_i coordinates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L = ⟨w0,...,wl⟩^⊥ ⊆ Z^{t+s+1} be a p/q-changemaker lattice in the q ≥ 2 setup, with w0 = e0 + σ1 f1 + ... + σt ft. Assume σ_i ≥ 1 for every i. Suppose z ∈ L has the coordinate form z = −f_k + ∑_{i∈A} f_i + ∑_{j∈B} e_j, where A ⊆ {1,...,k−1} and B ⊆ {0,...,s}. Then z is irreducible in L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume z = x + y with x,y ∈ L and x·y ≥ 0. In each orthonormal coordinate f_i or e_j, the two integer coordinates of x and y sum to −1, 0, or 1, because z has only coefficients in {−1,0,1}. For integer pairs with such a sum, the product of the two coordinates is always ≤ 0. Since x·y is the sum of all these coordinate products and is assumed nonnegative, every coordinate product must be 0. Thus the support of z is partitioned between x and y coordinate by coordinate. After possibly interchanging x and y, assume x·f_k = −1. Then every coordinate of y in the basis f_i,e_j is nonnegative. The condition y ∈ L gives y·w0 = y·e0 + ∑ σ_i(y·f_i) = 0, and because all terms are nonnegative and all σ_i ≥ 1, this forces y·e0 = 0 and y·f_i = 0 for all i. Orthogonality to w1,w2,... then recursively forces all e_j-coordinates of y to vanish. Hence y = 0, so no nontrivial decomposition exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the fractional part LF of a p/q-changemaker lattice, with basis v0,...,vm as in Section 6.1, a vector xF ∈ LF is irreducible if and only if xF = ±(v_a + v_{a+1} + ... + v_b) for some 0 ≤ a ≤ b ≤ m.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper identifies LF with the graph lattice of a connected graph whose vertices are v0,...,vm and w = −(v0 + ... + vm). The vertices v0,...,vm span a path, and the endpoints v0 and vm are connected to w; additional edges to w encode any larger diagonal norms. Lemma 3.2 then characterizes irreducibles as sums over vertex subsets whose induced subgraph and complement are connected. Up to replacing a subset by its complement, one may assume w is not in the subset. A connected subset of the path on v0,...,vm is exactly a contiguous interval {v_a,...,v_b}, and its complement remains connected because the path ends connect through w. Therefore the irreducibles are precisely the signed interval sums.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a p/q-changemaker lattice with q ≥ 2, and write each x ∈ L as x = xI + xF − x0 e0 with xI ∈ LI, xF ∈ LF, and x0 = x·e0. If x ∈ L is irreducible, then its fractional component xF ∈ LF is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If x0 = 0, then xI and xF both lie in L and xI·xF = 0. Since x = xI + xF is irreducible, one of these summands must be zero, so the conclusion is immediate in the convention used in the paper. Now assume x0 ≠ 0 and replace x by −x if necessary so x0 > 0. Write xF = ∑ c_i v_i with c0 = x0, and put c_{m+1} = 0. Choose gF minimal with c_{gF+1} ≤ 0, and set zF = v0 + ... + v_{gF}. Lemma 6.6 says zF is irreducible, and a direct computation using the chain Gram matrix gives (xF − zF)·zF = (∥v0∥^2−1)(c0−1) + ∑_{i=1}^{gF−1}(∥vi∥^2−2)(c_i−1) + (∥v_{gF}∥^2−1)(c_{gF}−1) − c_{gF+1} ≥ x0 − 1. Next choose gI minimal with x·f_{gI} ≤ 0. By Brown's criterion there is A ⊆ {1,...,gI−1} with σ_{gI} − 1 = ∑_{i∈A} σ_i, so zI = −f_{gI} + e0 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i lies in LI. Minimality of gI gives (xI − zI)·zI = −x·f_{gI} − 1 + ∑_{i∈A}(x·f_i − 1) + (x0 − 1) ≥ x0 − 2. Put z = zI + zF − e0 ∈ L. Using the dot-product decomposition, (x − z)·z = (xI − zI)·zI − (x0 − 1) + (xF − zF)·zF ≥ x0 − 2 ≥ −1. If this product is nonnegative, irreducibility forces x = z. If it equals −1, then necessarily x0 = 1, and with z′ = xI + zF − e0 one has (x − z′)·z′ = (xF − zF)·zF ≥ 0, so irreducibility forces x = z′. In both cases xF = zF, an interval sum, hence irreducible by Lemma 6.6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a p/q-changemaker lattice with q ≥ 2 and w0 = e0 + σ1 f1 + ... + σt ft. The following are equivalent: (i) L is indecomposable as an orthogonal direct sum; (ii) σ_i ≥ 1 for every 1 ≤ i ≤ t; (iii) L contains no vector of norm 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any i, the basis vector f_i lies in L exactly when σ_i = 0, because f_i is orthogonal to all w_j for j ≥ 1 and f_i·w0 = σ_i. No e_j lies in L, since some w_k has nonzero pairing with e_j. Thus norm-one vectors in L occur exactly as f_i with σ_i = 0, proving the equivalence of (ii) and (iii). If σ_i = 0, then ⟨f_i⟩ splits off orthogonally from L, so indecomposability implies (ii). Conversely assume all σ_i ≥ 1. For each k, Brown's criterion gives either σ_k = 1 + σ1 + ... + σ_{k−1}, in which case define u_k = −f_k + f_{k−1} + ... + f_1 + v0, or σ_k = ∑_{i∈A_k} σ_i for some A_k ⊆ {1,...,k−1}, in which case define u_k = −f_k + ∑_{i∈A_k} f_i. Then B = {u1,...,ut,v1,...,vm} is a basis for L. Lemmas 6.5 and 6.6 show every element of B is irreducible. If L = L1 ⊕ L2 were a nontrivial orthogonal splitting, every irreducible basis element would have to lie entirely in L1 or entirely in L2; otherwise its projections would give a nontrivial orthogonal decomposition. Since u1 = −f1 + v0, place u1 in L1. The pairings v1·u1 = −1 and vi·v_{i+1} = −1 force all vi into L1. Then for k > 1, if u_k contains v0, one has u1·u_k = ∥v0∥^2 − 1 ≥ 1, forcing u_k into L1; otherwise, with l = min A_k, one has u_k·u_l = −1 and induction forces u_k into L1. Hence all of B lies in L1, so L = L1 and the splitting was trivial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 7 preliminary observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be a reduced alternating diagram, and suppose its white Goeritz lattice Λ_D is isomorphic to a p/q-changemaker lattice L with p > q ≥ 2. Let V_D denote the set of vectors in L corresponding to the vertices of the white graph Γ_D under a fixed lattice isomorphism. Since D is reduced, Γ_D has no self-loops or cut-edges, and Λ_D has no norm-one vectors. Therefore Lemma 6.8 implies Λ_D is indecomposable, and Lemma 3.3 implies Γ_D is 2-connected and every vertex vector in V_D is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a direct chain of previously stated facts. Reduced alternating diagrams have white graphs without the degeneracies that would create norm-one vectors in the graph lattice. Under the changemaker identification, absence of norm-one vectors implies, by Lemma 6.8, that the changemaker lattice is indecomposable. Applying Lemma 3.3 to the graph lattice Λ(Γ_D) then shows Γ_D is 2-connected and all of its vertex classes are irreducible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup where a reduced alternating diagram D has white lattice Λ_D identified with a p/q-changemaker lattice L and where v0,...,vm is the fractional-part basis of LF, one may perform a sequence of flypes to obtain a diagram in which v1,...,vm are all vertices of the white graph, i.e. v1,...,vm ∈ V_D for the resulting diagram.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose c maximal with 1 ≤ c ≤ m such that v_c is not currently a vertex. By Lemma 6.6, v_c is irreducible in LF and hence, in the graph-lattice setting, Lemma 3.2 gives a subset R of current vertices with v_c = ∑_{x∈R} x. Therefore some current vertex u has fractional part uF = v_a + ... + v_b with a ≤ c ≤ b. Since v_c itself is not a vertex, u ≠ v_c, so a < b. The pairing v_b·u = v_b·uF = ∥v_b∥^2 − 1 is positive, which is impossible if v_b were a distinct current vertex because distinct graph vertices have nonpositive pairing. By maximality of c, this forces b = c. Then (u − v_c)·v_c = v_{c−1}·v_c = −1, so Lemma 4.1 applies and a flype replaces u by v_c among the vertex vectors. The already established vertices v_{c+1},...,vm remain vertices because v_c has nonpositive pairing with them except at its own place. Iterating decreases c and eventually makes every v1,...,vm a vertex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: After applying Lemma 7.2 so that v1,...,vm ∈ V_D, there is a unique vertex v ∈ V_D with v·e0 > 0, and it satisfies vF = v0. There is also a unique vertex w ∈ V_D with w·e0 < 0, and it satisfies wF = −(v0 + ... + vm).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let v be any vertex with v·e0 > 0. Lemma 6.7 says vF is irreducible in LF, so by Lemma 6.6 it has the form v0 + ... + v_b. If b > 0, then v_b is already a vertex and v_b·v = v_b·vF > 0, impossible for two distinct graph vertices. Thus vF = v0. Similarly, any vertex w with w·e0 < 0 has wF = −(v0 + ... + v_c); if c < m, then the vertex v_{c+1} has w·v_{c+1} = 1, again impossible, so wF = −(v0 + ... + vm). To prove uniqueness of the positive vertex, suppose u1 and u2 both have fractional part v0. If they are distinct, then U = u1 + u2 is a sum of distinct vertices and UF = 2v0. Let g be minimal with U·f_g ≤ 0. Brown's criterion gives A ⊆ {1,...,g−1} with σ_g − 1 = ∑_{i∈A} σ_i, and z = −f_g + v0 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i lies in L. Then (U − z)·z = −U·f_g − 1 + ∑_{i∈A}(U·f_i − 1) + ∥v0∥^2 ≥ −1 + ∥v0∥^2 > 0, contradicting Lemma 3.1 for the sum U of distinct vertices. Hence u1 = u2. Since the total sum of all graph vertices is zero in the graph lattice, pairing with e0 then also gives uniqueness of the negative vertex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same alternating-diagram/changemaker setup, let x = ∑_{u∈R} u be a sum of vertex vectors over some subset R ⊆ V_D. Then |x·f1| ≤ 2. Furthermore, if x is irreducible and x·e0 ≠ 0, then |x·f1| ≤ 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By replacing x by −x if needed, assume x·f1 ≥ 0. For the first bound, choose g > 1 minimal with x·f_g ≤ 0. Brown's criterion gives A ⊆ {1,...,g−1} with σ_g − 1 = ∑_{i∈A} σ_i, and z = −f_g + f1 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i lies in L. Let ε = z·f1, so ε is 1 or 2 depending on whether 1 ∉ A or 1 ∈ A. Lemma 3.1 applied to the vertex sum x gives 0 ≥ (x − z)·z = −x·f_g − 1 + ∑_{i∈A \ {1}}(x·f_i − 1) + ε(x·f1 − ε) ≥ −1 + ε(x·f1 − ε). Hence x·f1 ≤ ε + 1/ε ≤ 5/2, and integrality gives x·f1 ≤ 2. For the irreducible refinement, assume x·e0 > 0; the negative case follows by replacing x by −x. Lemmas 6.7 and 6.6 imply x·e0 = 1. Let g be minimal with x·f_g ≤ 0. If g = 1, take z = −f1 + xF. Irreducibility of x forces either x = z or (x − z)·z ≤ −1; the displayed calculation gives the desired bound. If g > 1, choose A as before. If 1 ∈ A, set z = −f_g + xF + ∑_{i∈A} f_i; if 1 ∉ A, set z = −f_g + f1 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i. Then z ∈ L and z·f1 = 1. Irreducibility gives either x = z or −1 ≥ (x − z)·z ≥ x·f1 − 2, forcing x·f1 ≤ 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let v and w be the unique vertices from Lemma 7.3, so vF = v0 and wF = −(v0 + ... + vm). Then v·w ≤ vF·wF + 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 6.5, z = −f1 + v0 is irreducible. Lemma 3.2 therefore represents z as a sum of vertices. Comparing e0-coordinates shows that this sum contains v and not w, so z − v + w is also a sum of vertices. Lemma 7.4 applied to this sum gives (z − v + w)·f1 = −1 − v·f1 + w·f1 ≥ −2, so either v·f1 ≤ 0 or w·f1 ≥ 0. Treat the case v·f1 ≤ 0; the other is analogous using z = f1 − (v0 + ... + vm). Lemma 7.4 gives v·f1 ∈ {0,−1}. If v·f1 = −1, then irreducibility forces v = z, and v·w = −w·f1 + vF·wF ≤ 1 + vF·wF. If v·f1 = 0, then (v − z)·z = −1 and v·w = (v − z)·w − w·f1 + vF·wF. Lemma 3.4 gives (v − z)·w ≤ 1, and Lemma 7.4 gives w·f1 ≥ −1; it remains only to rule out the simultaneous worst case. If (v − z)·w = 1, Lemma 3.4 produces a vertex u not among v,w with u·z = 1 and u + w irreducible. The equality u·z = 1 implies u has zero fractional part and u·f1 = −1. Applying Lemma 7.4 to the irreducible vector u + w gives −1 + w·f1 ≥ −1, hence w·f1 ≥ 0, completing the bound.

proof_techniques.name: Graph-lattice cut-vector calculus
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the graph lattice Λ(G), where a subset R of vertices gives a cut vector [R]. The key inequality is Lemma 3.1: for any lattice vector z, ([R] − z)·z ≤ 0. This turns arbitrary decompositions of [R] into edgewise constraints and is the basis for the characterization of irreducible graph-lattice elements as exactly those [R] for which R and its complement are connected.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a weighted-tree lattice can be compared to, embedded in, or reduced to a graph-lattice or cut-lattice situation, this gives a direct way to prove irreducibility of selected vertex sums. The connected-support criterion suggests looking for a vertex or branch whose removal leaves a connected complement in an auxiliary graph.

proof_techniques.name: Certifying irreducibility by coordinate sign splitting
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.5 proves irreducibility for vectors with one negative coordinate and all other nonzero coordinates equal to +1 in an orthonormal ambient lattice. If z = x + y and x·y ≥ 0, then because the coordinates of z are in {−1,0,1}, every coordinate product (x_i y_i) is ≤ 0; the nonnegative total pairing forces every product to be zero. Thus one summand receives a nonnegative coordinate vector. Orthogonality to a positive vector w0, together with recursive orthogonality to the plumbing-chain vectors wj, forces that summand to vanish.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust template for proving a vertex is irreducible after finding a convenient coordinate model: show any nonnegative-pairing split must separate coordinates, then use positivity/orthogonality constraints to kill one summand. It may be useful if the weighted tree lattice is embedded into a diagonal lattice or admits a basis with similar {−1,0,1} vertex coordinates.

proof_techniques.name: Brown changemaker subsets as test-vector constructors
proof_techniques.description: The changemaker condition is used through Brown's theorem to build subsets A of earlier indices with prescribed coefficient sums, such as ∑_{i∈A} σ_i = σ_g − 1. This allows construction of special lattice vectors like −f_g + e0 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i or −f_g + f1 + ∑_{i∈A} f_i. Pairing these test vectors with a suspected sum of vertices and applying Lemma 3.1 yields strong coefficient bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In an analogous weighted-tree setting, one can look for positive linear relations among coordinates or weights that let one build low-norm test vectors. Such vectors can be paired against hypothetical decompositions to force inequalities contradicting reducibility.

proof_techniques.name: Indecomposability from an irreducible connected basis
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.8 proves indecomposability by constructing an explicit basis B of irreducible elements and showing the graph of nonzero pairings among basis elements is connected. In any orthogonal splitting L = L1 ⊕ L2, an irreducible element must lie wholly in one summand; nonzero pairings then force adjacent basis elements into the same summand, and connectivity forces the whole basis into one summand.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A solver could try to build a basis or spanning set of irreducible candidates in the tree lattice and connect them by nonzero pairings. Even if the final goal is only one irreducible vertex, proving a stronger indecomposability statement for a sublattice may force the existence of such a vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Projection of irreducibility to a fractional part
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.7 decomposes a changemaker lattice vector x into integer and fractional components xI and xF with a correction in the shared e0-coordinate. The proof constructs interval vectors zF in the fractional chain and matching integer test vectors zI; inequalities show that if x is irreducible, then xF must equal a single interval sum. This transfers irreducibility information from the full lattice to a simpler chain-like lattice LF where irreducibles are classified.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, one might decompose the lattice along branches emanating from a distinguished vertex and try to prove that irreducibility or reducibility of a vertex is controlled by its projection to a branch lattice. The paper's method shows how to combine branch inequalities with a central coordinate correction.

proof_techniques.name: Algebraic graph modification from a near-splitting
