proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.4 and Lemma 4.1 analyze the special situation where a graph-lattice vertex v decomposes as v = x + y with x·y = −1. Rather than being an arbitrary decomposition, it comes from a precise cut-edge in G \ {v}; in alternating diagrams this corresponds to a flype that replaces the vertex data v,u1,u2 by x,y,u1+u2 while preserving the lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a vertex of the weighted tree is reducible or nearly reducible, an analogous structural description of the decomposition might permit induction or local modification of the tree. The idea is to turn algebraic splittings into graph-theoretic separations.

proof_techniques.name: Coefficient bounds for sums of vertices
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 7.4 bounds |x·f1| for any x that is a sum of graph vertices, and improves the bound when x is irreducible with nonzero e0-coordinate. The method is to choose a changemaker test vector z and apply the universal graph-lattice inequality (x − z)·z ≤ 0; in the irreducible case, the dichotomy that either x = z or (x − z)·z must be negative sharpens the estimate.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original problem, if one can identify a coordinate or linear functional measuring the defect w(v) − d(v), similar coefficient bounds may limit how all vertices could decompose and force at least one vertex to be irreducible.

proof_techniques.name: Using absence of norm-one vectors to force graph vertices irreducible
proof_techniques.description: In Section 7, a reduced alternating diagram with changemaker lattice has no norm-one vectors. Lemma 6.8 converts this into indecomposability of the changemaker lattice, and Lemma 3.3 then converts indecomposability of the graph lattice into 2-connectedness of the white graph and irreducibility of every graph vertex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original lattice is positive definite, and if one can rule out norm-one summands or orthogonal decompositions in a suitable sublattice, this chain suggests a route from indecomposability to existence of irreducible vertex elements.

other_useful_info: The paper states that all graph-lattice results in Section 3, including Lemmas 3.1–3.4, are recalled from McCoy [12], where complete proofs are available. Brown's subset-sum criterion for changemaker sequences is cited as Brown [1] and is the combinatorial engine behind the test-vector constructions. For reduced alternating diagrams, the white Goeritz lattice is identified with a graph lattice when the white graph has no self-loops or cut-edges; flypes preserve the lattice while changing the distinguished vertex set. The most reusable structural chain in the paper is: no norm-one vectors in a changemaker lattice implies indecomposable; indecomposable graph lattice implies 2-connected graph; 2-connected graph implies all graph vertices are irreducible.
why_relevant: Applies generalized changemaker lattices to alternating-link Goeritz lattices. Its structural arguments about lattices that are simultaneously graph-like and changemaker-like are relevant to proving irreducibility statements for vertices.

title: Bounds on Alternating Surgery Slopes
overall_summary: The paper proves bounds on Dehn surgery slopes yielding branched double covers of alternating links, using Greene-Gibbons changemaker lattices, Heegaard Floer correction terms, and graph-lattice properties of Goeritz forms. Its main topological results are not directly about weighted trees, but the lattice-theoretic middle of the paper is highly relevant: it records a dictionary between positive-definite lattices with obtuse superbases and graph lattices, and it characterizes irreducible vectors in graph lattices by connected cuts. The paper also uses a powerful mutation operation for obtuse superbases: if a vertex of an obtuse superbase splits as x+y with x·y=-1, one can replace three superbase elements and obtain another obtuse superbase. For the original problem, the most useful ingredients are Section 3 and the proof pattern in Lemma 4.4, where irreducible small-norm vectors are forced into an obtuse superbase and then local graph-degree constraints rule out possible decompositions or coefficient patterns.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2 (Brown subset-sum criterion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ=(σ1,...,σt) be a nondecreasing tuple of nonnegative integers. Then every integer k with 0≤k≤σ1+...+σt can be written as k=sum_{i in A} σ_i for some subset A of {1,...,t} if and only if 0≤σ1≤1 and, for every i with 1<i≤t, one has σ_{i-1}≤σ_i≤σ1+...+σ_{i-1}+1. In the paper, a tuple satisfying these inequalities is said to satisfy the changemaker condition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this classical result of Brown. The necessity is the usual no-gap argument: if σ1>1 then k=1 is not representable, and if σ_i>σ1+...+σ_{i-1}+1 then the integer σ1+...+σ_{i-1}+1 cannot be represented using earlier terms and is too small to use σ_i or any later term. For sufficiency, induct on t. By induction the previous terms represent every integer between 0 and S=σ1+...+σ_{t-1}; since σ_t≤S+1, the intervals [0,S] and [σ_t,σ_t+S] overlap or touch, so every integer from 0 to S+σ_t is represented either without σ_t or as σ_t plus a represented integer from the previous terms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.1 graph-lattice pairing identities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops. For disjoint subsets R,S of V, let e(R,S) be the number of edges between R and S, and put d(R)=e(R,V−R). Define a free abelian group on V with symmetric form v·w=d(v) if v=w and v·w=−e(v,w) if v≠w, where d(v)=d({v}). For R⊆V write [R]=sum_{v in R} v. Then for a vertex v and subset R one has v·[R]=−e(v,R) if v is not in R, and v·[R]=e(v,V−R) if v is in R. In particular [V] pairs trivially with every vector, the form descends to the quotient Lambda(G)=(free abelian group on V)/Z[V], and for connected G this quotient form is positive definite. Also [R]^2=d(R).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The displayed formula follows by direct expansion. If v is outside R, only edges from v to vertices of R contribute, each with coefficient −1, giving −e(v,R). If v lies in R, the diagonal contribution is d(v), and edges from v to other vertices of R subtract e(v,R−{v}), leaving the number of edges from v to V−R. Taking R=V gives v·[V]=0 for every vertex v, so the form descends to the quotient by Z[V]. Summing v·[R] over v in R gives [R]^2=e(R,V−R)=d(R). Positive definiteness on the quotient is the standard graph-Laplacian fact: the quadratic form can be written as a sum over edges of squared coefficient differences, whose kernel consists exactly of constant functions when G is connected.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 (irreducibles in graph lattices)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected graph with no self-loops, and let Lambda(G) be its positive-definite graph lattice as above. A nonzero vector x in Lambda(G) is irreducible, meaning it cannot be written as x=a+b with nonzero a,b in Lambda(G) and a·b≥0, if and only if x=[R] for some subset R⊆V such that both induced subgraphs on R and on V−R are connected. Here [R] is understood in the quotient Lambda(G), so [R]=−[V−R].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this result and refers to McCoy [17] for proof. The key mechanism is the graph-Laplacian level-set decomposition. Represent an element x by an integer-valued function on V, modulo adding constants; after changing sign if necessary, take the minimum coefficient to be 0 and write x as a sum of nested cut vectors [R_j], where R_j is the set of vertices whose coefficient is at least j. For nested subsets B⊆A one has [A]·[B]=e(B,V−A)≥0, so if more than one level occurs, x splits into two nonzero summands with nonnegative pairing and is reducible. Thus irreducibles must be represented by a single cut [R], up to sign. If R is disconnected, say R=A union B with no edges between A and B, then [R]=[A]+[B] and [A]·[B]=0, so [R] is reducible. If V−R is disconnected, use [R]=−[V−R] and split the complement similarly. Conversely, the cited proof in [17] shows that when both sides of the cut are connected, every attempted decomposition of [R] has negative cross-pairing unless one summand is zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 (2-connected graphs, irreducible vertices, and indecomposable graph lattices)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite connected graph with no self-loops, and let Lambda(G) be its graph lattice. The following are equivalent: (i) G is 2-connected, meaning that deleting any vertex does not disconnect G; (ii) every vertex class v in Lambda(G) is irreducible; (iii) Lambda(G) is indecomposable, meaning it is not an orthogonal direct sum L1⊕L2 of two nonzero sublattices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this and cites [17]. The equivalence between (i) and (ii) follows immediately from Lemma 3.1: the vertex v is the cut vector [{v}], the set {v} is connected, and v is irreducible exactly when V−{v} is connected. The equivalence with indecomposability is the usual block decomposition of a graph lattice. If G has a cut vertex, G is a one-point union of two nontrivial connected subgraphs, and the graph lattice splits orthogonally according to the corresponding blocks. Conversely, an orthogonal splitting of Lambda(G) separates the irreducible vertex/cut data and forces a cut vertex in G; the detailed argument is supplied in [17].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3 (structure of a -1 splitting of a graph-lattice vertex)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a 2-connected graph and let v be a vertex of G, viewed as an element of the graph lattice Lambda(G). Suppose x,y in Lambda(G) satisfy v=x+y and x·y=−1. Then there is a cut edge e in the graph G with v deleted. If R and S are the vertex sets of the two connected components of (G with v deleted) with the edge e deleted, then, up to swapping x and y, one has {x,y}={[R]+v,[S]+v}. If u1 and u2 are the endpoints of e, then u1 and u2 are the unique vertices distinct from v such that x·u1=1 and y·u2=1. Moreover, every vertex w not in {v,u1,u2} satisfies w·x≤0 and w·y≤0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from [17] rather than proving it. The content is a rigidity statement obtained by combining the connected-cut description of irreducibles with the equality v=x+y. Since G is 2-connected, the vertex v is irreducible, and the equality with x·y=−1 forces x and y to be cut vectors corresponding to two complementary regions of G−{v}. The numerical condition x·y=−1 says exactly that there is a single edge between these two regions; that edge is a cut edge of G−{v}. The endpoint assertions follow by computing pairings with vertex classes using the cut-pairing identity v·[R]=−e(v,R) or e(v,V−R): only the endpoint of the unique edge on each side can pair positively with the corresponding summand, and all other pairings are nonpositive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4 (obtuse superbase gives a graph lattice)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a positive-definite integral lattice of rank r. Suppose L admits an obtuse superbase B={v0,...,vr}, meaning that v1,...,vr is a basis for L, v0+...+vr=0, and vi·vj≤0 for all distinct i,j. Construct a graph G_B with vertex set B and with |vi·vj| edges between vi and vj for i≠j. Then G_B is connected, and L is isomorphic as a lattice to the graph lattice Lambda(G_B).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First prove connectedness. If R is the vertex set of a nonempty connected component of G_B, then [R]=sum_{x in R} x pairs as zero with every superbase element: there are no graph edges from R to its complement, equivalently all cross-pairings vanish, and the sum relation gives zero pairing with vertices inside R as well. Since L is positive definite, [R]=0. The only linear relation among the r+1 superbase elements is v0+...+vr=0, because any r of them form a basis; hence R must be all of B. For the lattice isomorphism, send each graph vertex of G_B to the corresponding vector in L. For off-diagonal terms, the graph-lattice form gives −e(vi,vj)=−|vi·vj|=vi·vj because the superbase is obtuse. For diagonal terms, the degree of vk in G_B is sum_{i≠k}|vk·vi|=−sum_{i≠k} vk·vi=vk·vk, using vk·(v0+...+vr)=0. Thus all pairings agree, and since the ranks agree and the images span L, this gives an isomorphism Lambda(G_B)≅L.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5 (mutation of an obtuse superbase along a -1 splitting)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be an indecomposable positive-definite integral lattice with an obtuse superbase B. Suppose v in B can be written as v=x+y with x,y in L and x·y=−1. Then there are unique u1,u2 in B such that u1·x>0 and u2·y>0, and the set B'=(B−{v,u1,u2}) union {x,y,u1+u2} is also an obtuse superbase for L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Proposition 3.4 to identify L with the graph lattice of G_B. Since L is indecomposable, Lemma 3.2 gives that G_B is 2-connected. Applying Lemma 3.3 to the decomposition v=x+y yields a cut edge e in G_B−{v}; deleting e splits G_B−{v} into components with vertex sets R and S, and, up to order, x=[R]+v and y=[S]+v. The endpoints u1,u2 of e are uniquely characterized by u1·x>0 and u2·y>0. Now replace v,u1,u2 by x,y,u1+u2. The sum of all elements is unchanged because x+y=v, so the superbase relation remains true. Pairings among unchanged elements remain nonpositive; Lemma 3.3 gives nonpositive pairings of x and y with all irrelevant old vertices; x·y=−1; and the pairings involving u1+u2 are checked directly from the fact that u1,u2 are the endpoints of the single cut edge. Thus the new set is obtuse. It also spans with the correct one linear relation, equivalently the corresponding graph G_B' is obtained from G_B by the local operation shown in the paper's Figure 2, so B' is an obtuse superbase.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2 (reducing non-integral changemaker lattices with obtuse superbases)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p/q=n−r/q with q>r≥1, and let L_{p/q}=<w0,w1,...,wl>^⊥ be the p/q-changemaker lattice in the standard positive lattice with orthonormal basis e0,...,es,f1,...,ft, where w0=e0+σ1 f1+...+σt f_t. If L_{p/q} admits an obtuse superbase, then the integral changemaker lattices L_n=<w0>^⊥ inside the span of e0,f1,...,ft and L_{n−1}=<w0−e0>^⊥ inside the span of f1,...,ft both admit obtuse superbases. Moreover, if σ_t>1, then the obtuse superbase for L_{n−1} can be chosen to contain a vector x with x·f1=−2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses two cited structural results, [16, Proposition 7.7] and results from [17]. From the assumed obtuse superbase on L_{p/q}, [16] implies that the half-integral changemaker lattice L_{n−1/2}=<w0,e1−e0>^⊥ inside the span of e1,e0,f1,...,ft has an obtuse superbase B. Results from [17] allow B to be chosen so that exactly two vertices v,w of B pair nontrivially with e0, with v·w≤−1, v=−f1+e0+e1, w·e0=w·e1=−1, and, if some σ_k>1, also w·f1=−1. For L_{n−1}, replace v,w by v+w. This new vector and all unchanged vectors are orthogonal to e0 and e1, so they lie in the f_i-span and in <w0−e0>^⊥. The resulting set B' spans L_{n−1} and is obtuse; graph-theoretically it contracts an edge between v and w. If σ_t>1, the chosen vector x=v+w has x·f1=−2. For L_n, replace v,w by v−e1 and w+e1. These vectors are orthogonal to e1 and lie in the span of e0,f_i; the same pairing check shows that the resulting set B'' is an obtuse superbase for <w0>^⊥, graph-theoretically corresponding to deleting an edge between v and w.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3 (Greene decomposability constraint for changemaker lattices)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=<w0>^⊥ be an integral changemaker lattice inside a standard positive lattice with orthonormal basis f1,...,ft, where w0=σ1 f1+...+σt f_t, all σ_i≥1, and σ_t>1. Let m≤t be the minimal index such that σ_m>1. If L is decomposable as a nontrivial orthogonal direct sum, then σ_m=m−1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as Greene [8, Lemma 5.1] and does not reproduce the proof. The result is a strong rigidity statement: after the initial run of unit coefficients σ1=...=σ_{m−1}=1, decomposability of the orthogonal complement can occur only when the first coefficient larger than one is exactly the number of preceding unit coefficients, namely m−1. In the paper it is used as a black box to dispatch the decomposable case before applying graph-lattice arguments to the indecomposable case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4 (coefficient bound for changemaker lattices admitting obtuse superbases)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=<w0>^⊥ be an integral changemaker lattice inside a standard positive lattice with orthonormal basis f1,...,ft, where w0=σ1 f1+...+σt f_t, all σ_i≥1, and the changemaker sequence has a nonempty stable part. Let m be minimal with σ_m>1, so σ1=...=σ_{m−1}=1 and the stable coefficients are (σ_m,...,σ_t). If L admits an obtuse superbase, then σ_m≥m−2 and ||w0||^2≤1+σ_m+sum_{i=m}^t σ_i^2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If L is decomposable, Lemma 4.3 gives σ_m=m−1, and the desired inequalities follow immediately from ||w0||^2=(m−1)+sum_{i=m}^t σ_i^2. Thus assume L is indecomposable. For 2≤i≤m−1 define the norm-two vectors v_i=f_i−f_{i−1}, which lie in L because the corresponding σ coefficients are both 1. The proof uses Lemma 3.5 iteratively to find an obtuse superbase containing all v_2,...,v_{m−1}. Start with any obtuse superbase B. If v_2 is absent, its irreducibility lets one express it as a sum of vertices in the graph-lattice model from B; some vertex u in B has u·v_2>0, and then u=v_2+(u−v_2) with (u−v_2)·v_2=−1, so Lemma 3.5 replaces u and yields a new obtuse superbase containing v_2. Inductively, if v_2,...,v_{k−1} are already present and v_k is absent, then v_k·v_{k−1}=−1 forces a vertex u with u·v_k=−u·v_{k−1}=1; again Lemma 3.5 inserts v_k, and the nonpositive pairings with the earlier v_i ensure they remain in the superbase. Now suppose, toward the coefficient bound, that σ_m=m−b for some b with 2≤b≤m−2, i.e. that σ_m≤m−2. Choose the sign of the vector z=f_m−f_{m−1}−...−f_b so that z lies in L and z·v_b=−1. The vector z is irreducible, hence is represented as a sum of vertices of the current obtuse superbase. Since z·v_b=−1, some summand u must satisfy u·v_b=−1 and u·z=1. But v_b is a norm-two superbase vertex, so in the graph G_B it has total degree 2; for b≥3 the two already-known adjacent superbase vertices v_{b−1} and v_{b+1} both pair −1 with v_b and pair 0 with z. Therefore no third vertex u with the required pairings can exist. This contradiction rules out b≥3, leaving only b≤2, equivalently σ_m≥m−2. The norm estimate is then just ||w0||^2=(m−1)+sum_{i=m}^t σ_i^2≤1+σ_m+sum_{i=m}^t σ_i^2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.5 (quadratic changemaker estimate under an obtuse superbase)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=<σ1 f1+...+σt f_t>^⊥ be an integral changemaker lattice in a standard positive lattice, and assume L admits an obtuse superbase and σ_t>1. Then sum_{i=1}^t σ_i^2≤2 sum_{i=1}^t σ_i(σ_i−1)+3. In the paper's main application there are no zero σ_i; if zero coefficients occur, they contribute nothing to either side and split off norm-one summands, so the estimate is applied to the positive part.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let m be minimal with σ_m>1. Lemma 4.4 gives sum σ_i^2=||w0||^2≤sum_{i=m}^t σ_i^2+σ_m+1 after ignoring any zero coefficients. For every i≥m, σ_i≥2, hence σ_i^2≤2σ_i(σ_i−1). For the first stable coefficient, the slightly sharper combined inequality σ_m^2+σ_m≤2σ_m(σ_m−1)+2 holds. Therefore sum σ_i^2≤sum_{i=m}^t σ_i^2+σ_m+1≤2 sum_{i=m}^t σ_i(σ_i−1)+3. Since σ_i=1 for positive coefficients before m, their contributions σ_i(σ_i−1) vanish, and the right side is 2 sum_{i=1}^t σ_i(σ_i−1)+3.

proof_techniques.name: Obtuse-superbase to graph-lattice conversion
proof_techniques.description: A positive-definite lattice with an obtuse superbase B={v0,...,vr} can be replaced by a graph G_B whose vertices are the superbase elements and whose edge multiplicities are |vi·vj|. Proposition 3.4 shows that the lattice is exactly the graph lattice Lambda(G_B). This converts algebraic questions about pairings and decompositions into graph-theoretic questions about cuts, connectivity, cut vertices, and cut edges.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original lattice has a natural basis with nonpositive off-diagonal pairings. Adding the negative of the sum of all basis vectors gives a candidate superbase; its pairing with a vertex u is d(u)−w(u), so the hypothesis w(v)<d(v) measures precisely a controlled failure of obtuseness. This suggests trying to modify the basis or isolate the exceptional vertex so that graph-lattice connectivity tools become available.

proof_techniques.name: Irreducibility as connected-cut condition
proof_techniques.description: In a graph lattice, Lemma 3.1 says that irreducible vectors are exactly cut vectors [R] for which both R and its complement induce connected subgraphs. The proof is driven by representing arbitrary lattice elements as sums of nested level-set cuts and observing that nested cuts have nonnegative pairings, so any multi-level element is reducible. Disconnectedness of either side gives an explicit zero-pairing decomposition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a generator in the weighted-tree lattice can be compared to a cut vector in an associated graph lattice, irreducibility can be certified by connectedness of the corresponding cut. Conversely, assuming every vertex is reducible may force a collection of graph-like disconnections that can be contradicted using the tree structure or positive definiteness.

proof_techniques.name: Rigidity of -1 decompositions and superbase mutation
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.3 identifies a decomposition v=x+y with x·y=−1 of a graph-lattice vertex v as coming from a unique cut edge in G−{v}. Lemma 3.5 then uses this structure to mutate an obtuse superbase: replace v and two endpoint vertices u1,u2 by x,y,u1+u2, preserving the obtuse-superbase property. This is a local graph operation expressed in lattice terms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Even though the original definition of reducibility uses a·b≥0, decompositions of nearby or modified vectors may naturally produce cross-term −1. If an obtuse or near-obtuse superbase can be built from the tree lattice, this mutation gives a way to normalize the basis while keeping control of pairings.

proof_techniques.name: Forcing small-norm chain vectors into an obtuse superbase
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 4.4, the author repeatedly applies the superbase mutation lemma to force vectors of the form f_i−f_{i−1} into an obtuse superbase. The essential ingredients are that these norm-two vectors are irreducible and that their pairings form a path: v_i·v_{i−1}=−1 and other relevant pairings are nonpositive. Once the chain is included, a candidate vector supported on an interval is shown impossible by a local degree count at a norm-two superbase vertex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Weighted trees contain many path-like configurations. If one can produce irreducible small-norm vectors associated to edges or paths, this technique suggests inserting them into a controlled basis and then using local degree constraints to rule out the assumption that all original vertices are reducible.

proof_techniques.name: Norm and degree-count obstructions
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Lemma 4.4 uses the fact that a norm-two vertex in the graph associated to an obtuse superbase has graph degree two, so once two incident edges are accounted for no further vector can pair nontrivially with it. More generally, in a positive-definite integral lattice, very small norm strongly restricts possible reducible decompositions because nonzero vectors have positive integral norm and a nonnegative cross-term increases the norm of a sum.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the original weighted-tree lattice, any vertex of weight 1 is immediately irreducible by a norm count, and vertices of weight 2 become irreducible if norm-one summands are excluded. Local degree-count arguments may also interact directly with the condition w(v)<d(v), since that condition compares a vertex norm with the number of negative pairings incident to it.

other_useful_info: Section 3 explicitly says that its graph-lattice statements, including Lemmas 3.1, 3.2, and 3.3, are quoted with proof from McCoy [17], Alternating knots with unknotting number one. Lemma 4.3 is quoted from Greene [8, Lemma 5.1]. An implicit observation used in Lemma 4.4 is that norm-two vectors are irreducible in an integral positive-definite lattice with no norm-one elements: if z=a+b with a,b nonzero and a·b≥0, then z^2=a^2+b^2+2a·b is at least 4 when all nonzero norms are at least 2. In graph lattices arising from obtuse superbases, the norm of a superbase element equals its graph degree; this lets local norm information be read as a graph-degree constraint.
why_relevant: Studies uniqueness and structure of changemaker lattices arising from sharp definite 4-manifolds. The norm and coefficient restrictions are useful tools for ruling out decompositions of lattice elements.

title: Alternating surgeries
overall_summary: The thesis develops the changemaker surgery obstruction and applies it to alternating surgeries, unknotting number one, and rational tangle replacement. Its core technical machinery combines sharp 4-manifolds, Donaldson diagonalization, Heegaard Floer d-invariants, and a detailed lattice-theoretic analysis of changemaker and graph lattices. For the original lattice problem, the most directly useful material is Chapter 4: it fixes the exact irreducibility definition, proves strong criteria for irreducible elements in graph lattices, and gives methods for proving irreducibility by analyzing possible decompositions x+y with x·y ≥ 0. Especially relevant are Lemmas 4.21 and 4.22, which characterize irreducible elements in reduced graph Laplacian lattices as sums over connected cuts, and Lemma 4.23, which relates irreducibility of vertices to 2-connectedness and indecomposability. The changemaker-lattice lemmas provide complementary techniques: coordinate-splitting in diagonal lattices, nonnegativity obstructions, and standard-basis irreducibility arguments.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Chapter 4 lattice terminology
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In Chapter 4, a positive-definite integer lattice L means a finitely generated free abelian group equipped with a positive-definite symmetric integer-valued bilinear form, written (x,y) ↦ x·y, with norm ||x||^2 = x·x. The lattice L is indecomposable if it cannot be written as an orthogonal direct sum L = L1 ⊕ L2 with L1 and L2 nonzero sublattices. A vector v in L is irreducible if there do not exist nonzero x,y in L such that v = x+y and x·y ≥ 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition/convention used throughout the lattice chapter. It is exactly the irreducibility notion used later in graph and changemaker lattices: reducibility is detected by a decomposition v=x+y whose cross-term is nonnegative, not merely by a decomposition into nonzero vectors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.13 (Brown)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (σ1,...,σt) be a nondecreasing tuple of positive integers. It satisfies the changemaker condition, meaning that every integer n with 1 ≤ n ≤ σ1+...+σt can be written as n = Σ_{i in A} σi for some subset A of {1,...,t}, if and only if σ1=1 and σi ≤ σ1+...+σ_{i-1}+1 for every i with 1<i≤t.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Necessity is by gaps: representing 1 forces σ1=1, and if σi exceeded 1 plus the previous partial sum, then σ1+...+σ_{i-1}+1 could not be represented using the first i−1 coefficients and is too small to use σi. Sufficiency is the standard induction on i: if all integers in [0,S_{i-1}] are representable using σ1,...,σ_{i-1}, and σi≤S_{i-1}+1, then using or not using σi gives all integers in [0,S_i].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Z^n have orthonormal basis e1,...,en, let Λ be any sublattice of Z^n, and let z in Λ have the form z = Σ_{i in A} ei − Σ_{j in B} ej for disjoint subsets A,B of {1,...,n}. If z=x+y for some x,y in Λ with x·y ≥ 0, then there are subsets A′⊆A and B′⊆B such that x = Σ_{i in A′} ei − Σ_{j in B′} ej and y = Σ_{i in A\A′} ei − Σ_{j in B\B′} ej.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write y=z−x and compute x·y coordinatewise. For i in A the contribution is (x·ei)(1−x·ei), for i in B it is −(x·ei)(1+x·ei), and for i outside A∪B it is −(x·ei)^2. Since x has integer coordinates, every summand is ≤0. The assumption x·y≥0 forces every summand to be exactly 0. Hence x·ei is 0 or 1 on A, 0 or −1 on B, and 0 outside A∪B, giving the asserted coordinate split; y is the complementary split.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.1 coordinate model for changemaker lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For p/q=[a0,...,al]− with a0≥1 and ai≥2 for i≥1, a p/q-changemaker lattice is an orthogonal complement L=⟨w0,...,wl⟩^⊥ inside a standard diagonal lattice Z^N, where wi·wj=ai if i=j, wi·wj=−1 if |i−j|=1, and wi·wj=0 if |i−j|>1, together with extra coordinate restrictions. In the coordinate convention used in Chapter 4, if p/q is an integer then one chooses an orthonormal basis f1,...,ft with w0=σ1 f1+...+σt ft, where σ1≤...≤σt are the changemaker coefficients. If p/q is not an integer, one chooses orthonormal coordinates f1,...,ft,e0,...,es with w0=e0+σ1 f1+...+σt ft and, for k≥1, wk=−e_{α_{k−1}}+e_{α_{k−1}+1}+...+e_{α_k}, where α0=0 and αk=Σ_{i=1}^k(ai−1). A coefficient σk is tight if σk=1+σ1+...+σ_{k−1}; the lattice is tight if some σk with k>1 is tight, and slack otherwise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the coordinate normalization and terminology adopted before the changemaker-lattice arguments. The special form of w0 encodes the changemaker condition, while the later wk form a chain in the e-coordinates. The normalization is what makes it possible to construct explicit test vectors and standard bases.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a p/q-changemaker lattice in the coordinate model of Section 4.1. In the integer case L=⟨w0⟩^⊥ with w0=Σ σi fi. In the non-integer case L=⟨w0,...,wl⟩^⊥ with coordinates f1,...,ft,e0,...,es and fractional vectors μ0,...,μm spanning the fractional part LF=⟨w1,...,wl⟩^⊥∩⟨e0,...,es⟩, where μ0=e0+...+e_{β1} and μi=−e_{βi}+e_{βi+1}+...+e_{β_{i+1}} for suitable indices βi determined by the gaps among the αj. For each k, choose Ak⊆{1,...,k−2} with Σ_{i in Ak} σi=σk−σ_{k−1} whenever σk is not tight, and define νk by νk=−fk+f_{k−1}+Σ_{i in Ak} fi in the non-tight case; if σk is tight, define νk=−fk+f_{k−1}+...+f1+μ0 in the non-integer case and νk=−fk+f_{k−1}+...+f2+2f1 in the integer case. Then the standard set S={ν1,...,νt,μ1,...,μm} in the non-integer case, respectively S={ν2,...,νt} in the integer case, is a basis for L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Linear independence is proved by looking at the largest coordinate that appears with a nonzero coefficient: for a nontrivial combination of νi, the largest f-coordinate detects the top nonzero coefficient; if all ν-coefficients vanish, the largest relevant e-coordinate detects the top nonzero μ-coefficient. To prove spanning in the non-integer case, first decompose any x in L as x=xI+xF−(x·e0)e0 with xI in the integral part and xF in the fractional part. If xI=0 then Lemma 4.7 says x lies in the span of μ1,...,μm. Otherwise, induct on the largest f-index k with x·fk≠0; adding (x·fk)νk kills that top f-coordinate and lowers the induction. The integer case is the same induction without the fractional part.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a p/q-changemaker lattice in the coordinate model of Section 4.1, with ambient orthonormal coordinates f1,...,ft and, in the non-integer case, also e0,...,es. If x in L satisfies x·fi ≥ 0 for every i and x·ej ≥ 0 for every j in the non-integer case, then x=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the integer case, x·w0=Σ σi(x·fi)=0 because x is orthogonal to w0; all summands are nonnegative and σi>0, so every x·fi=0. In the non-integer case, x·w0=(x·e0)+Σ σi(x·fi)=0, so nonnegativity gives x·e0=0 and x·fi=0 for all i. For any remaining e_j, choose k such that the vector W=w0+...+wk has W·e_j=1 and all relevant coordinates nonnegative; then 0=W·x≥x·e_j≥0. Thus every ambient coordinate of x is zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a p/q-changemaker lattice in the coordinate model of Section 4.1. Any vector z in L of either of the following coordinate forms is irreducible: (i) z=−fk+Σ_{i in A} fi+Σ_{j in B} ej for some subsets A⊆{1,...,t} and B⊆{0,...,s}; or (ii) z=−ek+Σ_{j in C} ej for some C⊆{k+1,...,s}. In the integer case only the f-coordinate form occurs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose z=x+y with x,y in L and x·y≥0. The vector z has coefficients only 0, 1, and a single −1 in the ambient diagonal basis, so Proposition 4.11 applies and shows that x and y split the positive and negative coordinates of z. One of the two summands has no negative coordinate in the ambient basis; all of its coordinates are nonnegative. Lemma 4.12 then forces that summand to be zero. Therefore z admits no nontrivial reducibility decomposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a p/q-changemaker lattice with q≤2, written in the coordinate convention of Section 4.1. If some changemaker coefficient σk is tight, then the vector z=−fk+f_{k−1}+...+f2+2f1 in L is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper proves the half-integer case q=2 explicitly; q=1 is analogous. Write L=⟨w0,e1−e0⟩^⊥. Suppose z=x+y with x,y in L and x·y≥0. Expanding x·y in coordinates gives a sum in which all terms are nonpositive except possibly the f1-term (x·f1)(2−x·f1), which is at most 1. This forces x·e0=y·e0=0, x·fi in {0,1} for 2≤i<k, and x·fk in {0,−1}; assume x·fk=−1. If y≠0, then y has a unique negative f-coordinate, say −fg with g>k, and the forced coordinate pattern gives y=−fg+Σ_{i in A} fi with y·f1=1. Orthogonality y·w0=0 then implies σg=Σ_{i in A}σi<σk, contradicting g>k and nondecreasing σi. Hence y=0, so z is irreducible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 4.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every element of a standard basis for a changemaker lattice, as constructed in Proposition 4.9, is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The standard basis vectors are exactly of the coordinate types covered by Lemma 4.13, except for tight integer or half-integer vectors, which are covered by Lemma 4.14. Thus each standard basis element is irreducible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a non-integer p/q-changemaker lattice, and let LF be its fractional part with basis μ0,...,μm as in Section 4.1.1. A nonzero vector xF in LF is irreducible if and only if xF=±(μa+...+μb) for some 0≤a≤b≤m.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write xF=Σ ci μi. Since irreducibility is unchanged by sign, choose the first nonzero coefficient positive. Let a be minimal with ca≠0 and let b≥a be minimal with c_{b+1}≤0. Set z=μa+...+μb. Direct calculation using the chain pairings μi·μ_{i+1}=−1 and nonnegative norms gives (xF−z)·z≥0. If xF is irreducible, this forces xF=z. Conversely, for z=μa+...+μb, its ambient e-coordinates have one possible −1 and otherwise 0 or 1 (or all 0 or 1 when a=0). If z=x+y with x·y≥0, Proposition 4.11 splits coordinates; one summand has nonnegative e-coordinates and zero e0-coordinate, hence lies in L with all ambient coordinates nonnegative. Lemma 4.12 forces that summand to be zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a non-integer p/q-changemaker lattice, and write every x in L uniquely as x=xI+xF−(x·e0)e0, where xI is in the integral part and xF is in the fractional part LF. If x is irreducible in L, then its fractional part xF is irreducible in LF.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If x·e0=0, then xI and xF both lie in L and are orthogonal; irreducibility of x forces one to vanish, so xF is either 0 or x and is irreducible. If x0=x·e0>0 after changing sign, write xF=Σ ci μi with c0=x0, and take the initial positive block zF=μ0+...+μ_{gF}. A direct chain calculation gives (xF−zF)·zF≥x0−1. Choose gI minimal with x·fgI≤0 and build zI=−fgI+e0+Σ_{i in A}fi in the integral part using the changemaker condition; then (xI−zI)·zI≥x0−2. For z=zI+zF−e0 in L, the decomposition formula gives (x−z)·z≥x0−2. If this is nonnegative, irreducibility forces x=z; if it is −1, then x0=1 and replacing zI by xI gives z′=xI+zF−e0 with (x−z′)·z′≥0, again forcing x=z′. In all cases xF=zF, a consecutive μ-sum, hence irreducible by Lemma 4.16.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every non-integer changemaker lattice L is indecomposable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let S={ν1,...,νt,μ1,...,μm} be a standard basis. By Remark 4.15 every element of S is irreducible, so in any orthogonal direct-sum decomposition L=L1⊕L2 each standard basis element must lie entirely in one summand. Put ν1 in L1. Since ν1·μ1=−1 and μk·μ_{k+1}=−1 for all k, all μ1,...,μm lie in L1. For k>1, if σk is tight then νk has positive pairing with ν1 or with the μ-chain; if σk is not tight and νk=−fk+Σ_{i in A}fi, then with d=min A one has νk·νd=−1. Induction puts every νk in L1. Thus S⊆L1 and L=L1, so no nontrivial orthogonal decomposition exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.20
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a decomposable integer changemaker lattice with changemaker coefficients σ1≤...≤σt. Let 2≤σm≤...≤σt be the stable coefficients, so σ1=...=σ_{m−1}=1 and σm>1. Then σm=m−1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The contrapositive is proved. Assume σm≠m−1. Let S={ν2,...,νt} be a standard basis; by Remark 4.15 its elements are irreducible. In a decomposition L=L1⊕L2, put ν2 in L1. For every k>2, one finds l<k with νk·νl≠0: if σk is tight, νk·ν2≥1; if σk=Σ_{i<k}σi, then k>m and νk·νm≥||νm||^2−2≥1; otherwise νk=−fk+Σ_{i in A}fi with A a proper subset, and either min A or the least omitted index gives a previous νl pairing nontrivially with νk. Induction forces all νk into L1, contradicting a nontrivial decomposition. Hence decomposability implies σm=m−1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.2 graph lattice construction and pairing formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops; multiple edges are allowed through the number e(u,v) of edges between u and v. Let d(v)=e(v,V\{v}). Define Λ(G) first as the free abelian group on V with bilinear form v·v=d(v) and v·w=−e(v,w) for v≠w. Since [V]=Σ_{v in V}v pairs trivially with every element, define the graph lattice Λ(G)=Λ(G)/Z[V]; connectedness makes the induced form positive definite. For x=Σ b_v v and y=Σ c_v v, the product is x·y = (1/2) Σ_{u,v in V} (c_v−c_u)(b_v−b_u)e(v,u). If y=[R]−x, where [R]=Σ_{v in R}v, then ([R]−x)·x equals Σ_{u in R, v outside R} b_{v,u}(1−b_{v,u})e(v,u) − (1/2)Σ_{u,v in R} b_{v,u}^2 e(v,u) − (1/2)Σ_{u,v outside R} b_{v,u}^2 e(v,u), where b_{v,u}=b_v−b_u.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The formula is obtained by expanding v·y=Σ_u(c_v−c_u)e(v,u), multiplying by b_v, and symmetrizing in u and v. Substituting y=[R]−x gives the displayed expression, with terms across the cut R,V\R of the form b(1−b) and terms within either side negative squares. Since b_{v,u} is integral, every summand is nonpositive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.21
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite connected graph and Λ(G) its graph lattice. If x=[R] is the class of a sum of vertices over some subset R⊆V(G), then for every z in Λ(G) one has (x−z)·z ≤ 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Represent z as Σ b_v v. Apply the second formula from Section 4.2 to ([R]−z)·z. Each cross-cut term is b(1−b) for an integer b and hence ≤0; each same-side term is a negative square. Therefore the entire sum is ≤0. Equality is very restrictive: across the cut coefficient differences must be 0 or 1, and within each side coefficient differences must vanish along edges.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.22
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite connected graph and Λ(G) its graph lattice. A nonzero vector x in Λ(G) is irreducible if and only if x=[R] for some subset R⊆V(G) such that the induced subgraphs on R and on V(G)\R are both connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward direction, write x=Σ a_v v with min a_v=0 and let a=max a_v≥1. Let R be the set of vertices where a_v=a. A direct calculation gives (x−[R])·[R]≥0. Since x is irreducible, x=[R]. If R were disconnected, say R=S∪T with e(S,T)=0, then [R]=[S]+[T] and [S]·[T]=0 would be a reducibility decomposition; similarly V\R must be connected because −x=[V\R] is also irreducible. Conversely, suppose x=[R] and both sides are connected. If [R]=y+([R]−y) with y·([R]−y)≥0, Lemma 4.21 forces equality. The equality conditions in the formula imply that the coefficients of y are constant on R and constant on V\R, and across the cut the constants differ by either 0 or 1. Thus y is either 0 or [R] in the quotient, proving irreducibility.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite connected graph G, the following are equivalent: (i) G is 2-connected, meaning deleting any single vertex leaves a connected graph; (ii) every vertex v in V(G), viewed as an element of Λ(G), is irreducible; (iii) the graph lattice Λ(G) is indecomposable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of (i) and (ii) is immediate from Lemma 4.22 applied to R={v}: the singleton is connected, and the complement is connected exactly when v is not a cut vertex. To prove (ii) implies (iii), suppose Λ(G)=L1⊕L2. Each irreducible vertex must lie entirely in one summand, producing a partition V=R1∪R2. Since [R1]+[R2]=[V]=0 and the summands are orthogonal, positive-definiteness forces [R1]=[R2]=0, so the partition is trivial and the decomposition is trivial. For (iii) implies (i), if deleting a vertex v disconnects G into nonempty components G1 and G2, then the sublattices spanned by the vertices of G1 and G2 are nonzero orthogonal summands whose direct sum is Λ(G), so Λ(G) is decomposable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.24
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite connected graph and Λ(G) its graph lattice. The lattice Λ(G) contains a vector of norm one if and only if G contains a cut-edge.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If z has norm one, then z is irreducible, so by Lemma 4.22 z=[G1] for a connected induced subgraph whose complement is connected. Its norm is e(G1,G\G1), so norm one means there is exactly one edge between G1 and the complement; that edge is a cut-edge. Conversely, if e is a cut-edge separating G into connected sides G1 and G\G1, then [G1] has norm e(G1,G\G1)=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite connected graph with no cut-edges. Suppose v is an irreducible vertex of Λ(G) and v=x+y for some x,y in Λ(G) with x·y=−1. Then there is a cut-edge e in G\{v}. If R and S are the vertex sets of the two components of (G\{v})\{e}, then {x,y}={[R]+v,[S]+v}. Furthermore, there are unique vertices u1,u2 distinct from v with x·u1=1 and y·u2=1, and every vertex w outside {v,u1,u2} satisfies w·x≤0 and w·y≤0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First show x and y are irreducible. If x=z+w with z·w≥0, then since x·y=−1 at least one of z·y or w·y is nonnegative; combining with v=x+y and irreducibility of v forces either a trivial summand or a vector of norm ≤1. Lemma 4.24 and the no-cut-edge hypothesis rule out norm one, so the decomposition is trivial. Thus x and y are irreducible. By Lemma 4.22, x=[R′] and y=[S′] for connected cuts. The equation v=[R′]+[S′] in the quotient forces R′∪S′=V and R′∩S′={v}; write R=R′\{v} and S=S′\{v}. Then x·y=[R]·[S]=−e(R,S)=−1, so there is a unique edge between R and S, and it is a cut-edge in G\{v}. Its endpoints are the unique u1 and u2 with the stated positive pairings; all other vertex pairings are nonpositive by the cut description.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let D be a reduced alternating diagram whose Goeritz graph lattice ΛD is identified with a half-integer changemaker lattice L, and let VD be the set of graph vertices viewed inside L. Let x=Σ_{v in R}v for some subset R⊆VD. Suppose there is a vector z=−fg+Σ_{f in A}f in L, where A is a subset of the ambient coordinate basis {e0,e1,f1,...,f_{g−1}}, such that x·f>0 for every f in A and x·fg≤0. Then (x−z)·z is either −1 or 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Lemma 4.21 to the sum of vertices x. Since z has the displayed coordinate form, direct expansion gives (x−z)·z=−x·fg−1+Σ_{f in A}(x·f−1). The hypotheses make all terms except the initial −1 nonnegative, so the expression is at least −1. Lemma 4.21 gives it is at most 0. Hence it is −1 or 0. The proof also records an important hidden consequence: almost all coordinates x·f for f in A must be exactly 1 whenever equality is tight.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Lemma 6.6, if x=Σ_{v in R}v is a sum of vertices in the half-integer changemaker lattice model, then |x·e0|≤1 and |x·f1|≤2. If x is irreducible and x·e0≠0, then |x·f1|≤1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For |x·e0|, replace x by −x if needed and choose g minimal with x·fg≤0. Use the changemaker condition to write σg−1 as a subset sum of earlier σi, producing z=−fg+e0+e1+Σ fi in L. Lemma 4.21 applied to (x−z)·z≤0 yields 0≥−1+2x·e0−2, hence x·e0≤1. For |x·f1|, choose g>1 minimal with x·fg≤0 and a similar vector z=−fg+f1+Σ fi; if z·f1=ε in {1,2}, the inequality gives 0≥−1+ε(x·f1−ε), forcing x·f1≤2. If x is irreducible and x·e0=1, the same test-vector construction plus irreducibility improves the estimate: either x equals the test vector or the cross-term must be ≤−1, forcing x·f1≤1; applying the argument to −x handles the opposite sign.

proof_techniques.name: Reduced Laplacian edge-sum formula
proof_techniques.description: For a connected graph G, the paper works with the reduced graph lattice Λ(G), i.e. the graph Laplacian pairing modulo the all-ones vector [V]. The key identity expresses x·y as a half-sum over edges of products of coefficient differences. When y=[R]−x, this becomes a sum of integer terms b(1−b) across the cut and negative squares inside the two sides, so it is always ≤0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a weighted tree lattice can be compared to or embedded into a reduced graph Laplacian lattice, Lemma 4.21 gives a powerful universal obstruction to decompositions of sums of vertices. Even without an exact graph-lattice model, the edge-difference expansion suggests trying to rewrite (u−a)·a for a vertex u in terms of coefficient differences plus correction terms from the weights.

proof_techniques.name: Characterizing irreducibles by connected cuts
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.22 proves that in a graph lattice the irreducible vectors are precisely cut-sums [R] where both R and its complement induce connected subgraphs. The proof has two complementary parts: an irreducible vector is forced to be a top-level set of its coefficient function, and connectedness of both sides is exactly what prevents a zero-cross-term decomposition of [R].
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a tree-like lattice, one can try to mimic the top-level-set argument: assume a vertex decomposes, examine maximal coefficient regions of one summand, and use connectedness of the underlying tree to force a contradiction or isolate a special reducible structure.

proof_techniques.name: Vertex irreducibility via 2-connectedness and indecomposability
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.23 links three notions in graph lattices: 2-connectedness of the graph, irreducibility of every vertex, and indecomposability of the lattice. This turns algebraic irreducibility into graph connectivity: cut vertices give orthogonal decompositions, while irreducible vertices cannot split across an orthogonal direct sum.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original object is a tree, hence maximally full of cut vertices, so the exact 2-connected criterion will not apply directly. However, the proof explains how reducibility of vertices is tied to decompositions supported on components after deleting vertices; this is a natural structure to exploit in any weighted-tree induction.

proof_techniques.name: Coordinate splitting in diagonal embeddings
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4.11 is a general diagonal-lattice tool: if a vector has only 0, 1, and −1 coordinates, then any decomposition with nonnegative cross-term must split those coordinates with no overlap and no extra coordinates. This converts an inequality x·y≥0 into a rigid combinatorial statement about supports.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree lattice is embedded into a standard diagonal lattice or if vertex candidates can be represented with small coordinates, Proposition 4.11 can show that possible reducibility decompositions are forced to be support splittings, often easy to rule out.

proof_techniques.name: Nonnegative-coordinate obstruction
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.12 shows that in a changemaker lattice there is no nonzero vector whose coordinates are all nonnegative in the ambient orthonormal basis. The proof uses orthogonality to w0, whose coefficients are positive, and in the non-integer case uses sums w0+...+wk to eliminate the remaining chain coordinates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A similar obstruction may be available if one can find a positive vector orthogonal to or controlling the lattice. For positive definite tree forms, Perron-Frobenius-type positive vectors or kernel vectors of related Laplacians might play an analogous role.

proof_techniques.name: Standard basis plus irreducible generator connectivity
proof_techniques.description: For changemaker lattices, the paper constructs explicit standard bases whose elements are irreducible. Indecomposability is then proved by showing the basis elements form a connected graph under nonzero pairings: once one basis element lies in a summand, all adjacent basis elements must lie in the same summand.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a weighted tree lattice, the original vertex basis already has a pairing graph equal to the tree. If one can prove irreducibility for one strategically chosen vertex or for certain auxiliary combinations, nonzero-pairing propagation may force global constraints on any assumed decomposition.

proof_techniques.name: Minimal-index test vector from changemaker subset sums
proof_techniques.description: Several proofs in Chapters 6 and 7 choose the first coordinate where a sum of vertices becomes nonpositive, then use the changemaker condition to build a vector z in the lattice with one negative coordinate and controlled positive coordinates. Lemma 4.21 then bounds (x−z)·z, producing sharp coordinate inequalities such as |x·e0|≤1 or |x·f1|≤2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The transferable idea is to choose a minimal place where coefficients change sign and build a test vector adapted to that place. In a tree lattice, analogous test vectors might be supported on paths or branches emanating from the unique bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Cut-edge and norm-one detection
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.24 identifies norm-one vectors in graph lattices with cut-edges of the graph. Lemma 4.25 refines this: if an irreducible vertex v decomposes as x+y with x·y=−1 and the graph has no cut-edges, then x and y come from the two sides of a unique cut-edge in G with v removed.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original lattice is not a reduced graph lattice, reducible decompositions of a vertex in a tree are likely controlled by branches after deleting that vertex. Lemma 4.25 gives a model for translating an algebraic decomposition into a precise separating-edge statement.

proof_techniques.name: Graph-lattice realization by adjoining a root
proof_techniques.description: The paper's graph lattice Λ(G) is the reduced Laplacian: after choosing one vertex as a root and deleting its row and column, the remaining basis has diagonal entries equal to graph degrees and off-diagonal entries equal to minus edge multiplicities among non-root vertices. This is implicit in the construction Λ(G)=free(V)/Z[V] and is used throughout the Goeritz-lattice discussion.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If all or most weights of the tree satisfy w(u)≥d_T(u), one can try to add a root vertex and w(u)−d_T(u) extra root-edges to realize the form as a graph lattice or a principal sublattice. The unique bad vertex is exactly the obstruction to this naive realization, so isolating that obstruction may be a useful proof route.

other_useful_info: The graph-lattice material in Section 4.2 is explicitly based on Greene's work, cited as Greene [Gre13, Section 3.2]. The paper's graph lattices are reduced Laplacian lattices of connected graphs and are positive definite only after quotienting by the all-vertices vector [V]; the original weighted-tree lattice is not automatically of this form, so any direct use requires an augmentation, quotient, or comparison argument. The paper repeatedly uses the principle that a sum of graph vertices behaves like a convex or extremal object with respect to decompositions, via Lemma 4.21. In changemaker lattices, the stable-coefficient and standard-basis machinery is probably less directly relevant to a bare weighted-tree problem, but the irreducibility proofs in Lemmas 4.13 and 4.14 are useful templates for ruling out decompositions by coordinate rigidity.
why_relevant: Contains a consolidated treatment of graph lattices, p/q-changemaker lattices, and the exact irreducibility definition used in the problem. It is especially useful as a reference for the lattice-theoretic lemmas spread across Greene, Gibbons, and McCoy.

title: The Prism Manifold Realization Problem
overall_summary: The paper classifies prism manifolds P(p,q) with q < 0 that can arise by positive integral surgery on knots in S^3, using Donaldson theory, Heegaard Floer correction terms, Greene changemaker lattices, and a detailed classification of D-type changemaker lattices. The part most relevant to the original problem is Section 3, where the authors study positive definite D-type tree lattices with diagonal weights a_i and off-diagonal pairings -1 along edges, prove that every vertex basis element is irreducible, prove that interval sums are irreducible, and classify all irreducible elements up to signs and explicit lattice reflections. The key transferable ideas are: reduce irreducibility checks to the all-weights-equal-2 root lattice by monotonicity in diagonal weights; use support connectedness and sign separation for irreducible vectors in lattices with nonpositive off-diagonal entries; and exploit interval decompositions along the tree. Later sections use these irreducibility results to control changemaker standard bases, but those parts are less directly tied to the original problem than the D-type lattice arguments.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X(p,q) be the plumbed four-manifold whose plumbing graph is D-shaped: there are two vertices x_* and x_** of weight -2, both adjacent to a vertex x_0 of weight -a_0, and x_0 is the first vertex of a linear chain x_0-x_1-...-x_n with weights -a_0,-a_1,...,-a_n, where the integers a_i satisfy a_i >= 2. Then the intersection form Q_X on H_2(X(p,q)) is negative definite. Equivalently, the lattice with basis x_*, x_**, x_0,...,x_n, diagonal pairings 2,2,a_0,...,a_n, and off-diagonal pairings -1 along the same D-shaped graph is positive definite.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof considers the positive form -Q_X. For a fixed vector v, the value -Q_X(v,v) is an increasing function of each diagonal parameter a_i, so it suffices to prove positive definiteness in the minimal case where every a_i is 2. In that case, an induction on b_2(X)=n+3 gives det(-Q_X)=4. The induction also gives positivity of all principal minors. By Sylvester's criterion, the all-2 form is positive definite; increasing any of the a_i preserves positive definiteness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ∆(p,q) be the D-type lattice with vertex basis x_*, x_**, x_0,...,x_n, diagonal norms |x_*|=|x_**|=2 and |x_i|=a_i >= 2 for 0 <= i <= n, and pairings -1 exactly when two basis vertices are adjacent in the D-shaped graph, namely x_*--x_0, x_**--x_0, and x_i--x_{i+1}. In this positive definite lattice, every vertex basis element x_i, including x_* and x_**, is irreducible: there do not exist nonzero lattice elements y,z with x_i=y+z and ⟨y,z⟩ >= 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a vertex x_i and suppose w is a lattice element distinct from 0 and x_i. Write w = w_* x_* + w_** x_** + sum_{j=0}^n w_j x_j. The relevant pairing for a decomposition x_i = w + (x_i-w) is ⟨w,x_i-w⟩. As a function of the diagonal weights, this has the form a_i w_i(1-w_i) - sum_{j != i} a_j w_j^2 plus terms independent of the a_j, with a_*=a_**=2. Since the coefficients w_i(1-w_i) and -w_j^2 are nonpositive for integer w_j, increasing diagonal weights can only decrease this pairing. Thus it is enough to prove irreducibility when all diagonal weights are 2. In that case ∆(p,q) is isomorphic to the standard D_{n+3} root lattice, the sublattice of Z^{n+3}=⟨e_{-2},e_{-1},e_0,...,e_n⟩ consisting of vectors whose coordinate sum is even, via x_* ↦ e_{-2}+e_{-1}, x_** ↦ -e_{-2}+e_{-1}, and x_j ↦ -e_{j-1}+e_j. Each vertex maps to a root of the form ±e_r ± e_s. Such a norm-2 root cannot be written as a sum of two nonzero D-lattice vectors with nonnegative pairing: in the paper's description, the only possible summands would be the coordinate vectors ±e_r and ±e_s, which are not in the D-lattice. Therefore the pairing ⟨w,x_i-w⟩ is always negative for nontrivial decompositions, proving irreducibility.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The D-type lattice ∆(p,q) is indecomposable as an orthogonal direct sum of nontrivial lattices. That is, there do not exist nonzero positive definite lattices L_1 and L_2 with ∆(p,q) isomorphic to L_1 ⊕ L_2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume ∆(p,q) = L_1 ⊕ L_2. Decompose each vertex basis element x_i into its L_1 and L_2 components. Because the two components pair to zero, any vertex with nonzero components in both summands would be a reducible element, contradicting Proposition 3.2. Hence every x_i lies entirely in one summand. But the pairing graph of the vertex basis is connected, and adjacent vertices have nonzero pairing, so adjacent vertices must lie in the same summand. Therefore all vertex basis elements lie in the same summand, and the other summand is zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the D-type lattice ∆(p,q), for any subset A of the vertex basis indices {*, **, 0,...,n}, write [A] = sum_{i in A} x_i. Call [A] an interval if the induced pairing graph on A is connected and A does not contain both * and **. Every interval [A] is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof repeats the reduction used in Proposition 3.2. For a decomposition [A]=w+([A]-w), each coefficient of [A] in the vertex basis is 0 or 1. Consequently, the coefficient of a diagonal parameter a_j in ⟨w,[A]-w⟩ is w_j(c_j-w_j), where c_j is 0 or 1, and this is nonpositive for integral w_j. Thus increasing the a_j cannot create a nonnegative pairing decomposition, so it suffices to prove the claim when all a_j are 2. Under the same embedding into the D_{n+3} root lattice, a connected interval not containing both branch vertices maps to a vector ±e_r ± e_s of norm 2. As in Proposition 3.2, such a root is irreducible in the D-lattice. Hence the original interval is irreducible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7 and Lemma 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let m be the smallest index with a_m >= 3 in a D-type lattice ∆(p,q). For each 0 <= j <= m, define y_j = x_* + x_** + 2 sum_{0 <= i < j} x_i, and define τ_j: ∆(p,q) -> ∆(p,q) by τ_j(x_*)=x_**, τ_j(x_**)=x_*, τ_j(x_i)=x_i for 0 <= i < j, τ_j(x_j)= -x_* - x_** - 2x_0 - ... - 2x_{j-1} - x_j, and τ_j(x_i)= -x_i for i > j. Then τ_j is an isometric involution: ⟨τ_j(v),τ_j(w)⟩=⟨v,w⟩ and τ_j^2(v)=v for all v,w. Moreover, for all v,w in ∆(p,q), one has the parity congruence ⟨v,w⟩ ≡ ⟨τ_j(v),w⟩ mod 2. In particular, for every x in ∆(p,q), ⟨x,x_*⟩ ≡ ⟨x,x_**⟩ mod 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The map τ_j is the reflection in the subspace spanned by y_0,...,y_j, which immediately gives that it preserves the bilinear form and has square the identity. The parity statement is verified on the vertex basis: one checks directly from the displayed formula for τ_j and the D-type pairings that replacing a basis vector by its τ_j-image changes all pairings by even integers. The final congruence follows by taking j=0, since τ_0 exchanges x_* and x_**.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x be any irreducible element of a D-type lattice ∆(p,q). Then either x or -x is an interval, or there exists an index j such that τ_j(x) or -τ_j(x) is an interval, where τ_j is one of the reflections from Definition 3.6. Thus, up to sign and the explicit reflections τ_j, every irreducible element is an interval sum of vertex basis elements.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write x = sum c_i x_i in the vertex basis. After replacing x by -x if necessary, assume c_0 >= 0 and at least one coefficient is positive. First remove sign changes: let A be the set of indices with c_i < 0 and set y = sum_{i in A} c_i x_i. Because all off-diagonal pairings between opposite-sign coefficients contribute nonnegatively, ⟨y,x-y⟩ is nonnegative. Irreducibility forces y=0, so all c_i >= 0. Next, the support B={i : c_i != 0} must have connected pairing graph; otherwise x splits as an orthogonal sum over connected components, again contradicting irreducibility. If every nonzero coefficient is 1, then x=[B]. Since B is connected, either [B] is already an interval, or B contains both branch vertices and has the form {*,**,0,...,j}; in that exceptional case -τ_0(x)=x_0+...+x_j is an interval. It remains to handle a coefficient larger than 1. Let z=[B]. If 0 is not in B, choose the first and last indices j_0,j_1 in the linear chain part of B; the paper computes ⟨z,x-z⟩ = sum_{j_0 <= i <= j_1} (a_i-2)(c_i-1) + c_{j_0}+c_{j_1}-2, which is nonnegative, contradiction. Hence 0 lies in B, and connectedness gives c_i >= 1 for all 0 <= i <= max B. Let j be the largest index with c_j > 1. If there were a largest k<j with c_k<c_j, then for w=x_{k+1}+...+x_j the paper computes ⟨w,x-w⟩ = sum_{k+1 <= i <= j} (a_i-2)(c_i-1) + (c_{k+1}-c_k) + (c_j-c_{j+1}) - 2, again nonnegative, contradiction; hence all earlier c_i are at least c_j. Finally compare the two possible initial intervals w_0=x_0+...+x_j and w_1=x_*+x_**+w_0. The paper computes ⟨w_0,x-w_0⟩ = sum_{0 <= i <= j}(a_i-2)(c_i-1) - (c_*+c_**-c_0) + (c_j-2) - c_{j+1} and ⟨w_1,x-w_1⟩ = the same first term + (c_*+c_**-c_0) + (c_j-2) - c_{j+1}. For both pairings to be negative, the nonnegative terms must vanish: c_j=2, c_0=c_*+c_**, all a_i=2 for 0 <= i <= j, and c_{j+1}=1. Thus j is before the first index m with a_m>=3. Applying -τ_{j+1} to x produces a new irreducible vector whose coefficients before j vanish, whose coefficient at j is zero, and whose remaining nonzero coefficients are 0 or 1; by the earlier case, it is an interval. This proves the classification.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [A] be an interval in a D-type lattice ∆(p,q). If A contains at least two indices i and k in the linear chain with a_i >= 3 and a_k >= 3, then [A] is breakable. Here an element ℓ is breakable if ℓ = u+v with |u| >= 3, |v| >= 3, and ⟨u,v⟩ = -1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose an index J with i <= J < k. Split the interval A into T_0={j in A : j <= J} and T_1={j in A : J < j}. Since A is connected and contains both i and k, both T_0 and T_1 are nonempty connected subintervals; the sums [T_0] and [T_1] have norms at least 3 because they contain vertices of diagonal weight at least 3. The two subintervals meet across exactly one edge of the D-shaped graph, so ⟨[T_0],[T_1]⟩=-1. Therefore [A]=[T_0]+[T_1] is a breakable decomposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For D-type lattices arising from prism-manifold data, if ∆(p,q) is isomorphic as a lattice to ∆(p',q'), then p=p' and q=q'. Equivalently, the isomorphism type of the D-type lattice determines the ordered vertex-weight sequence |x_0|,|x_1|,...,|x_n| and hence the continued fraction data determining p and q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reconstructs the weighted D-shaped tree intrinsically from the lattice. Let R be the sublattice generated by all norm-2 vectors. Using Proposition 3.9, R is generated by the vertex basis elements of weight 2. Let V_0 be the set of irreducible, unbreakable elements of norm at least 3, and identify v,u in V_0 if v-u or v+u lies in R. By Proposition 3.9 and Lemma 3.10, each such equivalence class corresponds to exactly one vertex of the original D-shaped tree with weight at least 3; applying the reflections τ_j changes high-weight coefficients only by signs, and adding elements of R changes only weight-2 directions. The norm of a representative gives the weight of the corresponding high-weight vertex. Next let W be the set of indecomposable summands of R. Each w in W corresponds to a connected block of weight-2 vertices in the tree; as a root lattice it is of type A_N, except possibly one block of type D_N when the block contains the branch x_*,x_**,x_0,x_1. Form a bipartite graph B with vertex set V union W, joining a high-weight class v to a norm-2 block w if some representative of v pairs nontrivially with some vector in w. This records which high-weight vertices attach to which weight-2 blocks. The proof also defines a special edge: w is A_3 and a representative of v pairs as -1 with exactly four norm-2 vectors in w; this occurs precisely for the configuration a_0=2, a_1>2, with w generated by x_*,x_**,x_0 and v corresponding to x_1. From B one reconstructs the full weighted tree: replace each A_N block by a path of N weight-2 vertices, attach high-weight vertices to the ends according to non-special edges, attach special edges to the middle vertex of the A_3 path, replace a D_N block by the corresponding D Dynkin diagram, and add direct edges between high-weight vertices when representatives pair nontrivially and they do not share a common norm-2 block. This recovers the original weighted tree and therefore the continued fraction data, proving p=p' and q=q'.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Section 4 setting, let a D-type lattice also be identified with a changemaker lattice, and for each standard basis vector v_j choose an associated interval [A_j] in the D-type vertex basis so that v_j is either [A_j] or τ([A_j]) for the relevant reflection τ. With the choices made in the paper, one of the two branch vertices * or ** appears in none of the A_j. If A_j and A_k are disjoint, then ⟨[A_j],[A_k]⟩ is either 0 or -1. If A_j and A_k intersect, then ⟨[A_j],[A_k]⟩ is either |[A_j ∩ A_k]| - 1 or |[A_j ∩ A_k]| - 2, where |·| denotes the lattice norm of the interval sum. Moreover, if [A_j] is unbreakable, then |[A_j ∩ A_k]| is either |[A_j]| or 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the fact that, after excluding one branch vertex from all chosen intervals, the relevant intervals can be regarded as intervals in a single linearly ordered chain, with the remaining branch vertex relabeled as an endpoint. If two connected intervals are disjoint, their sums pair as 0 unless they are adjacent, in which case exactly one edge crosses between them and the pairing is -1. If they intersect, the pairing consists of the norm contribution from the common subinterval, minus one boundary edge if they share a common endpoint, or minus two boundary edges if neither endpoint is common; this gives |[A_j ∩ A_k]|-1 or |[A_j ∩ A_k]|-2. Finally, for any interval A one has the norm formula |[A]| = 2 + sum_{i in A}(a_i-2). If [A_j] is unbreakable, Lemma 3.10 implies it contains at most one vertex of weight at least 3, so the norm of its intersection with another interval is either the full norm |[A_j]|, when the unique high-weight vertex is retained, or just 2, when only weight-2 vertices remain.

proof_techniques.name: Diagonal-weight monotonicity reduction
proof_techniques.description: To prove irreducibility of a vertex or interval in a D-type lattice, the paper writes a hypothetical decomposition as x=w+(x-w) and studies the pairing ⟨w,x-w⟩ as a function of the diagonal weights a_i. When the target vector has vertex-basis coefficients 0 or 1, the coefficient of each a_i in this pairing is nonpositive for integer coordinates of w. Therefore, if the pairing is always negative in the minimal-weight case a_i=2, then it remains negative after increasing weights.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem also concerns positive definite tree lattices with diagonal weights and off-diagonal entries -1. For vertices, whose coefficients are 0 or 1, this monotonicity suggests reducing irreducibility questions to a smaller or extremal weighted tree where the lattice may be identifiable with a root lattice.

proof_techniques.name: Root-lattice model for irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: After reducing all D-type diagonal weights to 2, the paper identifies the lattice with the standard D root lattice, embedded in an orthonormal integer lattice as the even-coordinate-sum sublattice. Vertex basis elements and interval sums become roots ±e_i ± e_j of norm 2. Such roots cannot split into two nonzero D-lattice vectors with nonnegative inner product, because the only coordinate-level split would use norm-1 coordinate vectors that are not in the D lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a tree lattice in the original problem can be compared to an A-, D-, or E-type root lattice after lowering or normalizing weights, irreducibility of vertices may follow from minimal-norm root behavior.

proof_techniques.name: Sign separation for irreducible vectors
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 3.9, the authors show that an irreducible vector in a D-type lattice can be assumed, after multiplying by -1, to have all vertex-basis coefficients nonnegative. The key observation is that the sum of the negative-coefficient part pairs nonnegatively with the remaining part, because off-diagonal pairings are nonpositive and opposite signs contribute nonnegatively. Irreducibility rules out such a nontrivial splitting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This argument depends mainly on the tree lattice having off-diagonal entries 0 or -1. It is likely transferable to arbitrary positive definite tree lattices as a first step in analyzing possible reducible decompositions of vertices or other elements.

proof_techniques.name: Connected support criterion
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the pairing graph of the support of a vector. If the support of a nonzero vector splits into two disconnected parts in the pairing graph, then the vector decomposes as an orthogonal sum of two nonzero lattice elements. Such a decomposition has pairing 0, so it contradicts irreducibility.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For any tree lattice, an irreducible element must have connected support. This gives a simple structural constraint on decompositions and may help show that certain vertex decompositions are impossible.

proof_techniques.name: Interval decomposition and breakability
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats connected sums of consecutive vertices, called intervals, as fundamental irreducible objects in D-type lattices. It also shows that an interval containing two vertices of weight at least 3 is breakable: split the interval between those two high-weight vertices, obtaining two subintervals of norm at least 3 that pair exactly -1. This converts geometry of the tree into algebraic decompositions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In an arbitrary weighted tree, paths between high-weight or bad vertices may similarly provide natural decompositions. The original problem asks for an irreducible vertex; interval-splitting arguments may help identify where reducibility can and cannot occur.

proof_techniques.name: Reflection symmetries from chains of weight-2 vertices
proof_techniques.description: The D-type lattice has explicit isometric involutions τ_j built from the initial segment before the first vertex of weight at least 3. These reflections swap the two branch vertices, negate or transform parts of the chain, and preserve pairings. They allow the authors to classify all irreducible vectors up to sign and reflection as interval sums.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original tree contains weight-2 chains or root-lattice subconfigurations, local Weyl-group reflections may normalize a hypothetical reducible or irreducible vector into a simpler interval-like form.

proof_techniques.name: Pairing computations with test subintervals
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Proposition 3.9, the authors choose carefully tailored subintervals w, such as x_{k+1}+...+x_j or x_0+...+x_j, and compute ⟨w,x-w⟩ exactly. Nonnegativity of these expressions would contradict irreducibility, so the formulas force strong constraints on coefficients, such as monotonicity of coefficients, equality c_j=2, and vanishing of certain weight-excess terms a_i-2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original problem, one can test a hypothetical decomposition by pairing with sums over paths or rooted subtrees. Exact formulas may force contradictions using positive definiteness or the unique bad-vertex hypothesis.

proof_techniques.name: Recovering a weighted tree from irreducibles and norm-2 sublattices
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.12 reconstructs the D-type weighted tree from the lattice alone. The proof identifies high-weight vertices as equivalence classes of irreducible unbreakable elements of norm at least 3 modulo the sublattice generated by norm-2 elements, and identifies chains of weight-2 vertices as indecomposable A- or D-type root sublattices. The incidence between these pieces is recovered from nonzero pairings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests that irreducible elements encode the combinatorics of positive definite tree lattices. A proof by contradiction in the original problem might attempt to show that if no vertex were irreducible, the lattice could not reconstruct a positive definite tree with the stated bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Interval abutment calculus
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a simple but powerful calculus for pairings of interval sums: disjoint intervals pair 0 or -1 depending on adjacency, while intersecting intervals pair as the norm of their overlap minus one or two boundary contributions. Combined with the formula |[A]| = 2 + sum_{i in A}(a_i-2), this reduces many lattice inequalities to combinatorics of interval overlap.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For any tree or path subconfiguration in the original problem, analogous overlap formulas can turn reducibility inequalities into graph-theoretic statements about supports.

other_useful_info: The paper uses the term bad vertex for a negative definite plumbing graph vertex with weight m(v) > -d(v); after reversing signs this is exactly the condition w(v) < d(v) appearing in the original problem. It cites Ozsváth-Szabó [18] for the result that a negative definite weighted graph with at most two bad vertices gives a sharp four-manifold, though this topological result is not used in the Section 3 irreducibility proof. The paper also notes that Greene [10] proved an analogous classification for linear lattices: in that setting every irreducible element is an interval or the negative of an interval.
why_relevant: Analyzes D-type plumbing lattices and explicitly proves irreducibility properties of their vertex bases. The classification of irreducible vectors in these tree-like lattices gives model arguments for the present problem.

title: The Prism Manifold Realization Problem II
overall_summary: The paper classifies prism manifolds P(p,q) with q>p that arise by positive integral surgery on knots in S^3, using Donaldson theory, Heegaard Floer correction terms, and Greene's changemaker-lattice machinery. The core lattice-theoretic work compares a C-type plumbing lattice, with its vertex basis, to a changemaker lattice, with its standard basis, by classifying and controlling irreducible and unbreakable elements. The parts most useful for the original problem are Sections 3 and 4: they give interval descriptions of irreducible elements in a positive definite path-like plumbing lattice, formulas for pairings and norms of interval sums, criteria for breakability in terms of high-weight vertices, and graph-theoretic constraints on how irreducible elements can intersect. The paper also repeatedly uses the principle that irreducible basis elements cannot split across an orthogonal direct-sum decomposition, so connectedness of the pairing graph forces lattice indecomposability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C(p,q) be the C-type positive definite lattice with basis x0,x1,...,xn, pairings ⟨x0,x0⟩=4, ⟨xi,xi⟩=ai for i>0, ⟨x0,x1⟩=-2, ⟨xi,xi+1⟩=-1 for i>0, and all other basis pairings zero, where a1≥3 and ai≥2. For a subset I={xa,xa+1,...,xb} of consecutive basis elements, write [I]=xa+xa+1+...+xb and call [I] an interval. If v is an irreducible element of C(p,q), meaning v cannot be written v=x+y with nonzero x,y and ⟨x,y⟩≥0, then v=ε[I] for some ε in {1,-1} and some interval [I].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove this classification internally; it states that, because a1≥3, the result is immediate from Greene's Proposition 3.3 in [Gre13]. In the paper it is used as a black box converting arbitrary irreducible elements of the C-type lattice into signed sums over consecutive vertices of the distinguished path-like basis. This conversion is the gateway for all later combinatorial arguments, because pairings of irreducibles can then be computed by looking at overlaps and boundary edges of intervals.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the C-type lattice C(p,q) with vertex basis x0,...,xn and pairings as in Proposition 3.2, the lattice C(p,q) is indecomposable: it is not isomorphic to a direct orthogonal sum L1⊕L2 of two nonzero lattices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume C(p,q)=L1⊕L2. The paper uses that each vertex basis element xi is irreducible.
