If xi has projections ai in L1 and bi in L2, then xi=ai+bi and ⟨ai,bi⟩=0; irreducibility forces one projection to vanish, so xi lies entirely in one summand. But adjacent vertex basis elements have nonzero pairing, namely ⟨x0,x1⟩=-2 and ⟨xi,xi+1⟩=-1 for i>0, so two adjacent xi cannot lie in different orthogonal summands. The pairing graph on the vertex basis is connected, hence all xi lie in the same summand and the other summand is trivial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type lattice C(p,q), for every lattice element v, the pairing ⟨x0,v⟩ is an even integer. Consequently the reflection rx0 defined by rx0(v)=v−2⟨x0,v⟩/⟨x0,x0⟩ x0 is an involution of the integral lattice C(p,q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write v=c0x0+c1x1+...+cnxn. Since x0 pairs only with x0 and x1, and those pairings are ⟨x0,x0⟩=4 and ⟨x0,x1⟩=-2, one has ⟨x0,v⟩=4c0−2c1, which is even. Therefore rx0(v)=v−(⟨x0,v⟩/2)x0 has integral coefficients. The usual reflection identity in a nonisotropic vector gives rx0(rx0(v))=v and preserves the form.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type lattice C(p,q), call a vertex xi high weight if i>0 and |xi|=⟨xi,xi⟩=ai>2. An element ℓ is breakable if ℓ=x+y with |x|≥3, |y|≥3, and ⟨x,y⟩=-1; otherwise it is unbreakable. For any interval [I] and sign ε in {1,-1}, the signed interval ε[I] is unbreakable if and only if [I] contains at most one high-weight vertex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The case I={x0} is immediate. If [I] does not contain x0, the statement is exactly the analogous result for linear lattices, cited as Greene's Corollary 3.5(4) in [Gre13]. If [I] contains x0, Lemma 3.5 gives the integral reflection rx0 across x0⊥. This reflection exchanges intervals with left endpoint 0 and intervals with left endpoint 1, preserves norms and pairings, and therefore preserves breakability. Thus the case of intervals containing x0 is reduced to intervals not containing x0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [I] and [J] be intervals in the C-type lattice C(p,q). Let C be the graph with vertices x0,...,xn, two edges between x0 and x1, and one edge between xi and xi+1 for 0<i<n. An edge of C is called dangling for [I] and [J] if one endpoint lies in I, the other endpoint lies in J, and at least one endpoint is not in I∩J. Let δ([I],[J]) be the number of dangling edges. Then ⟨[I],[J]⟩=|[I∩J]|−δ([I],[J]), where |[I∩J]| means the norm of the sum of the common vertices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Expand ⟨[I],[J]⟩ as the double sum of ⟨xi,xj⟩ over xi in I and xj in J. Terms with nonadjacent distinct vertices vanish. The remaining terms either have both xi and xj in the overlap I∩J, in which case they are exactly the terms appearing in the norm |[I∩J]|, or they come from an adjacency edge connecting I to J with at least one endpoint outside the overlap. Each such boundary adjacency contributes -1, with the double edge between x0 and x1 counted twice, and these are exactly the dangling edges subtracted by δ([I],[J]).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let [I] be an interval in the C-type lattice C(p,q), and assume I is not the singleton {x0}. Then |[I]|=2+Σxi in I, xi≠x0 (|xi|−2). Equivalently, the norm of a nontrivial interval is obtained by starting with 2 and adding the excess ai−2 over all non-x0 vertices in the interval.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this without proof. Directly, if I={xa,...,xb} avoids x0, then |[I]| is the sum of the diagonal weights over I minus twice the number of ordinary edges inside the interval, so |[I]|=Σai−2(b−a)=2+Σ(ai−2). If I contains x0 and at least x1, then the diagonal term 4 from x0 is cancelled by twice the pairing -2 between x0 and x1, while all subsequent internal ordinary edges contribute -2; the same formula remains. This compact formula is used repeatedly to translate the number and size of high-weight vertices in an interval into its norm.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.11 and Remark 3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any two intervals [I] and [J] in the C-type lattice C(p,q), the dangling-edge count δ([I],[J]) is one of 0,1,2,3. If δ([I],[J])=3, then ⟨x0,[I]⟩=−⟨x0,[J]⟩=±2. More precisely, define two intervals to be distant if they are separated by at least one vertex, to share a common end if they have the same left endpoint or the same right endpoint, to be consecutive if the right endpoint of one is immediately before the left endpoint of the other, to abut if they are consecutive or share a common end, and to satisfy [I] ⋔ [J] if they overlap nontrivially but do not share a common end. If ⟨[I],x0⟩=⟨[J],x0⟩ or one of these two pairings is zero, then δ is 0 for distant intervals, 1 for abutting intervals, and 2 for [I] ⋔ [J]. If ⟨[I],x0⟩ and ⟨[J],x0⟩ are nonzero and unequal, then δ is 2 when the intervals abut and 3 when [I] ⋔ [J]; in this latter unequal-nonzero case the intervals are never distant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The bound δ≤3 is a combinatorial consequence of the path with one doubled edge: ordinary intervals have at most two boundary adjacencies, and the only way to gain an extra dangling edge is through the doubled x0-x1 edge. The special case δ=3 can only occur when the intervals meet the doubled edge on opposite sides in a way that gives nonzero and opposite pairings with x0. Remark 3.13 refines this by enumerating the possible relative positions of two intervals and whether the doubled edge contributes once or twice to the dangling count.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type lattice C(p,q), if an interval [A] contains at least two high-weight vertices, meaning at least two vertices xi with i>0 and |xi|>2, then [A] is breakable: it can be written [A]=x+y with |x|≥3, |y|≥3, and ⟨x,y⟩=-1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as Lemma 3.10 of [BHM+16] and gives no proof. In combination with Proposition 3.7, it supplies the practical criterion that intervals with two or more high-weight vertices are exactly the breakable ones among signed intervals. The intended decomposition cuts the interval between high-weight vertices so that the two pieces have norms at least 3 and meet across a single edge, giving pairing -1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If two C-type lattices C(p,q) and C(p′,q′), with q>p and q′>p′ in the paper's setting, are isomorphic as lattices, then p=p′ and q=q′. Equivalently, the abstract lattice structure determines the ordered continued-fraction weight sequence (|x1|,|x2|,...,|xn|), and hence determines the prism-manifold parameters p and q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reconstructs the vertex weights from irreducible and unbreakable elements. First, except in the special case (p,q)=(2,3), the element ±x0 is characterized intrinsically as the unique signed unbreakable irreducible element y of norm 4 such that ⟨y,v⟩ is even for every lattice element v. Indeed, any other norm-4 unbreakable interval with all even pairings would have to avoid odd pairings with neighboring vertices, forcing it to run to an end of the chain; unbreakability then forces only x1 to be high weight, and a final endpoint-pairing check leaves only the exceptional n=1, |x1|=4 case. Next let R be the sublattice generated by x0 and all norm-2 vectors. Since the lattice has no norm-1 vector, norm-2 vectors are irreducible; by the interval norm formula, R is generated by x0 and exactly those vertex basis elements xi with |xi|=2. Let V0 be the set of unbreakable irreducibles outside ±x0 with norm at least 3, and quotient V0 by the relation v∼u if v−u or v+u lies in R. Each equivalence class corresponds to one high-weight vertex, and the norm of any representative gives that vertex's weight. Let W be the set of indecomposable components of R; these correspond to x0 and to maximal runs of 2's in the weight sequence. Build a bipartite graph B on V∪W, connecting a high-weight class to a run component when representatives pair by -1, and connecting to the x0 component when the representative pairs with x0 by -2. This graph is a path whose distinguished end is the component containing x0. Traversing the path from that end recovers, in order, the runs of 2's and the high weights, hence the sequence (|x1|,...,|xn|). The defining continued fraction then recovers p and q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.3, 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ=(σ0,...,σn+1) be a changemaker vector in Z^{n+2}, let e0,...,en+1 be the standard orthonormal basis, and let S={v1,...,vn+1} be Greene's standard basis of the changemaker lattice (σ)⊥. In this basis, vj is tight if σj=1+σ0+...+σj−1 and vj=2e0+e1+...+ej−1−ej; otherwise vj=Σi in A ei−ej for a subset A of {0,...,j−1} representing σj and chosen lexicographically maximally, and vj is just right or gappy according to whether A has a gap. The paper records the following facts from Greene and [BHM+16]: if |vk+1|=2 then k is not a gappy index for any vj; every standard basis element vj is irreducible in (σ)⊥; if vj is breakable then vj is tight; if vt is tight and vj=et+ej−1−ej with j>t, then vt+vj is irreducible; if vt is tight and vt+1=e0+e1+...+et−et+1, then vt+1−vt is irreducible; and for every vj one has j−1 in supp(vj).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are cited rather than reproved: Lemma 4.3 is Greene's Lemma 3.12(3), Lemma 4.4 is Greene's Lemma 3.13, Lemma 4.5 is Greene's Lemma 3.15, Lemma 4.6 is Greene's Lemma 3.14(2),(3), and Lemma 4.7 is Lemma 4.9 of [BHM+16]. The common mechanism is the lexicographically maximal choice in the standard basis: gaps in support are restricted by norm-2 basis vectors, each vj has a compulsory last preceding support coordinate j−1, and tight vectors are the only standard basis vectors with enough support to break. These facts are later combined with the interval classification in the C-type lattice to compare the changemaker standard basis to the C-type vertex basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In a C-type lattice C(p,q), there is no element v with |v|=2 and ⟨v,x0⟩≠0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 3.4, C(p,q) is indecomposable, so it contains no norm-1 vector: a norm-1 vector in an integral positive definite lattice would split off a rank-one orthogonal summand. If v had norm 2 and were reducible, v=a+b with a,b nonzero and ⟨a,b⟩≥0; positive definiteness would force |a|=|b|=1 and ⟨a,b⟩=0, impossible without norm-1 vectors. Thus v is irreducible. Proposition 3.2 then gives v=±[I] for an interval. Lemma 3.10 implies a norm-2 interval contains only x0 or norm-2 non-x0 vertices. It cannot contain x1 because a1≥3, and it cannot contain x0 unless it is the singleton x0, whose norm is 4. Hence the interval is disjoint from x0 and x1, so its pairing with x0 is zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.9 and Corollary 4.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume a C-type lattice C(p,q) is isomorphic to a changemaker lattice (σ)⊥ inside Z^{n+2}, with σ0=1, and let x0 be the distinguished norm-4 vertex-basis element of C(p,q). Then, after possibly changing the global sign of the isomorphism, there exist indices k1<k2<k3 such that x0 is either e0+ek1+ek2−ek3 or e0−ek1−ek2+ek3. Moreover, the first standard basis vector v1 is 2e0−e1 if k1>1 and is e0−e1 if k1=1; in the sign pattern x0=e0−ek1−ek2+ek3 the first case k1>1 must occur.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A norm-4 vector in the ambient orthonormal lattice is either ±2ek or a signed sum of four distinct unit vectors. The first type cannot lie in (σ)⊥ because all σi are positive once σ0=1. Write x0=δ0ek0+δ1ek1+δ2ek2+δ3ek3 with k0<k1<k2<k3. After a global sign change take δ0=1. If k0>0, then x0 pairs oddly with vk0, contradicting Lemma 3.5, so k0=0. If σki=σkj but δi≠δj, then δieki+δjekj is a norm-2 vector in (σ)⊥ pairing nontrivially with x0, contradicting Lemma 4.8; hence equal σ-coordinates must have equal signs. The equation ⟨x0,σ⟩=0 and the monotonicity of σ then restrict the signs: if δ1=-1, one must have δ2=-1 and δ3=1; if δ1=1, one must have δ3=-1 and δ2=1, since δ2=-1 would force σ0+σk1=σk2+σk3 and hence equality patterns forbidden by the previous observation. Corollary 4.10 follows because v1 is either e0−e1 or 2e0−e1, and Lemma 3.5 plus Lemma 4.8 exclude the wrong parity or norm-2 pairing with x0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.13 and Corollaries 4.14, 4.15, 4.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Continue in the setting where C(p,q)≅(σ)⊥, with standard changemaker basis S={v1,...,vn+1}, and replace vk3 by x0 to form S′=(S−{vk3})∪{x0}. Each vi is irreducible, so in C(p,q) write vi=εi[vi] for a signed interval. If vi and vj are unbreakable standard basis vectors with i,j≠k3, then it cannot happen that [vi] contains x0 while [vj] contains x1 but not x0; in particular δ([vi],[vj])≤2. Consequently, if vi and vj are distinct unbreakable standard basis vectors with i,j≠k3, then |⟨vi,vj⟩|≤1, and equality can occur only when their intervals abut. If vi and vj are unbreakable, have norms at least 3, are not indexed by k3, and are distinct, then their unique high-weight vertices xzi and xzj are distinct. Finally, among unbreakable standard basis vectors vj with j≠k3, at most one has ⟨vj,x0⟩ nonzero.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper first proves Lemma 4.12: for j≠k3, ⟨vj,x0⟩≥0. This is checked from the two possible sign patterns for x0 in Proposition 4.9; in the harder sign pattern, a hypothetical value -2 would allow replacing supp+(vj) by a lexicographically larger subset, contradicting the definition of the standard basis. For Lemma 4.13, suppose [vi] contains x0 and [vj] contains x1 but not x0. Since i,j≠k3, Lemma 4.12 forces both pairings with x0 to be +2, fixing the signs and left endpoints of the intervals. Unbreakability and a1≥3 imply both intervals have the same unique high-weight vertex x1. The interval-pairing formula then gives ⟨vi,vj⟩=-|vi|+δ, so this pairing is nonpositive and very restricted. An explicit support analysis using Proposition 4.9 shows in every possible sign pattern for x0 that the standard-basis supports force ⟨vi,vj⟩≥1, a contradiction. Corollary 4.14 reduces all remaining configurations either to the known linear-lattice case from Greene or to the just-excluded configuration, using the reflection in x0 when needed. Corollary 4.15 follows because if two such unbreakable intervals shared the same high-weight vertex, Lemmas 3.9 and 3.10 would force their pairing to be at least 1; Corollary 4.14 would then force them to abut, but abutting gives δ=1 and the same calculation yields a pairing too large. Corollary 4.16 follows because a nonzero pairing with x0 for an unbreakable interval forces the unique high-weight vertex to be x1, and Corollary 4.15 allows this for at most one vector.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.19, Lemma 4.21, Lemma 4.23, Lemma 4.24, and Corollary 4.25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type/changemaker comparison setting, let T be a set of irreducible elements of C(p,q), each represented by a signed interval. The intersection graph G(T) has vertex set T and an edge when the corresponding intervals abut; the pairing graph Ĝ(T) has an edge when the lattice pairing is nonzero. For T⊂S′, Proposition 4.19 says G(T) is obtained from Ĝ(T) by deleting only some edges incident to breakable vectors; in particular, on the subset of unbreakable elements of S′, the two graphs agree. Lemma 4.21 says G(T) has no claw, i.e. no vertex adjacent to three pairwise nonadjacent vertices. Lemma 4.23 says the graph G of the unbreakable part of S′ has no heavy triple: there do not exist three vertices of norm at least 3, none equal to ±x0, such that each pair is connected by a path disjoint from the third. Lemma 4.24 says that if the elements of T are linearly independent, then every cycle in G(T) induces a complete subgraph. Corollary 4.25 says every cycle in G of the unbreakable part of S′ has length three.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 4.18 first observes that abutting intervals always have nonzero pairing: if one interval is x0 the pairing is ±2, and otherwise Lemma 3.9 gives a nonzero contribution because δ=1 in an abutting configuration. Proposition 4.19 combines this with Corollary 4.14, which says that for unbreakable standard-basis intervals nonzero pairing can only occur through abutment; hence any discrepancy between the pairing and intersection graphs must involve a breakable vector. Lemma 4.21 is cited from Greene's Lemma 4.8 and is a combinatorial property of interval-abutment graphs. Lemma 4.23 uses Corollary 4.15: three unbreakable norm-at-least-3 intervals have distinct high-weight vertices, so order them along the path as xzi<xzj<xzk. Any path in the intersection graph from the left interval to the right interval must pass through an interval containing the middle high-weight vertex, and Corollary 4.15 forces that interval to be the middle chosen vertex itself; hence the path cannot be disjoint from it. Lemma 4.24 is cited as identical to Greene's Lemma 3.8. For Corollary 4.25, Corollary 4.16 prevents x0 from lying on a cycle in the unbreakable graph. A cycle is complete by Lemma 4.24. It cannot contain three vertices of norm greater than 2 by Lemma 4.23. If it contained two norm-2 vertices, the standard-basis support restrictions from Lemma 4.3 imply no third vector can pair nontrivially with both, contradicting completeness. Therefore the only possible cycles are triangles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.26
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the C-type/changemaker setting, let m<N be positive integers with k3 not in the interval [m,N]. Suppose vm is unbreakable and neighbors either x0 or some unbreakable vj with j<m in the intersection graph. Suppose also that for every i with m<i≤N, the standard basis vector vi is unbreakable and min supp(vi)≥m. Then |vi|=2 for every i with m<i≤N.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on i. For i=m+1, Lemma 4.7 gives m in supp(vm+1), and min supp(vm+1)≥m forces vm+1=em−em+1, so its norm is 2. Assume |vi|=2 for m<i<l≤N. Let t=min supp(vl). By Lemmas 4.3 and 4.7, the vector vl is just right relative to the norm-2 string already constructed. If m<t<l−1, then the intersection graph has a claw centered at vt with leaves vl, vt−1, and vt+1. If t=m and vm neighbors x0, then vm is the center of a claw with leaves vl, x0, and vm+1; if t=m and vm neighbors an earlier unbreakable vj, then vm is the center of a claw with leaves vl, vj, and vm+1. Lemma 4.21 forbids all these cases. Thus t=l−1, which forces vl=el−1−el and |vl|=2.

proof_techniques.name: Classify irreducibles as signed interval sums
proof_techniques.description: The paper's C-type lattice is path-like, and Proposition 3.2 allows every irreducible element to be treated as a signed sum of consecutive vertex-basis elements. This replaces arbitrary lattice decompositions with combinatorics of intervals: endpoints, overlaps, containment, consecutive intervals, and boundary edges. Once irreducibles are intervals, norm and pairing computations become finite graph calculations rather than coefficient-by-coefficient algebra.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a general weighted tree, the analogous object to try is a signed sum over a connected subtree. A proof of the original problem might attempt to show that if a vertex is reducible, its decomposing pieces force connected-subtree behavior, and then use the bad-vertex condition w(v)<d(v) to contradict positive definiteness or to isolate an irreducible vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Overlap-minus-dangling-edges formula
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.9 computes the pairing of two interval sums as the norm of their overlap minus the number of dangling edges between them. This packages all diagonal contributions and all adjacency contributions into a single graph-theoretic identity. Lemmas 3.10 and 3.11 then make the formula effective: interval norms are controlled by excess weights |xi|−2, and the dangling-edge count is bounded by the interval geometry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For any weighted tree lattice, a similar formula holds for sums over connected vertex sets: the inner product is the weighted overlap contribution minus boundary adjacencies, with internal edges counted in the norm. This can be used to quantify how a proposed reducible decomposition interacts with the tree's edges and with a vertex whose weight is smaller than its degree.

proof_techniques.name: Use irreducible basis elements to prove indecomposability
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 3.4 uses a general lattice principle: if an irreducible element is projected to an orthogonal direct sum, one projection must vanish, since otherwise the element is decomposed into two nonzero orthogonal summands with nonnegative pairing. Therefore irreducible basis elements must lie wholly in one summand. If the pairing graph of those basis elements is connected, all basis elements lie in the same summand, proving indecomposability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can establish irreducibility for a subset of vertices or for strategically chosen combinations of vertices, connectedness of the tree can force global restrictions. Conversely, assuming all vertices reducible may create a decomposition or projection structure incompatible with the connected tree.

proof_techniques.name: Detect breakability through high-weight vertices
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines a stronger notion than reducibility: an interval is breakable if it splits into two norm-at-least-3 pieces pairing -1. Proposition 3.7 and Lemma 3.14 show that, in the C-type lattice, breakability of signed intervals is exactly controlled by whether the interval contains at least two high-weight vertices, i.e. vertices of weight greater than the path baseline 2. This allows the authors to separate unbreakable intervals, which behave like single high-weight blocks, from breakable intervals, which can be cut at an edge between high-weight vertices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem involves a vertex whose weight is small relative to degree, so an analogous baseline might be degree rather than 2. A useful adaptation would be to measure a connected subtree by the excess Σ(w(x)−d_subtree_baseline(x)) and show that too many high-excess regions force a controlled splitting, leaving some vertex or small subtree irreducible.

proof_techniques.name: Parity and reflection at a distinguished vertex
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.5 exploits the special C-type feature that x0 pairs evenly with every lattice element. This makes the reflection across x0⊥ an integral lattice automorphism. The paper uses this reflection to reduce intervals containing x0 to intervals not containing x0 and to transfer known linear-lattice arguments to the C-type setting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree has a vertex whose pairings impose congruence restrictions, an integral reflection or parity argument may reduce local configurations. Even when no such reflection exists globally, checking pairings modulo small integers can rule out decompositions of low-norm vertices.

proof_techniques.name: Rule out low-norm interactions using absence of norm-one vectors
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.8 shows that a norm-2 element in an indecomposable positive definite integral lattice with no norm-1 vector must be irreducible. In the C-type lattice, this reduces a hypothetical norm-2 vector pairing with x0 to a signed interval; the interval norm formula then forces it away from x0, contradiction. This is a compact way of turning positive definiteness and indecomposability into restrictions on possible pairings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Positive definite tree lattices often have no norm-one vector under natural weight assumptions. If a vertex has small norm or if a reducible decomposition would create norm-one or norm-two pieces, this technique can eliminate the decomposition.

proof_techniques.name: Compare bases via pairing and intersection graphs
proof_techniques.description: In Section 4, the authors compare the changemaker standard basis to the C-type vertex basis by representing standard basis elements as signed intervals. They define an intersection graph using interval abutment and compare it to the pairing graph. For unbreakable elements, these graphs coincide, and the graph is constrained by no-claw, no-heavy-triple, and triangle-only-cycle rules.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of the original problem proceeds by assuming every vertex is reducible and replacing vertices by decomposing elements, graph constraints on how irreducible or minimal pieces can pair may be useful. The no-claw style arguments are especially suggestive for trees, where high-degree branching must be reconciled with interval- or subtree-like irreducible pieces.

proof_techniques.name: Inductive forcing of norm-two strings
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.26 is a template for proving that a long run of standard-basis elements must all have norm 2. The induction uses support constraints, the fact that each standard basis vector contains the immediately preceding coordinate, and graph obstructions: any attempt to start support too early creates a forbidden claw. Thus local adjacency plus unbreakability forces a rigid chain of norm-2 vectors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, a similar induction could show that certain branches forced by reducibility must consist of minimal-weight vertices. A bad vertex with weight below degree may then be incompatible with all incident branches being minimal in the required way.

proof_techniques.name: Recover the weighted graph from intrinsic irreducible data
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.16 reconstructs the ordered C-type weight sequence from the abstract lattice by identifying x0, norm-2 runs, and high-weight vertices using irreducible and unbreakable elements. It then builds a bipartite path whose vertices are high-weight classes and norm-2 components, and traversal of this path recovers the original vertex basis weights.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests a robust strategy: define intrinsic classes of irreducible or minimally reducible elements, then recover enough of the tree's combinatorics to force one original vertex to appear among them. Such reconstruction ideas may be helpful if the original problem is attacked by contradiction from a different basis.

other_useful_info: Section 3's main irreducible-element classification is imported from Greene [Gre13, Proposition 3.3], and its unbreakability criterion uses Greene [Gre13, Corollary 3.5(4)] and [BHM+16, Lemma 3.10]; those cited results may contain broader linear-plumbing arguments useful beyond the C-type case. The introduction explicitly states that the vertex basis elements of a C-type lattice are irreducible, and Corollary 3.4 uses this fact, although the paper does not isolate it as a numbered lemma. The C-type lattice is not an arbitrary tree lattice: it is a path-like positive definite lattice with one doubled edge between x0 and x1, so direct transfer to the original problem likely requires replacing intervals by connected subtrees and replacing the baseline weight 2 by a degree-sensitive quantity.
why_relevant: Continues the changemaker-lattice analysis for C-type plumbing lattices and uses irreducible elements to compare standard and vertex bases. Its methods are relevant for proving that a weighted-tree basis must contain irreducible elements.

title: The Prism Manifold Realization Problem III
overall_summary: The paper completes the classification of prism manifolds P(p,q) with 0 < q < p that arise by positive integral surgery on knots in S^3, using Heegaard Floer correction terms, Donaldson diagonalization, and changemaker lattice embeddings. Its core technical work is a lattice-theoretic analysis of positive definite linear lattices embedded as orthogonal complements of changemaker vectors in a standard Euclidean lattice. The portions most relevant to the original problem are the reducible/irreducible toolkit in Section 3: irreducible elements in linear lattices are signed intervals, standard changemaker basis vectors are irreducible, and a key coordinate-support lemma forces any nonnegative-pairing decomposition of a ±1-coordinate vector to split its support. The paper also uses interval intersection graphs, high-weight vertices, and forbidden claw/heavy-triple configurations to turn plumbing-graph combinatorics into irreducibility constraints.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p and q be relatively prime positive integers and let Λ(q,-p) be the positive definite linear lattice with vertex basis x_0,...,x_n and pairings ⟨x_i,x_i⟩ = a_i, ⟨x_i,x_j⟩ = -1 when |i-j| = 1, and ⟨x_i,x_j⟩ = 0 when |i-j| > 1, where p/q = [a_{-1},a_0,...,a_n]_- is a Hirzebruch-Jung continued fraction with a_i ≥ 2 for i ≥ 0. An element ℓ of a lattice is reducible if ℓ = x+y for nonzero lattice elements x,y with ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0, and irreducible otherwise. If v ∈ Λ(q,-p) is irreducible, then v = ε(x_i+x_{i+1}+...+x_j) for some 0 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ n and ε ∈ {1,-1}. In the paper's terminology, irreducible elements in a linear lattice are signed intervals.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this directly from Greene, Proposition 3.3, and does not reproduce the proof. The result is used as a black box to replace any irreducible vector in a linear lattice by a signed contiguous interval in the vertex basis, thereby making its interactions with other irreducibles purely combinatorial: one can discuss left and right endpoints, containment, abutment, consecutive intervals, and overlaps.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same linear lattice Λ(q,-p), call a vertex x_i high weight if ⟨x_i,x_i⟩ = a_i > 2. An element ℓ is breakable if ℓ = x+y with ⟨x,y⟩ = -1 and |x|,|y| ≥ 3, where |z| = ⟨z,z⟩; otherwise ℓ is unbreakable. If ε[I] is a signed interval in Λ(q,-p), then ε[I] is unbreakable if and only if the interval [I] contains at most one high-weight vertex.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this from Greene, Corollary 3.5(4). The underlying interval calculation is as follows. For a positive interval [x_r+...+x_s], its norm is 2 plus the sum of the excesses a_i-2 over vertices in the interval. If the interval contains two high-weight vertices, split it at an edge between them; the two resulting consecutive subintervals pair by -1, and each has norm at least 3, giving a breaking. Conversely, if an interval contains at most one high-weight vertex, then any consecutive split has at least one side with no high-weight vertex and hence norm 2, so it cannot be a breaking of the required kind. Greene's result supplies the reduction from arbitrary breakings to this interval picture.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The linear lattice Λ(q,-p) is indecomposable: it is not isomorphic to an orthogonal direct sum of two nontrivial integral lattices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as Greene, Corollary 3.5(2), and does not give a proof. The statement is used later in the paper to rule out a zero coordinate σ_0 in a changemaker embedding: if σ_0 = 0, then e_0 would give an orthogonal rank-one direct summand of σ^⊥, contradicting indecomposability of the linear lattice.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a sublattice of a standard Euclidean lattice with orthonormal basis e_0,...,e_{n+1}. Let A be a subset of {0,1,...,n+1}, let each b_i be either 1 or -1, and suppose v = Σ_{i∈A} b_i e_i lies in L. If v = x+y for x,y ∈ L and ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0, then there is a subset B ⊂ A such that x = Σ_{i∈B} b_i e_i and y = Σ_{i∈A\B} b_i e_i. In other words, any nonnegative-pairing decomposition of a ±1-coordinate vector splits its coordinate support without changing signs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write x = Σ x_i e_i and y = Σ y_i e_i. Since x+y = v, each coordinate sum x_i+y_i is either 0 or ±1. For integers x_i,y_i with sum 0 or ±1, one has x_i y_i ≤ 0. Therefore ⟨x,y⟩ = Σ x_i y_i ≥ 0 forces every product x_i y_i to be exactly 0. If i is not in A, then x_i+y_i = 0 and x_i y_i = 0, so x_i = y_i = 0. If i is in A, then x_i+y_i = b_i and x_i y_i = 0, so one of x_i,y_i is b_i and the other is 0. Thus the support A is partitioned into the coordinates assigned to x and those assigned to y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let σ = (σ_0,...,σ_{n+1}) be a changemaker vector and let σ^⊥ ⊂ Z^{n+2} be its orthogonal complement with the standard Euclidean form. Let S = {v_1,...,v_{n+1}} be the standard basis of σ^⊥ as defined in the paper: v_j is either tight, v_j = 2e_0+e_1+...+e_{j-1}-e_j when σ_j = 1+σ_0+...+σ_{j-1}, or is of the form v_j = Σ_{i∈A} e_i - e_j with A ⊂ {0,...,j-1} chosen to represent σ_j and maximize Σ_{i∈A}2^i. Then each standard basis vector v_j is irreducible in σ^⊥. The paper also records the stronger Greene statement that a vector of the form -e_j+Σ_{i∈A}e_i lying in the relevant changemaker lattice is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as Greene, Lemma 3.13, and does not reproduce the proof. The mechanism is closely related to Lemma 3.14: a putative decomposition v_j = x+y with ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0 would have to split the ±1-coordinate support of v_j. Orthogonality to σ then forces each summand to satisfy a subset-sum equality involving the σ_i. The defining choice of the standard basis vector, together with the changemaker ordering/maximality convention, rules out a proper nonempty split. Thus one summand must be zero, contradicting reducibility.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.12, 3.15, 3.16, and 3.17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the standard basis S = {v_1,...,v_{n+1}} of a changemaker lattice σ^⊥, the paper records four structural constraints. First, if |v_{k+1}| = 2, then k is not a gappy index for any v_j, where a gappy index i for v_j = Σ_{r∈A}e_r - e_j means i ∈ A, i < j-1, and i+1 ∉ A. Second, if a standard basis vector v_j is breakable, then v_j is tight. Third, if a linear lattice Λ(q,-p) is isomorphic to a changemaker lattice, then it contains at most one tight vector. Fourth, for every standard basis vector v_j, the index j-1 lies in supp(v_j).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: All four statements are quoted from Greene: Lemma 3.12(3), Lemma 3.15, Lemma 4.2(1), and Lemma 3.12(1), respectively. The paper uses them as black-box combinatorial restrictions on possible supports of standard changemaker basis vectors. Their role in the later case analysis is to prevent gaps immediately before norm-two vectors, to isolate all breakable behavior into the single possible tight vector, and to guarantee that each v_j interacts with the preceding coordinate e_{j-1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 3.18, 3.22, 3.24 and Lemmas 3.19, 3.20, 3.21
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be a set of irreducible vectors in a linear lattice Λ(q,-p). For v ∈ T, write [v] for the interval corresponding to v up to sign. The intersection graph G(T) has vertex set T and an edge between v and w if [v] and [w] abut, meaning that the intervals are consecutive or share a common end. The paper records the following interval-pairing facts. If [v] and [w] abut, then ⟨v,w⟩ ≠ 0. If v_i and v_j are distinct unbreakable vectors with |v_i|,|v_j| ≥ 3, then |⟨v_i,v_j⟩| ≤ 1, with equality if and only if [v_i] and [v_j] are consecutive. In particular, two distinct unbreakable vectors of norm at least 3 contain different high-weight vertices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 3.19 is an immediate interval calculation: consecutive intervals have exactly one edge between them and hence pair by ±1, while intervals sharing a common endpoint have a nonzero contribution from their overlap/end behavior. Lemmas 3.20 and 3.21 are quoted from Greene. They combine the signed-interval classification with the unbreakability criterion: if two high-norm unbreakable intervals overlap too much or share a high-weight vertex, their pairing becomes too large in absolute value or creates a forbidden breaking; hence the only way to obtain absolute pairing 1 is the minimal interaction of consecutive intervals.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.23 and 3.25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the changemaker-linear-lattice setting in which the paper applies Greene's interval graph machinery, the intersection graph G(T) has two forbidden configurations. A claw is a quadruple (v;w_1,w_2,w_3) of vertices such that v neighbors each w_i but no two of the w_i neighbor each other; the paper states that G(T) has no claws. A heavy triple is a triple (v_1,v_2,v_3) of elements of T such that |v_i| ≥ 3 for each i and each pair among the v_i is connected by a path in G(T) disjoint from the third; the paper states that G(T) has no heavy triples.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The no-claw statement is quoted as Greene, Lemma 4.8, and the no-heavy-triple statement is based on Greene, Lemma 4.10. The paper does not reproduce their proofs. In Sections 7 and 8 these are used as graph-theoretic contradictions: after irreducible vectors have been converted into intervals, certain assumed supports force three mutually incompatible abutments around a center or force three high-norm intervals to occupy separated branches of the interval graph, and Greene's lemmas rule out those configurations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.11 and Corollaries 6.12-6.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the paper's q < p < 2q surgery setup, the linear lattice Λ(q,-p) embeds in Z^{n+2} as σ^⊥, and a sublattice D_4 ⊕ Z^{n-2} is specified by four indices a > b > c > d through the parity condition ⟨v,e_a+e_b+e_c+e_d⟩ even. Let Short_0 and Short_1 be the even and odd short characteristic covectors for D_4 ⊕ Z^{n-2}; define χ_0 = -Σ_i e_i and χ_1 = -2e_a - Σ_{i notin {a,b,c,d}} e_i, and define test-vector sets T_0 = {(χ-χ_0)/2 : χ ∈ Short_0} and T_1 = {(χ-χ_1)/2 : χ ∈ Short_1}. Then the sets {⟨χ,σ⟩ : χ ∈ T_0} and {⟨χ,σ⟩ : χ ∈ T_1} are both intervals of integers beginning at 0. Moreover Σ_i σ_i = max{⟨χ,σ⟩ : χ ∈ T_0} = max{⟨χ,σ⟩ : χ ∈ T_1} ± 1. Consequently σ is a changemaker vector, and σ_a = σ_b + σ_c + σ_d + θ for some θ ∈ {1,-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses Proposition 6.7, which says that the pairings of σ with all short characteristic covectors of D_4 ⊕ Z^{n-2} form a consecutive interval of integers. Short_0 and Short_1 give the two parities in this interval. Subtracting the minimal element χ_0 or χ_1 and dividing by 2 turns each parity class into an interval of integers starting at 0. For T_0, the test vectors are precisely 0-1 coordinate vectors, so the fact that their σ-pairings fill every value from 0 to Σ_i σ_i is exactly the changemaker condition. Comparing the two maxima gives the displayed ±1 relation, and after expanding the definition of χ_1 this becomes σ_a = σ_b+σ_c+σ_d+θ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same embedded setup, assume Λ(q,-p) = σ^⊥ has vertex basis x_0,...,x_n and that Λ(q,-p) ∩ (D_4 ⊕ Z^{n-2}) is the sublattice generated by 2x_0,x_1,...,x_n. Let v ∈ σ^⊥ be irreducible, and let [v] be its associated interval in the vertex basis. Then ⟨v,e_a+e_b+e_c+e_d⟩ is odd if and only if [v] contains x_0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The parity condition defining D_4 ⊕ Z^{n-2} says that ⟨v,e_a+e_b+e_c+e_d⟩ is even exactly when v lies in D_4 ⊕ Z^{n-2}. Intersecting with σ^⊥ = Λ(q,-p), this is equivalent to v lying in the sublattice generated by 2x_0,x_1,...,x_n. Since v is irreducible, Proposition 3.4 gives v = ±[I] for a single interval. Such an interval lies in the sublattice generated by 2x_0,x_1,...,x_n exactly when it does not contain x_0; if it contains x_0, the coefficient of x_0 is ±1 and cannot be generated by 2x_0 and the other basis elements. This proves the equivalence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup of Section 7, assume d > 0, σ_0 = 1, and σ_a = σ_b+σ_c+σ_d+θ with θ ∈ {1,-1}. Define w = θ e_0 + e_d + e_c + e_b - e_a. Then w is an irreducible element of σ^⊥. Also, the interval [w] associated to w in the linear lattice does not contain x_0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The identity σ_a = σ_b+σ_c+σ_d+θ and σ_0 = 1 imply ⟨w,σ⟩ = 0, so w ∈ σ^⊥. Suppose w = x+y with x,y ∈ σ^⊥ nonzero and ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0. Lemma 3.14 forces x and y to be obtained by partitioning the coordinate support of w. Orthogonality to σ leaves no proper split except, in the θ = -1 case, the split e_d-e_0 and -e_a+e_b+e_c. Both of those vectors are irreducible and, by Lemma 6.14, their corresponding intervals contain x_0; two such intervals cannot be orthogonal in the way the split would require, giving a contradiction. Therefore w is irreducible. Finally ⟨w,e_a+e_b+e_c+e_d⟩ = -1+1+1+1 = 2 is even, so Lemma 6.14 implies that [w] does not contain x_0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.1 and Corollary 8.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the d = 0 case of the paper's changemaker analysis, Lemma 8.1 proves that c = 1, σ_c = 1, and σ_a = σ_b+1. Define w' = -e_a+e_b+e_c. Then Corollary 8.2 states that w' is an irreducible, unbreakable vector in σ^⊥, and that the interval [w'] contains x_0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 8.1, the paper uses Lemma 6.15 with G = 1. That lemma gives 1 ≥ min{σ_f, σ_a-σ_b}; using the ordering convention on equal σ-coordinates and the relation from Corollary 6.13 forces σ_c = 1 and hence c = 1, and then the only possible value is σ_a = σ_b+1. For Corollary 8.2, the equality σ_a = σ_b+σ_c shows w' is orthogonal to σ. Lemma 3.13 gives irreducibility of this vector of the form -e_a+e_b+e_c. Since |w'| = 3, it cannot be breakable: a breaking x+y with |x|,|y| ≥ 3 and x·y = -1 would have norm at least 3+3-2 = 4. Finally ⟨w',e_a+e_b+e_c+e_d⟩ = -1+1+1+0 = 1 is odd, so Lemma 6.14 implies that [w'] contains x_0.

proof_techniques.name: Support-splitting test for ±1-coordinate vectors
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.14 is a very portable irreducibility test inside a Euclidean ambient lattice. If a vector v has only coefficients 0 and ±1 in an orthonormal basis, then any decomposition v = x+y with nonnegative dot product must split the coordinate support of v exactly; no coordinate can be shared between x and y, and no new coordinate can appear. The proof is purely coordinatewise and uses only integrality and the inequality ⟨x,y⟩ ≥ 0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a vertex or a strategically chosen lattice element in the tree lattice can be embedded or represented with ±1 coordinates, reducibility can be reduced to checking finitely many support partitions. This can turn an abstract decomposition u = a+b into a combinatorial statement about which coordinates or subtrees lie on each side.

proof_techniques.name: Convert irreducibles into connected intervals
proof_techniques.description: For positive definite linear lattices, Proposition 3.4 says every irreducible element is a signed interval in the vertex basis. This converts algebraic irreducibility into graph combinatorics: an irreducible has connected support, coefficient ±1 on that support, and can be studied through endpoints, containment, consecutive intervals, and overlapping intervals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem concerns a tree lattice rather than a chain. A natural analogue to try is that irreducible elements, or minimal counterexample pieces, should have connected support in the tree; disconnected support often gives an orthogonal or nonnegative decomposition. This suggests proving connected-support reductions before analyzing the bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Use norm formulas for connected plumbing sums
proof_techniques.description: In a linear lattice, the norm of an interval x_i+...+x_j is the sum of the vertex weights in the interval minus twice the number of internal edges, equivalently 2 plus the sum of a_k-2 over the interval. Proposition 3.6 uses this to characterize when an interval can be broken into two norm-at-least-3 pieces pairing by -1: this happens exactly when the interval contains at least two high-weight vertices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For any connected subset S of vertices in a weighted tree, the square of the sum of its vertices is Σ_{v∈S} w(v) - 2|E(S)|. The hypothesis w(v) < d(v) is a local failure of the usual weight-versus-degree inequality, so this type of norm bookkeeping may identify a connected subset whose decomposition behavior is forced.

proof_techniques.name: Intersection graph obstructions
proof_techniques.description: The paper associates to a set of irreducible interval vectors an intersection graph whose edges record abutment of intervals. Greene's no-claw and no-heavy-triple lemmas then rule out certain configurations of irreducible unbreakable vectors. In the classification proof, the authors repeatedly assume a possible support pattern, translate it into interval adjacencies, and get a contradiction from a claw or heavy triple.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A tree with a single bad vertex naturally has several branches adjacent to that vertex. If one assumes all relevant vertex-basis elements are reducible, the resulting decompositions may create several irreducible pieces meeting around the bad vertex; an analogue of the no-claw obstruction could force one vertex to be irreducible.

proof_techniques.name: Parity sublattice detects whether an irreducible interval contains a distinguished vertex
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.14 uses an index-two parity sublattice to detect whether an irreducible interval contains x_0. The key input is that the intersection of the linear lattice with the parity sublattice is generated by 2x_0 and all other vertex-basis vectors. Because an irreducible is a signed interval, membership in this sublattice is equivalent to having even coefficient of x_0, which is equivalent to not containing x_0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original tree lattice admits a useful index-two sublattice or mod-2 functional that singles out the bad vertex or a branch, parity could separate irreducible elements according to whether their support contains that vertex. This may help force an irreducible supported near the bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Construct special irreducible vectors from linear relations among coordinates
proof_techniques.description: In Sections 7 and 8, the paper derives coordinate relations such as σ_a = σ_b+σ_c+σ_d+θ or σ_a = σ_b+σ_c, then forms vectors like θe_0+e_d+e_c+e_b-e_a or -e_a+e_b+e_c. These vectors are automatically orthogonal to σ. Irreducibility is then proved either by the support-splitting lemma or by the standard-basis irreducibility lemma, and their intervals are located using parity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The bad-vertex inequality w(v) < d(v) may similarly provide a linear or quadratic imbalance among the vertex and neighbor classes. One possible strategy is to build an auxiliary lattice element from the bad vertex and its neighbors, prove it is irreducible by a support-splitting argument, and then use its interaction with the vertex basis to force an irreducible vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Minimal-index and gap elimination case analysis
proof_techniques.description: The paper's classification repeatedly chooses the smallest index with a property, then uses support constraints for standard changemaker basis vectors: j-1 must lie in supp(v_j), norm-two vectors prevent gaps, breakable vectors must be tight, and there is at most one tight vector. This makes possible supports extremely rigid and often reduces to a few small cases.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a finite tree, one can imitate this by choosing a minimal subtree, a closest vertex to the bad vertex, or a leaf among reducible vertices. Positive definiteness and minimality may force support patterns that can then be contradicted by local pairing computations.

other_useful_info: Most lattice lemmas in Section 3 are imported from Joshua Greene's work on the lens space realization problem, especially [Gre13]; the present paper uses them as a toolkit rather than reproving them. The terminology high weight in this paper means a chain vertex with norm greater than 2, while breakable means a decomposition with pairing -1 and both summands of norm at least 3; these are distinct from the original problem's bad vertex condition w(v) < d(v), but the same norm bookkeeping for sums over connected plumbing subgraphs is relevant. Sections 7 and 8 are useful as worked examples of proving irreducibility indirectly: establish a special irreducible interval, determine whether it contains a distinguished vertex by parity, and then use interval adjacencies to rule out support configurations via no-claw or no-heavy-triple lemmas.
why_relevant: Completes the prism-manifold lattice classification and repeatedly uses the same reducible/irreducible criterion for positive-definite lattices. It provides further examples of how plumbing-graph combinatorics force irreducible vertex-basis elements.

title: The irreducible vectors of a lattice: Some theory and applications
overall_summary: The paper introduces an additive notion of irreducibility for full-rank Euclidean lattices: a nonzero lattice vector is paper-irreducible if it cannot be written as a sum of two lattice vectors both strictly shorter than it. It proves that shortest vectors and certain successive-minimum vectors are paper-irreducible, gives an exact norm and inner-product characterization of paper-irreducibility, shows paper-irreducible vectors generate the lattice, and proves that every paper-irreducible vector is Voronoi relevant. Independently of the paper's new notion, its recalled Voronoi theorem is especially useful: a vector is relevant exactly when ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² for all lattice vectors x other than 0 and v. For the original problem, this is likely the most important bridge, because the original decomposition obstruction u=a+b with a·b≥0 is algebraically the negation of that relevant-vector inequality after taking x=a.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1 (relevant vectors define the Voronoi cell minimally)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n with Euclidean norm ∥·∥. Let V(L)={x∈R^n : ∥x∥≤∥x−y∥ for every y∈L} be its Voronoi cell at the origin, and for v∈L define H(v)={x∈R^n : ∥x∥≤∥x−v∥}. Let R(L) be the set of nonzero lattice vectors r such that V(L) and r+V(L) share a non-empty boundary. Then R(L) is the minimal subset A⊂L such that V(L)=∩_{v∈A} H(v).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality V(L)=∩_{y∈L}H(y) follows immediately from the definition of V(L). The relevant vectors are precisely those lattice vectors whose bisector inequality H(r) actually supports a boundary piece shared by the Voronoi cell of 0 and the Voronoi cell of r; such inequalities cannot be removed without enlarging the intersection. Conversely, if a nonzero lattice vector is not relevant, its inequality does not define a boundary piece of the Voronoi cell and is redundant once the inequalities from relevant vectors are imposed. This gives both the intersection formula using R(L) and minimality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (Voronoi criterion for relevant vectors)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n, and let v∈L\{0}. Then v∈R(L) if and only if 0 and v are the only closest lattice vectors of L to the point v/2. Equivalently, R(L)={v∈L\{0} : ∥v/2−x∥>∥v/2∥ for every x∈L\{0,v}}={v∈L\{0} : ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² for every x∈L\{0,v}}. Moreover, to check the inequality ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² it is enough to check lattice vectors x with ∥x∥<∥v∥.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes the Voronoi characterization: v is relevant exactly when the midpoint v/2 has exactly two closest lattice points, namely 0 and v. The first displayed description is just this closest-vector condition, since the distance from v/2 to both 0 and v equals ∥v/2∥. Expanding ∥v/2−x∥²>∥v/2∥² gives ∥x∥²−⟨v,x⟩>0, hence ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥². The restriction to ∥x∥<∥v∥ uses Cauchy-Schwarz: if ∥x∥≥∥v∥ and x≠v, then ⟨v,x⟩≤∥v∥∥x∥≤∥x∥², and equality in both inequalities would force x=v.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1 (witness for non-relevance)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. The paper states that if v∈L and v∉R(L), then there exists r∈R(L) such that ⟨v,r⟩≥∥r∥²; the meaningful nonzero case is v≠0, since 0 is not relevant and cannot satisfy the conclusion. In applications, this says that a non-relevant nonzero vector has a relevant vector witnessing failure of the strict inequality in Theorem 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as a known lemma from Micciancio and Voulgaris. A geometric reconstruction is as follows. Since relevant vectors give all irredundant halfspace inequalities for V(L), a point y lies in V(L) exactly when 2⟨y,r⟩≤∥r∥² for every r∈R(L). Apply this to y=v/2. If every relevant r satisfied ⟨v,r⟩<∥r∥², then v/2 would lie in the interior of the Voronoi cell. But v/2 is equidistant from 0 and v, so for nonzero v it cannot be an interior point for which 0 is strictly closer than every other lattice point. Hence some relevant r has ⟨v,r⟩≥∥r∥².

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2 (shortest vectors are relevant)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Let λ_1(L)=min{∥x∥ : x∈L\{0}} and S_1(L)={v∈L : ∥v∥=λ_1(L)} be the first shell. Then S_1(L)⊆R(L).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the inequality characterization of relevant vectors. Let v∈S_1(L). For any x∈L\{0,v}, there is no nonzero lattice vector shorter than v. If ∥x∥>∥v∥, then ⟨v,x⟩≤∥v∥∥x∥<∥x∥². If ∥x∥=∥v∥ and x≠v, then Cauchy-Schwarz is strict unless x is a positive scalar multiple of v; since the norms are equal, equality would force x=v, excluded. Thus ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² for all x≠0,v, so v is relevant by Theorem 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3 (relevant vectors generate)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Then there exists a generating set G of L such that G⊆R(L). Equivalently, the subgroup generated by R(L) is all of L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take any nonzero vector y∈L. If y is relevant, subtract y and reach 0. If y is not relevant, Lemma 1 gives r∈R(L) with ⟨y,r⟩≥∥r∥². Then ∥y−r∥²=∥y∥²+∥r∥²−2⟨y,r⟩≤∥y∥²−∥r∥²<∥y∥², so replacing y by y−r strictly decreases the norm. Since a lattice has only finitely many points in a bounded ball, repeated reductions terminate at 0. Reversing the reductions writes the original y as an integer sum of relevant vectors. Hence R(L) spans L as an abelian group, and a generating subset G⊆R(L) may be chosen.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4 and Lemma 3 (paper-irreducibility and shortest vectors)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the paper's terminology, a nonzero vector v∈L is irreducible if there do not exist v_1,v_2∈L such that v=v_1+v_2 and ∥v_i∥<∥v∥ for i=1,2. Let Irr(L) be the set of such vectors. For any full-rank lattice L in R^n, S_1(L)⊆Irr(L), where S_1(L) is the set of shortest nonzero lattice vectors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let v be shortest, so ∥v∥=λ_1(L). If v=v_1+v_2 with ∥v_i∥<∥v∥ for both i, then each v_i has norm strictly less than λ_1(L). Since λ_1(L) is the smallest positive norm in L, both v_i must be 0. This contradicts v≠0. Therefore no such decomposition exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4 (successive-minimum vector outside the shorter sublattice is paper-irreducible)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. For λ>0, let L_λ be the sublattice spanned by all lattice vectors of norm strictly less than λ. Let λ_i(L) be the i-th successive minimum. If v∈L satisfies ∥v∥=λ_i(L) for some 1≤i≤n and v∉L_{λ_i(L)}, then v∈Irr(L) in the paper's sense.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The case i=1 is Lemma 3. For i≥2, suppose v is not paper-irreducible. Then v=v_1+v_2 with both ∥v_j∥<∥v∥=λ_i(L). Hence v_1 and v_2 both lie in L_{λ_i(L)} by definition of that shorter-vector sublattice. Since L_{λ_i(L)} is a subgroup, v=v_1+v_2 lies in L_{λ_i(L)}, contradicting the hypothesis v∉L_{λ_i(L)}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1 (paper-irreducibles attain all successive minima)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. For every i=1,...,n, there exists v∈Irr(L) such that ∥v∥=λ_i(L).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 4 it suffices, for each i, to find a vector of norm λ_i(L) that is not in the sublattice L_{λ_i(L)} spanned by shorter vectors. Let k be the smallest index with λ_k(L)=λ_i(L), so λ_{k−1}(L)<λ_k(L)=λ_i(L), with the convention λ_0(L)=0. The shorter-vector sublattice L_{λ_i(L)} has rank k−1: there are k−1 independent vectors of norm below λ_i(L), but not k. If every vector of norm λ_i(L) lay in L_{λ_i(L)}, then all vectors of norm at most λ_i(L) would lie in a rank k−1 sublattice, making it impossible to find k linearly independent vectors of norm at most λ_k(L). This contradicts the definition of λ_k(L). Therefore some norm-λ_i vector lies outside L_{λ_i(L)}, and Proposition 4 makes it paper-irreducible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5 (paper-irreducible vectors generate)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Then there exists a generating set G of L such that G⊆Irr(L). Equivalently, the subgroup generated by the paper-irreducible vectors is all of L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take any v∈L. If v is paper-irreducible, it is already in Irr(L). Otherwise write v=v_1+v_2 with ∥v_i∥<∥v∥. For each summand that is not paper-irreducible, decompose it again into two strictly shorter lattice vectors. At every branch the norm strictly decreases, and there are only finitely many lattice points in the closed ball B(0,∥v∥). Thus the recursive decomposition terminates after finitely many steps. The final leaves are paper-irreducible vectors whose sum is v. Hence every lattice vector is an integer sum of paper-irreducibles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4 (characterization of paper-irreducible vectors)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. A nonzero vector v∈L is paper-irreducible if and only if for every x∈L with ∥x∥<∥v∥ one has ∥v−x∥≥∥v∥. Equivalently, v∈Irr(L) if and only if for every x∈L with ∥x∥<∥v∥ one has 2⟨v,x⟩≤∥x∥².
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p is paper-irreducible and x is shorter than p, then ∥p−x∥ cannot also be shorter than p, because p=x+(p−x) would be a decomposition into two shorter lattice vectors. Thus ∥p−x∥≥∥p∥. Conversely, suppose v has the stated norm property. In any decomposition v=v_1+v_2, if ∥v_1∥<∥v∥ then the property with x=v_1 gives ∥v_2∥=∥v−v_1∥≥∥v∥, so the decomposition is not into two shorter vectors. If ∥v_1∥≥∥v∥, it is also not a two-shorter decomposition. Therefore v is paper-irreducible. The inner-product form is obtained by expanding ∥v−x∥²≥∥v∥², which is equivalent to ∥x∥²−2⟨v,x⟩≥0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6 (paper-irreducible vectors are relevant)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Then every paper-irreducible vector of L is a relevant vector: Irr(L)⊆R(L). Consequently Irr(L) is finite.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Theorem 1 and Lemma 4. It is enough to show that if v is paper-irreducible and x∈L\{0} has ∥x∥<∥v∥, then ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥². Lemma 4 gives 2⟨v,x⟩≤∥x∥². If ⟨v,x⟩≤0, then ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² because x≠0. If ⟨v,x⟩>0, then ⟨v,x⟩≤∥x∥²/2<∥x∥². By the shorter-vector reduction in Theorem 1, this proves v∈R(L). Finiteness follows because R(L) is finite.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 7 and 8 (complete systems of paper-irreducible vectors generate and are angle-separated)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a full-rank lattice in R^n. Partition Irr(L) into equivalence classes by equal norm. For each class S choose a subset tilde S such that if p∈tilde S then −p∈tilde S, and tilde S is maximal subject to the condition that for every p,q∈tilde S with q≠−p one has ∥p+q∥≥∥p∥. Define P(L)=∪_S tilde S, called a complete system of irreducible vectors. Then every such P(L) contains a generating set of L. Moreover, if p_1,p_2∈P(L) and p_1≠±p_2, then min{∥p_1+p_2∥,∥p_1−p_2∥}≥max{∥p_1∥,∥p_2∥}; consequently, if θ is the Euclidean angle between p_1 and p_2, then |cos θ|≤1/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For generation, order the finitely many norm classes C_1,...,C_t by increasing norm, and define A_i=(∪_{j<i}C_j)∪(∪_{j≥i}tilde C_j). Then A_1=P(L) and A_{t+1}=Irr(L), and Irr(L) spans L by Proposition 5. Descend on i. If A_k spans, the only vectors removed when passing to A_{k−1} are those in C_{k−1}\tilde C_{k−1}. For such a vector v, maximality of tilde C_{k−1} gives a representative tilde v∈tilde C_{k−1} such that, after choosing the appropriate sign, w=v+tilde v has ∥w∥<∥v∥. The shorter vector w is either paper-irreducible of a smaller class or decomposes into still shorter paper-irreducibles, all of which lie in A_{k−1}; hence v=w−tilde v is generated by A_{k−1}. Thus P(L) spans. For the separation property, assume ∥p_2∥≤∥p_1∥. If the norms are unequal, Lemma 4 applied to the longer paper-irreducible p_1 with x=±p_2 gives ∥p_1∓p_2∥≥∥p_1∥. If the norms are equal, the defining condition of the chosen representative set gives the same conclusion for sums, and sign symmetry gives it for differences. Expanding ∥p_1±p_2∥²≥∥p_1∥² yields 2|⟨p_1,p_2⟩|≤∥p_2∥²; dividing by ∥p_1∥∥p_2∥ gives |cos θ|≤1/2.

proof_techniques.name: Voronoi relevance as an additive irreducibility test
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls the classical Voronoi criterion that v is relevant exactly when ⟨v,x⟩<∥x∥² for all x∈L\{0,v}. This converts a geometric statement about adjacent Voronoi cells, or about the midpoint v/2 having only 0 and v as closest lattice points, into a purely algebraic inequality against every other lattice vector.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original positive definite lattice, u=a+b with a·b≥0 is equivalent to taking x=a and obtaining u·x≥x². Thus the original irreducibility of a vertex u is exactly the relevant-vector inequality from Theorem 1, after realizing the positive definite form as a Euclidean lattice.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction by relevant vectors
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the fact that if a nonzero vector is not relevant, then some relevant vector r satisfies ⟨v,r⟩≥∥r∥². Subtracting r strictly decreases norm, since ∥v−r∥²=∥v∥²+∥r∥²−2⟨v,r⟩≤∥v∥²−∥r∥². Iterating gives a finite descent to 0 and proves that relevant vectors generate the lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one assumes all tree vertices are reducible/non-relevant, this reduction mechanism supplies relevant witnesses and strictly decreasing norm replacements. Such a descent might be combined with the tree basis and the bad-vertex hypothesis to force a contradiction.

proof_techniques.name: Norm-descent decomposition into paper-irreducibles
proof_techniques.description: For the paper's stronger irreducibility notion, any reducible vector splits as a sum of two strictly shorter lattice vectors. Repeating this split must terminate because a lattice has finitely many points in any ball, so every lattice vector is a sum of paper-irreducible vectors. This is a well-founded induction on norm rather than on coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a positive definite lattice, any original decomposition with a·b≥0 automatically has both summands strictly shorter than u. Therefore proving a vertex is paper-irreducible would be sufficient for the original problem, and norm-descent arguments may help organize contradictions.

proof_techniques.name: Testing irreducibility by inequalities against shorter vectors
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4 replaces the existential condition of paper-reducibility by a universal inequality: v is paper-irreducible iff every shorter lattice vector x satisfies 2⟨v,x⟩≤∥x∥². The proof is just the expansion of ∥v−x∥≥∥v∥, but it is powerful because only shorter x need be checked.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a graph lattice, local control of dot products u·x and norms x² may be easier than direct control of all decompositions. The analogous relevant-vector test for the original notion is u·x<x² for all x≠0,u, and Theorem 1 says longer x can often be discarded by Cauchy-Schwarz.

proof_techniques.name: Successive-minimum outside-span criterion
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4 shows that a vector attaining a successive minimum is paper-irreducible provided it does not lie in the sublattice generated by strictly shorter vectors. The corollary proves that every successive minimum is attained by some paper-irreducible vector by a rank argument on the shorter-vector sublattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree hypotheses can be used to identify a vertex that realizes a new successive minimum or is outside the span of all shorter vectors, the paper immediately gives paper-irreducibility, hence relevance/original irreducibility.

proof_techniques.name: Angle-separated complete systems
proof_techniques.description: The paper selects, from equal-norm classes of paper-irreducibles, sign-symmetric maximal representative sets whose non-opposite pairwise sums are not shorter. The union P(L) still spans the lattice and has strong pairwise separation: for distinct non-opposite p,q∈P(L), both p+q and p−q have norm at least max{∥p∥,∥q∥}, so |cos θ(p,q)|≤1/2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This may be useful if a proof needs a small or well-separated generating set of relevant or irreducible vectors and then compares it with the distinguished vertex basis of a tree lattice.

other_useful_info: Terminology caution: the paper's irreducible vectors are not the same as the original problem's irreducible elements; the paper's notion forbids all decompositions into two strictly shorter vectors, while the original notion forbids decompositions with nonnegative cross term. The paper proves the chain S_1(L)⊆Irr(L)⊆R(L), and for any complete system P(L), S_1(L)⊆P(L)⊆Irr(L)⊆R(L). It also recalls Minkowski's bound |R(L)|≤2(2^n−1), conjectures that Irr(L) contains a basis, and proves that complete systems P(L) generate and satisfy |P(L)|<2^{0.402n}; these are mostly structural rather than directly tailored to the tree problem. Appendix A gives examples showing that the inclusions S_1(L)⊆Irr(L)⊆R(L) can be strict or equal in different ways for special lattices such as D_n^* and A_n^*, and it references Conway–Sloane obtuse-superbasis results in that context.
why_relevant: Studies a closely related general notion of irreducible lattice vector and connects it to Voronoi-relevant vectors and obtuse superbases. Although the definition is not identical, its lattice-geometric tools may support an independent proof strategy.

title: On the Floer homology of plumbed three-manifolds
overall_summary: The paper computes Heegaard Floer homology HF+ for three-manifolds obtained by plumbing according to negative-definite weighted forests with at most one bad vertex, where the plumbing form has diagonal entries m(v) and off-diagonal entries +1 along edges, and a vertex is bad when m(v) > -d(v). The main construction is a purely combinatorial module H+(G), defined from characteristic covectors and adjunction-type relations, and the paper proves that the cobordism map T+ identifies HF+(-Y(G)) with H+(G) under the one-bad-vertex hypothesis. The proof is by induction using graph operations that delete a vertex, increase a vertex weight, or add a -1 leaf, together with surgery exact triangles and a finite characteristic-vector algorithm based on full paths. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the paper's sign convention for bad vertices, its induction on definite weighted trees with at most one bad vertex, the leaf-removal recurrence for strict no-bad graphs, and the full-path algorithm that canonically controls characteristic-vector equivalence classes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A weighted graph G is a graph with an integer weight m(v) on each vertex. It determines a symmetric form on the free abelian group generated by the vertices by v·v = m(v), v·w = 1 if v and w are joined by an edge, and v·w = 0 otherwise. The graph G is called negative-definite if it is a disjoint union of trees and this form is negative definite. The degree d(v) of a vertex v is the number of incident edges. A vertex v is called bad if m(v) > -d(v).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition. The important sign convention in the paper is that edges contribute +1 to the intersection form, so the bad-vertex inequality is m(v) > -d(v).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 / Theorem 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a negative-definite weighted graph with at most one bad vertex. Let Y(G) be the oriented boundary of the plumbing four-manifold X(G). Let Char(G) be the set of characteristic covectors K for the intersection form, and for a Spin^c structure t on the boundary let Char_t(G) be the subset corresponding to Spin^c structures on X(G) restricting to t. Let T0+ = Z[U,U^{-1}]/U·Z[U], with U lowering degree by 2. Define H+(G,t) to be the finite-support functions φ: Char_t(G) -> T0+ satisfying the following adjunction relations for every vertex v and characteristic vector K: put 2n = <K,v> + v·v; if n >= 0 then U^n φ(K + 2PD[v]) = φ(K), while if n <= 0 then φ(K + 2PD[v]) = U^{-n} φ(K). The grading on H+(G,t) is defined by requiring a homogeneous φ of degree d to satisfy deg φ(K) - (K^2 + |G|)/4 = d whenever φ(K) is nonzero. Then the map T+ defined by the plumbing cobordism W(G), namely T+(ξ)(K) = F^+_{W(G),s}(ξ) where c1(s)=K, is a grading-preserving Z[U]-module isomorphism HF+(-Y(G),t) ≅ H+(G,t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is first carried out over F = Z/2Z, where signs are absent, and then upgraded to Z in Subsection 2.1 by choosing coherent orientations for cobordism maps. The map T+ is shown to land in H+(G,t) because cobordism maps have finite support and satisfy the adjunction relations for embedded spheres; the grading follows from the degree-shift formula for cobordism maps. The inductive mechanism compares three graphs associated to a distinguished vertex v: G'(v), obtained by adding a new -1 vertex e adjacent only to v; G, the original graph; and G-v, obtained by deleting v. The surgery exact triangle gives, under suitable hypotheses, a short exact sequence of HF+ groups for -Y(G'(v)), -Y(G), and -Y(G-v). In parallel, the paper defines algebraic maps A+: H+(G'(v)) -> H+(G) and B+: H+(G) -> H+(G-v), proves that the squares with T+ commute, proves A+ is injective and B+ A+ = 0, and uses rank counts and diagram chases to prove exactness on the H+ side. The base case is handled for strictly no-bad graphs m(v) < -d(v) at every vertex by induction on the number of vertices and on the weight of a leaf; the key rank input is Lemma 2.7, which counts Ker U in H+(G) by Spin^c structures. Proposition 2.12 extends the result from strict no-bad graphs to all no-bad graphs m(v) <= -d(v). Finally, for a graph with one bad vertex, deleting the bad vertex gives a no-bad graph, and the same exact-triangle/diagram-chase induction proves the theorem. Over Z, the proof fixes one Spin^c structure over the cobordism and chooses signs for all other cobordism maps so the induced HF∞ maps agree up to powers of U; the signs in the algebraic maps A+ and B+ are then chosen compatibly so the cancellation B+A+=0 remains valid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a negative-definite weighted graph with at most two bad vertices. For each Spin^c structure t on Y(G), the same map T+ identifies H+(G,t) with the even-parity summand HF+_ev(-Y(G),t) of HF+(-Y(G),t), where the parity is the absolute Z/2Z grading.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof repeats the induction used for Theorem 2.1 but chooses one of the two bad vertices as the distinguished vertex. After deleting that vertex, the graph has at most one bad vertex, so Theorem 2.1 and Corollary 1.4 imply that the odd part of HF+ for the deleted graph vanishes. Thus the surgery exact sequence restricts to a useful exact sequence on even-parity summands. The same algebraic sequence for H+ and the same commutative diagram with T+ then force the desired isomorphism by induction and a diagram chase.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K+(G) be the quotient of Z_{>=0} × Char(G), whose elements are written U^m ⊗ K, by the following equivalence relation. For a vertex v and 2n = <K,v> + v·v, if n >= 0 impose U^{n+m} ⊗ (K+2PD[v]) ~ U^m ⊗ K, while if n <= 0 impose U^m ⊗ (K+2PD[v]) ~ U^{m-n} ⊗ K. For n >= 0 define B_n = {K in Char(G) such that for every vertex v, |<K,v>| <= -m(v)+2n}. Then the quotient map induces a surjection from the finite union ⋃_{i=0}^n U^i ⊗ B_{n-i} onto K+(G)/(Z_{>n} × Char(G)), where the denominator means that classes represented with U-exponent greater than n are killed. Moreover Ker(U^{n+1}) inside H+(G;F) is naturally identified with Hom(K+(G)/(Z_{>n} × Char(G)),F), and the same identification holds over Z.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The surjectivity follows directly from the defining equivalence relation: if a representative U^i ⊗ K has some evaluation <K,v> outside the bound defining B_{n-i}, one applies the corresponding relation at v to replace it by an equivalent representative with a better evaluation or with U-exponent exceeding n, which is zero in the quotient. The duality map pairs φ in Ker(U^{n+1}) with U^ell ⊗ K by taking the degree-zero part of U^ell φ(K). This pairing is well-defined on K+(G) precisely because φ satisfies the adjunction relations. It is injective because the degree-zero parts of U^ell φ(K) for 0 <= ell <= n determine φ(K) when U^{n+1}φ=0. It is surjective because any homomorphism τ on the quotient defines φ(K)=Σ_{ell=0}^n τ(U^ell ⊗ K) U^{-ell}, and the relations in K+(G) ensure that this φ lies in H+(G).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a negative-definite plumbing graph G, the cobordism map T+: HF+(-Y(G),t) -> Hom(Char_t(G),T0+) is F[U]-equivariant and degree-preserving, has finite support, and its image lies in H+(G,t); equivalently, the functions T+(ξ) satisfy the adjunction relations defining H+(G,t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Finite support follows from general finiteness properties of HF+ cobordism maps, or from the degree-shift formula for cobordism maps in a negative-definite cobordism. The same degree-shift formula proves that T+ respects the grading on H+(G,t). The adjunction relations are exactly the adjunction relations for maps induced by cobordisms containing the embedded spheres represented by the plumbing vertices; the paper cites Theorem 3.1 of Ozsváth-Szabó, Holomorphic disk invariants for symplectic four-manifolds, where these relations are proved for embedded surfaces of arbitrary genus.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a weighted graph with distinguished vertex v. Let G'(v) be obtained from G by adding a new vertex e of weight -1 connected only to v. Let G+1(v) be obtained from G by increasing the weight of v by one and leaving all other weights unchanged. Then there is a grading-preserving isomorphism R: H+(G'(v)) -> H+(G+1(v)). Moreover this isomorphism is natural with respect to T+: under the natural diffeomorphism Y(G'(v)) ≅ Y(G+1(v)), the square with HF+(-Y) on top and H+ on bottom commutes.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first changes basis in the plumbing lattice for G'(v). Sliding the handle corresponding to v over the new -1 handle identifies the graph G'(v) with the disjoint union of G+1(v) and an isolated -1 vertex f. This gives H+(G'(v)) ≅ H+(G+1(v) ∪ f). Next define Q: H+(G+1(v)) -> H+(G+1(v) ∪ f) by Q(φ)(K,2m+1)=U^{m(m+1)/2}φ(K) and Q(φ)(K,-(2m+1))=U^{m(m+1)/2}φ(K) for m >= 0, where (K,l) records the evaluation l on f. The adjunction relation for the isolated -1 vertex shows Q is an isomorphism. The desired R is the composite inverse/forward isomorphism. Naturality follows from handleslide invariance of HF+ cobordism maps and the blow-up formula: the map associated to G'(v) with the corresponding characteristic vector equals the map for G+1(v) ∪ f with evaluation 1 on f, which equals the map for G+1(v).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If G is a negative-definite graph with no bad vertices, meaning m(v) <= -d(v) for every vertex v, then |H1(Y(G);Z)| equals rk HFhat(Y(G)) over F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Both quantities satisfy the same additivity relation N(G)=N(G+1(v))+N(G-v) whenever G+1(v) also has no bad vertices. For |H1(Y(G);Z)| this is an elementary determinant identity for the plumbing matrix. For rk HFhat(Y(G)) it follows from the long exact sequence for HFhat associated to the surgery triple corresponding to G+1(v), G, and G-v. Both quantities are normalized by N(empty graph)=1. The induction is completed by observing that if G has no bad vertices and contains a leaf of weight -1, then blowing down that leaf produces a graph with no bad vertices and one fewer vertex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Positive-U criterion used in Lemma 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a negative-definite weighted graph G, suppose a characteristic vector L satisfies m(v)+2 <= <L,v> <= -m(v) for every vertex v. In the proof of Lemma 2.7 the paper uses the following criterion: L is equivalent in K+(G) to a vector of the form U^n ⊗ L' with n > 0 if and only if there is a nonempty set of vertices {v1,...,vk} and an element A = a1 PD[v1] + ... + ak PD[vk] with all ai > 0 such that L·A + A·A > 0. If G satisfies the strict inequalities m(v) < -d(v) at every vertex, then the coefficients ai can be chosen in {0,1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The criterion is obtained by expanding the defining equivalence relation of K+(G). A sequence of allowed moves from L to a representative with positive U-exponent records a cumulative sum A of Poincare duals of vertices; the condition that the U-exponent increases is equivalent to the inequality L·A + A·A > 0. Conversely, such an A can be realized by a sequence of vertex moves producing a positive U power. Under the strict condition m(v) < -d(v), repeated use of the same vertex can be shortened or canceled without destroying the positivity condition, so one may take only coefficients 0 or 1. The paper points forward to Proposition 3.2 for the underlying path-shortening argument.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a negative-definite plumbing graph satisfying the strict inequality m(v) < -d(v) at every vertex. Then the rank of Ker U inside H+(G) equals the number of Spin^c structures over Y(G), equivalently |H1(Y(G);Z)|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 2.3 with n=0, Ker U is dual to the equivalence classes in K+(G) represented by characteristic vectors satisfying |<K,v>| <= -m(v) and not equivalent to any positive U multiple.
