By subtracting Poincare duals as needed, these are represented by vectors satisfying m(v)+2 <= <K,v> <= -m(v). The paper calls such representatives short vectors when they are not positive-U-equivalent. Let S(G) be the set of short vectors. Choose a leaf v of G and let w be its neighbor. The key combinatorial recurrence is |S(G)| = -m(v)|S(G-v)| - |S(G-v-w)|. To prove it, fix a short vector K on G-v-w. There is a maximal integer c(K) such that (K,p) is short on G-v precisely for p in the interval m(w)+2 <= p <= c(K). The existence and interval property use the positive-U criterion: if (K,m(w)+2) were not short on G-v, then there would be A=A0+PD[w] with positive coefficients and (K,m(w)+2)·A + A·A > 0; if j is the number of components of A0 adjacent to w, the expression becomes K·A0 + A0·A0 + 2m(w)+2+2j, which is negative because K is short on G-v-w and j <= d(w) < -m(w). A similar count shows that the short vectors on G restricting to K are all triples (K,p,i) with m(w)+2 <= p <= c(K) and m(v)+2 <= i <= -m(v), except for the single excluded vector (K,c(K),-m(v)). Summing over K gives the recurrence. The determinant/order |H1(Y(G);Z)| = (-1)^{|G|} det(G) satisfies the same leaf recurrence and the same initial normalization, so |S(G)| = |H1(Y(G);Z)|, proving the lemma.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose G+1(v) is a negative-definite plumbing diagram and G-v contains no bad vertices. Let G'(v) be obtained by adding a -1 vertex adjacent to v. Then the HF+ maps induced by the two-handle cobordisms fit into a short exact sequence 0 -> HF+(-Y(G'(v))) -> HF+(-Y(G)) -> HF+(-Y(G-v)) -> 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The surgery exact triangle gives a long exact sequence HF+(-Y(G'(v))) -> HF+(-Y(G)) -> HF+(-Y(G-v)) -> HF+(-Y(G'(v))) -> ... . The hypothesis that G+1(v) is negative definite implies that the two cobordisms inducing the first two maps are negative definite; therefore the remaining cobordism in the triangle is not negative definite and induces the zero map on HF∞. Since G-v has no bad vertices, Lemma 2.6 implies HF+_red(-Y(G-v))=0. Hence the connecting map out of HF+(-Y(G-v)) is zero, and the long exact sequence breaks into the asserted short exact sequence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 2.9 and 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Write characteristic vectors for G'(v) as triples (K,i,l), where K is the restriction to G-v, i is the evaluation on v, and l is the evaluation on the added -1 vertex e; write characteristic vectors for G as pairs (K,i). For finitely supported φ on Char(G'(v)), define A+(φ) on Char(G) by A+(φ)(K,p)=Σ_{j in Z} φ(K,p,2j+1). For finitely supported ψ on Char(G), define B+(ψ) on Char(G-v) by B+(ψ)(K)=Σ_{i in Z} ψ(K,2i+m(v)). Lemma 2.9 says these formulas induce maps A+: H+(G'(v)) -> H+(G) and B+: H+(G) -> H+(G-v). Lemma 2.10 says that if G'(v) is negative definite, then the corresponding squares with the HF+ cobordism maps commute, A+ is injective, and B+∘A+ = 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The fact that A+ and B+ preserve H+ is a direct verification of the adjunction relations after summing over all extensions of a characteristic vector. Commutativity with the HF+ maps follows from naturality of cobordism maps under composition: summing over Spin^c structures restricting to a fixed boundary structure gives exactly the algebraic sums defining A+ and B+. To prove injectivity of A+, put a filtration F on T0+ by the highest nonzero grading. For nonzero φ in H+(G'(v)), choose a characteristic vector K maximizing F(φ(K)); among these choose one maximizing <K,v>. Among all extensions of K|_G to G'(v), the adjunction relation at the -1 vertex e shows any extension with |<L,e>|>1 has strictly lower filtration, while if <K,e>=+1 then adding 2PD[e] preserves φ but increases the v-evaluation, contradicting maximality. Thus <K,e>=-1 and K is the unique filtration-maximal term in A+(φ)(K|_G), so A+(φ) is nonzero. For B+A+=0, the adjunction relation at e gives for i>=0 the identities φ(K,p,2i+1)=U^{i(i+1)/2}φ(K,p+2i+2,-1) and φ(K,p,-(2i+1))=U^{i(i+1)/2}φ(K,p-2i,-1). These pair the terms in the double sum defining B+A+ and make them cancel over F; Subsection 2.1 explains the sign choices needed for the same cancellation over Z.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If G is a negative-definite plumbing diagram satisfying m(v) < -d(v) at every vertex v, then T+: HF+(-Y(G)) -> H+(G) is an isomorphism.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the number of vertices, with a subinduction on -m(v) for a chosen leaf v. The empty graph and the single -1 vertex are immediate. The subinduction allows the case m(v)=-1 because such a leaf can be blown down, and Proposition 2.5 identifies the relevant H+ groups naturally with T+. For the inductive step, the paper first works with Ker U, denoted Hhat(G). The commutative diagram built from G'(v), G, and G-v has a short exact top row by Proposition 2.8, and the side vertical maps are isomorphisms by induction. Lemma 2.10 gives Bhat Ahat=0, and Lemma 2.7 gives the rank equality needed to conclude exactness in the bottom row; the five lemma then gives that T induces an isomorphism on Ker U. The same diagram for full HF+ and H+ is then used: A+ is injective, B+ is surjective by the HF+ exact sequence and the inductive isomorphisms, exactness in the middle follows from the Ker U calculation together with B+A+=0, and another five-lemma argument proves the full isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If G is a negative-definite plumbing diagram with no bad vertices, meaning m(v) <= -d(v) for every vertex, then T+: HF+(-Y(G)) -> H+(G) is an isomorphism.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof inducts on the number of vertices for which d(v) = -m(v). If there are none, the graph satisfies the strict inequalities and Proposition 2.11 applies. For the inductive step, the paper again uses the diagram relating G'(v), G, and G-v, now with v not necessarily a leaf and chosen so that the side graphs are simpler in the induction. The induction hypothesis gives that T+ is an isomorphism for the two side terms. Lemma 2.10 gives the commutative diagram, injectivity of A+, and B+A+=0. A straightforward diagram chase then forces T+ to be an isomorphism for the remaining graph.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume G is a negative-definite weighted graph and use K+(G) as in Lemma 2.3. Start with a characteristic vector K satisfying the initial inequalities m(v)+2 <= <K,v> <= -m(v) for every vertex v. A full path is a sequence K=K0,K1,...,Kn=L obtained by repeatedly choosing a vertex v_{i+1} with <K_i,v_{i+1}> = -m(v_{i+1}) and setting K_{i+1}=K_i+2PD[v_{i+1}], until the final vector L either satisfies the terminal inequalities m(v) <= <L,v> <= -m(v)-2 for every vertex v, or else violates the upper bound at some vertex, namely <L,v> > -m(v). Proposition 3.2 states: every equivalence class in K+(G) that contains no representative U^m ⊗ K' with m>0 has a unique representative K satisfying the initial inequalities. Moreover, a characteristic vector K satisfying the initial inequalities is not equivalent to any U^m ⊗ K' with m>0 if and only if there exists a full path starting at K and ending at a vector L satisfying the terminal inequalities.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let M be a characteristic vector not equivalent to any positive U multiple. Extend M forward by repeatedly adding 2PD[v] when <L_j,v>=-m(v). During this process all evaluations remain in the closed interval m(v) <= <L_j,v> <= -m(v); otherwise the equivalence relation would produce a positive U multiple. Negative definiteness ensures the process terminates, and it terminates at a vector satisfying the terminal inequalities. Similarly extend backward by subtracting 2PD[v] when the lower endpoint <L_j,v>=m(v) occurs; this terminates at a vector satisfying the initial inequalities. Thus every such class has an initial representative lying on a full path. The endpoint of a full path through a fixed intermediate vector is unique: if several vertices are at the upper endpoint, any path to a terminal vector must add all their Poincare duals, and induction on the remaining distance to the endpoint proves independence of choices. If two characteristic vectors are equivalent with no positive U powers, they can be connected by a sequence of square-preserving moves M_{i+1}=M_i-2ε_{i+1}PD[u_{i+1}], where ε_{i+1}=±1 and <M_i,u_{i+1}>=ε_{i+1}m(u_{i+1}); one-step equivalences lie on a common full path, and induction over the sequence shows they have the same terminal endpoint. Reversing the argument gives uniqueness of the initial representative. Finally, if an initial vector K is equivalent to a positive U multiple, choose a sequence from K to a vector violating the closed bounds. The paper shows that any negative move in such a sequence can be canceled with an earlier positive move at the same vertex, shortening the sequence; hence one may take all moves to be positive. If K also had a full path to a good terminal vector L, the positive sequence would be a subsequence of that full path after reordering, and extending it would imply L ~ U^m ⊗ L for m>0, impossible. Therefore exactly the initial vectors admitting good full paths represent non-positive-U classes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a negative-definite graph with at most two bad vertices, and fix a Spin^c structure t over Y(G). Then the correction term satisfies d(Y(G),t) = max_{K in Char_t(G)} (K^2 + |G|)/4. The paper also notes that this maximum is achieved among the finitely many characteristic vectors K in Char_t(G) satisfying |K·v| <= |m(v)| for every vertex v, with an even smaller finite set described by Proposition 3.2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose K0 in Char_t(G) maximizing K^2. Define Φ_N in H+(G,t) by Φ_N(K)=U^{(K0^2-K^2)/8 - N}, with positive powers of U interpreted as zero in T0+. Then Φ_0 is supported exactly on the maximal-square characteristic vectors, UΦ_0=0, and UΦ_{N+1}=Φ_N. Its degree is -(K0^2+|G|)/4. Theorem 2.1 for at most one bad vertex, and Theorem 2.2 for at most two bad vertices on the even part, identify this tower in H+(G,t) with the corresponding HF+ tower for -Y(G), so d(-Y(G),t)=deg Φ_0. Since d(-Y,t)=-d(Y,t), the formula follows.

proof_techniques.name: Bad-vertex sign convention and definite-graph dictionary
proof_techniques.description: The paper works with plumbing graphs whose form has diagonal entries m(v) and off-diagonal entries +1 along edges. In this convention negative definiteness and the inequality m(v) > -d(v) define a bad vertex. Most arguments are organized around whether a graph has no bad vertices, one bad vertex, or two bad vertices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem uses the opposite edge sign and a positive-definite form. Negating that form changes weights by m=-w and changes edge signs to +1, so the hypothesis w(v)<d(v) is exactly the paper's bad-vertex inequality m(v)>-d(v).

proof_techniques.name: Full-path characteristic-vector algorithm
proof_techniques.description: Section 3 replaces infinite characteristic-vector data by a finite vertex-firing algorithm. Starting from a characteristic vector satisfying m(v)+2 <= <K,v> <= -m(v), one repeatedly fires any vertex at the upper endpoint by replacing K with K+2PD[v]. A full path is successful when it reaches the opposite terminal window m(v) <= <L,v> <= -m(v)-2 and unsuccessful when some evaluation exceeds -m(v). Proposition 3.2 proves that successful full paths classify exactly the K+(G)-classes that are not equivalent to positive U multiples, and that the initial representative is unique.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After sign reversal, this gives a canonical finite dynamical system attached to the original weighted tree. A solver could try to encode reducibility or irreducibility of vertices in terms of whether certain firing paths can terminate successfully or must violate a bound.

proof_techniques.name: Leaf-removal recurrence for strict no-bad graphs
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.7 proves a determinant-type recurrence for the number of short characteristic vectors when m(v)<-d(v) at every vertex. Choosing a leaf v with neighbor w, it shows |S(G)| = -m(v)|S(G-v)| - |S(G-v-w)|. The proof hinges on the positive-U criterion and the inequality j <= d(w) < -m(w), where j counts how many selected vertices adjacent to w occur in a potential obstructing set A.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original graph is a tree, so leaf induction is natural. In the sign-reversed graph, nonbad vertices satisfy m(v)<=-d(v), and strict vertices satisfy the exact hypothesis of this recurrence; this may help isolate how the unique bad vertex forces a special local configuration.

proof_techniques.name: Positive-U obstruction via subsets of vertices
proof_techniques.description: A key combinatorial test used in Lemma 2.7 says that a characteristic vector in the initial window is equivalent to a positive U multiple exactly when there is a positive combination A of vertex Poincare duals with L·A+A·A>0; under strict no-bad inequalities the coefficients of A can be taken to be 0 or 1. This converts an equivalence problem into an inequality over subsets of vertices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The inequality L·A+A·A>0 is a purely lattice-theoretic condition. It may be adaptable to prove that if every vertex were reducible, then one could construct an impossible positive subset A or violate definiteness.

proof_techniques.name: Weight shift by adding a -1 leaf
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.5 relates G'(v), obtained by adding a -1 vertex adjacent to v, to G+1(v), obtained by increasing the weight at v by one. Algebraically, H+(G'(v)) and H+(G+1(v)) are isomorphic; geometrically, this is a handleslide/blow-up relation. The explicit map is controlled by the isolated -1 vertex formula Q(φ)(K,±(2m+1))=U^{m(m+1)/2}φ(K).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests comparing trees whose distinguished vertex weight differs by one, possibly after adjoining or removing a simple auxiliary vertex. Such a move may be useful in an induction on the defect d(v)-w(v) of the unique bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Exact-triangle induction around a distinguished vertex
proof_techniques.description: The main proof repeatedly compares three graphs: G'(v), G, and G-v. On the Floer side a surgery exact triangle often becomes a short exact sequence; on the combinatorial H+ side explicit maps A+ and B+ give a parallel sequence. Knowing the theorem for two of the graphs and proving injectivity/cancellation for A+ and B+ lets the authors prove it for the third by a diagram chase.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A pure lattice proof may be able to imitate this structure: remove the bad vertex, modify its weight, and compare the desired property across the resulting smaller or less bad trees.

proof_techniques.name: Filtration-maximal term prevents cancellation
proof_techniques.description: To prove A+ is injective, the paper puts a highest-degree filtration on T0+, then chooses a characteristic vector K where φ(K) has maximal filtration and, subject to that, maximal evaluation at v. The adjunction relation at the added -1 vertex shows that all other extensions either have lower filtration or contradict maximality, so a unique leading term survives in the sum defining A+(φ).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This lexicographic extremal-choice argument is independent in spirit of Floer theory. It can be useful whenever one sums or compares contributions from several extensions of a vector and needs to prove that a maximal contribution cannot cancel.

proof_techniques.name: Finite reduction of infinite characteristic data
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.3 shows that Ker(U^{n+1}) in H+(G) is dual to a quotient of K+(G) represented by the finite set ⋃_{i=0}^n U^i⊗B_{n-i}, where B_n is defined by the inequalities |<K,v>| <= -m(v)+2n. For n=0, Proposition 3.2 reduces this further to initial vectors admitting successful full paths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a strategy introduces characteristic covectors, this finite reduction can keep the argument combinatorial and bounded, rather than requiring control of all covectors in the lattice.

other_useful_info: The paper's Section 3 gives concrete full-path calculations for the E8 plumbing of the Poincare homology sphere and for Brieskorn spheres Σ(2,3,7) and Σ(3,5,7); these are useful examples of how the algorithm in Proposition 3.2 is executed. Corollary 1.5 emphasizes that maxima of K^2 over a Spin^c orbit for graphs with at most two bad vertices are achieved in a finite window |K·v| <= |m(v)|. The proof over Z, in Subsection 2.1, is mostly a sign-refinement of the F-coefficient proof: signs for cobordism maps are fixed using HF∞ isomorphisms for negative-definite cobordisms, and signs in A+ and B+ are chosen so paired terms in B+A+ cancel. The paper cites Ozsváth-Szabó [14] for the adjunction relations and Ozsváth-Szabó [12] for the surgery exact triangles used throughout.
why_relevant: Defines negative-definite weighted plumbing graphs and bad vertices; after sign reversal, the condition in the problem is the same bad-vertex condition. The paper's combinatorial handling of definite weighted trees with at most one bad vertex is directly relevant.

title: Heegaard Floer homology and alternating knots
overall_summary: Ozsváth and Szabó give a combinatorial description of the generators, filtration levels, and absolute gradings of the knot Floer complex associated to a decorated knot projection, using Kauffman states. For alternating knots and non-split alternating links, this description collapses the homology onto a single grading line determined by the Alexander polynomial and signature. The central combinatorial mechanism is the checkerboard or Tait graph dictionary: Kauffman states correspond to spanning trees of the black graph, and global equalities are proved by checking elementary transposition moves between states. For the original weighted-tree lattice problem, the paper is most useful as a source of graph-state encodings, local-to-global arguments via connected move graphs, canonical state constructions by finite exhaustion, and the quoted definite-intersection-form constraints involving characteristic vectors and correction terms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Kauffman state/spanning-tree correspondence, Section 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be given by a decorated planar projection with four-valent projection graph G, distinguished edge, and distinguished adjacent complementary regions A and B, with A chosen to be the unbounded region. A Kauffman state assigns to each crossing of G one of its four incident quadrants, such that distinct crossings are assigned quadrants lying in distinct complementary regions and no assigned quadrant lies in A or B. Chessboard-color the complementary regions of S^2 − G, and form the black graph whose vertices are the black regions and whose edges are the crossings of G, joining the two opposite black regions incident at that crossing. Then Kauffman states are in one-to-one correspondence with maximal subtrees, i.e. spanning trees, of the black graph: a state x is sent to the set of crossings at which x chooses a black quadrant, viewed as edges of the black graph.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a simple graph-theoretic description. A Kauffman state gives a bijection between the crossings of G and the non-distinguished complementary regions S^2 − G − A − B. In particular, among the black regions, every black region except the distinguished black one receives exactly one marker. Thus the crossings at which the marker is black give exactly one fewer edge than the number of black vertices. Planar duality with the white graph shows that these selected black edges cannot contain a cycle and cannot fail to span: a black cycle would separate white regions in a way incompatible with the distinct-region condition for the complementary white markers, and disconnectedness is dual to a white cycle. Conversely, a spanning tree of the black graph determines black markers on its edges and the dual complement determines the white markers, giving a unique Kauffman state.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2, Kauffman's Clock Theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a fixed decorated knot projection, any two distinct Kauffman states x and y can be connected by a finite sequence of transpositions. Here two states differ by a transposition if there are two crossings v1 and v2 and a straight path P along the knot projection from v1 to v2, not containing the distinguished edge, such that x and y agree at every crossing except v1 and v2, and at v1 and v2 the two states switch the two quadrant choices adjacent to the first and last edges of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is not reproduced in this paper; it is cited from Kauffman's Formal Knot Theory, Chapter 2. The paper uses the theorem as a connectivity statement for the graph of Kauffman states. Once a quantity is shown to transform in the desired way under a single transposition, the Clock Theorem promotes the local verification to all states.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2, Kauffman-state model for the classical knot Floer data
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an oriented knot in S^3, and choose a decorated planar projection of K. For each crossing v, let ε(v) be the sign of the crossing. The orientation of K singles out at v a quadrant pointed towards v, whose boundary contains the two incoming edges, and the opposite quadrant pointed away from v, whose boundary contains the two outgoing edges. For a Kauffman state x, define a local filtration contribution s(x,v) by 2 ε(v) s(x,v)=1 if x(v) is the pointed-towards quadrant, 2 ε(v) s(x,v)=−1 if x(v) is the pointed-away quadrant, and s(x,v)=0 otherwise. Define S(x)=sum_v s(x,v). Define a local grading contribution m(x,v) by m(x,v)=−ε(v) if x(v) is the pointed-away quadrant and m(x,v)=0 otherwise, and set M(x)=sum_v m(x,v). Then there is a Heegaard diagram for K such that the knot Floer complex widehat CFK(S^3,K) is freely generated by the Kauffman states of the decorated projection. If a generator corresponds to a state x, its filtration level is F(x)=S(x), and its absolute grading is gr(x)=M(x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The diagram is built directly from the projection graph G. Let Σ be the boundary of a regular neighborhood of G in S^3, oriented as the boundary of the complement. For every complementary region r except the unbounded region A, place an α-curve α_r following the boundary of r. For every crossing v, place a β-curve β_v locally as in Figure 4 of the paper. Add a meridian μ supported near the distinguished edge. The meridian meets only the α-curve corresponding to the other distinguished region B, so an intersection point in T_α ∩ T_β chooses, for every crossing β_v, one incident region other than A or B, with no region repeated. Thus generators are exactly Kauffman states. For the filtration, Lemma 2.3 proves that if two states differ by a transposition, then the difference of their Floer filtration levels equals the difference of the combinatorial sums S. By the Clock Theorem this gives S(x)=F(x)+c for a constant c independent of x. The constant is zero because the Kauffman state sum sum_x (±1) T^{S(x)} is the Conway-normalized Alexander polynomial, while the Euler characteristic of knot Floer homology gives the symmetrized Alexander polynomial with the normalization used to define F. For the absolute grading, Lemma 2.4 gives the corresponding equality of grading differences under any transposition, so M(x)=gr(x)+c' for a constant c'. Lemma 2.5 constructs a canonical state x0 for which all local terms m(x0,v) vanish. Sliding the β-curves forward across the reference point z but never across w gives a Heegaard diagram for S^3 with a unique intersection point corresponding to x0; that unique generator has absolute degree zero. Hence c'=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3, filtration change under a transposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x and y be Kauffman states of a decorated knot projection which differ by a transposition, and let the same letters denote the corresponding generators of the knot Floer complex in the Heegaard diagram constructed from the projection. Then F(x) − F(y) = S(x) − S(y), where F is the knot Floer filtration and S is the combinatorial filtration sum from Theorem 1.2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Orient the edge path P involved in the transposition according to the knot orientation. There are four cases, according to whether the path leaves or enters under-crossings or over-crossings at its two endpoints. In the case where the path goes from an under-crossing to an over-crossing, after possibly switching x and y one constructs a Whitney disk class φ from x to y whose domain has local multiplicities zero or one, with n_w(φ)=0 and n_z(φ)=1. The domain is a connected surface with boundary components coming from the meridian, from β-circles for intermediate vertices, and from the α-curves surrounding intermediate regions; its detailed topology is irrelevant for the filtration calculation. Thus F(x)−F(y)=n_z(φ)−n_w(φ)=1, and a direct local check of the four quadrants gives S(x)−S(y)=1. If both endpoints are under-crossings, there is a square domain, possibly with holes from intermediate crossings, disjoint from both basepoints w and z, so n_w=n_z=0 and both filtration differences vanish. The two remaining over/under endpoint cases are handled analogously.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4, absolute grading change under a transposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x and y be Kauffman states which differ by a transposition. For the ordered pair for which the positive transposition domain is a Whitney disk class φ from x to y with n_w(φ)=0, the absolute gradings satisfy gr(x) − gr(y)=1. In particular, a transposition changes absolute grading by exactly one in absolute value, with the sign determined by the orientation of the transposition domain.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The domains constructed for transpositions in Lemma 2.3 are topologically squares with finitely many handles attached and finitely many disks removed. Writing the corresponding intersection points as x={x1,...,xg} and y={y1,...,yg}, only two coordinates change; the remaining coordinates either lie in the handles, on the circular boundary components, or outside the domain. Coordinates in handles can be removed by destabilizing the Heegaard diagram, which does not change the Maslov index relevant to this disk. After these deletions the computation reduces to a Whitney disk whose domain is a square with finitely many disks removed. The paper computes the Maslov index of such a class to be μ(φ)=1 by comparing with an explicit Heegaard diagram for a connected sum of S^1 × S^2's, where the two generators are known to differ in grading by one. Since n_w(φ)=0 and gr(x)−gr(y)=μ(φ)−2 n_w(φ), the grading difference is one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5, existence and uniqueness of the canonical state
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a decorated oriented knot projection. Order the edges by traversing the knot from the distinguished edge. For a crossing v, let the edge after v be the last edge in this traversal whose closure contains v, and let the v-penultimate edge e_v be the edge immediately preceding it. Then there is a unique Kauffman state x0 such that, for every crossing v, the quadrant x0(v) is one of the two quadrants whose closure contains e_v.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let X0=A ∪ B be the union of the two distinguished regions. The proof inductively exhausts S^2 by regions X0 ⊂ X1 ⊂ ... ⊂ Xm=S^2 while defining x0 on crossings v1,...,vn. The induction maintains that x0 maps the chosen crossings bijectively to the regions in Xn − G − X0 and that each previously chosen v_i-penultimate edge lies in the interior of Xn. If Xn is not all of S^2, then because the graph is a knot projection, Xn has corners. Choose the last corner v_{n+1} with respect to the traversal order. Its penultimate edge has one adjacent quadrant in Xn and one adjacent quadrant in a new outside region R_{n+1}; otherwise there would be a later corner. This crossing was not previously chosen, since all earlier penultimate edges lie in the interior of Xn. Define x0(v_{n+1}) to be the outside quadrant in R_{n+1}, and set X_{n+1}=Xn ∪ R_{n+1}. At every step there is no choice, so the resulting state exists and is unique.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1, Gordon-Litherland signature formula for alternating projections
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a knot with an alternating projection G. Color the complementary regions in a chessboard pattern so that the under-first quadrants at crossings are white and the over-first quadrants are black, where a quadrant is over-first if, when its boundary is oriented as part of the plane, the first of the two crossing edges is the over-strand. With the paper's convention that the left-handed trefoil has signature +2, the signature of K is σ(K)=number of black regions in the planar projection − number of positive crossings − 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is not given in this paper; the result is cited as Theorem 6 of Gordon-Litherland and as interpreted by Lee. Ozsváth and Szabó use it as the bridge between the local Kauffman-state grading calculation and the classical signature. In the proof of Theorem 1.3, after summing local contributions over all crossings, the expression obtained is exactly the right-hand side of this formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3, knot Floer homology of alternating knots
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K ⊂ S^3 be an alternating knot. Write its symmetrized Alexander polynomial as ∆_K(T)=a_0 + sum_{s>0} a_s (T^s + T^{−s}). Let σ(K) be the knot signature with the convention that the left-handed trefoil has signature +2. Then for every integer s, widehat HFK(S^3,K,s) is supported entirely in absolute grading s + σ(K)/2, and widehat HFK(S^3,K,s) is isomorphic to Z^{|a_s|}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose an alternating decorated projection and use the Heegaard diagram from Theorem 1.2. A local inspection of the four possible quadrants at each crossing relates m(x,v)−s(x,v), the crossing sign, and whether the chosen quadrant is under-first or over-first. In an alternating diagram all over-first quadrants have the same checkerboard color, chosen black. Since a Kauffman state represents every black region except the distinguished black region exactly once, summing the local identity gives 2(M(x)−S(x)) = number of black regions − number of positive crossings − 1. By Theorem 3.1, this equals σ(K), hence every generator in filtration level S=s has absolute grading M=s+σ(K)/2. Therefore the differential in each fixed filtration level must vanish for grading reasons. The Euler characteristic of widehat HFK in filtration s is the coefficient a_s of the Alexander polynomial, so the rank of the group is |a_s| and it lies in the stated grading.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Skein exact sequence for link Floer homology, used in Section 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose L_+ is an oriented link projection with a positive crossing p whose two strands belong to the same component of L_+. Let L_- be obtained by changing p to a negative crossing, and let L_0 be the oriented resolution at p. Then for each filtration level s there is a long exact sequence ... → widehat HFK(L_-,s) → widehat HFK(L_0,s) → widehat HFK(L_+,s) → ... . In the grading convention used in the paper, the first two maps mentioned drop absolute grading by 1/2, and the remaining map is non-increasing in absolute grading.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This exact sequence is recalled from the authors' earlier work on link Floer homology and is not proved in this paper. In Section 4 it is combined with the alternating-knot calculation and signature shifts under local skein modifications to induct on the number of link components.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1, link Floer homology of non-split alternating links
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L ⊂ S^3 be a non-split oriented alternating link with n components, Alexander-Conway polynomial ∆_L, and signature σ(L). Write (T^{−1/2} − T^{1/2})^{n−1} ∆_L(T) = a_0 + sum_{s>0} a_s (T^s + T^{−s}). Then for every integer s, widehat HFK(S^3,L,s) is supported entirely in absolute grading s + σ(L)/2, and widehat HFK(S^3,L,s) is isomorphic to Z^{|a_s|}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the number of components. The base case is Theorem 1.3 for knots. For the inductive step, choose a crossing p where two different strands of L meet. The paper constructs two alternating links L_- and L_+ with one additional crossing q whose oriented resolution at q is L. According to the sign of p, the changes alter either the number of positive crossings or the number of black regions in a controlled way. Applying the Gordon-Litherland signature formula gives σ(L_-)−1=σ(L)=σ(L_+)+1. The skein exact sequence then relates the groups of L_-, L, and L_+. By the induction hypothesis, the groups for L_- and L_+ lie on grading lines shifted by exactly the amounts dictated by the maps in the exact sequence. Exactness forces the group for L to lie on the line s+σ(L)/2. Finally, the Euler characteristic calculation for link Floer homology identifies the rank with the absolute value of the coefficient a_s.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Quoted correction-term inequality and Elkies lattice theorem, Section 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper recalls two external definite-lattice constraints. First, from Ozsváth-Szabó's absolutely graded Floer theory: if Y is an integer homology three-sphere, W is a smooth negative-definite four-manifold with boundary Y, and ξ is any characteristic vector for the intersection form on H_2(W;Z), meaning Q(ξ,v) ≡ Q(v,v) mod 2 for every v, then ξ^2 + rk H_2(W;Z) ≤ 4 d(Y), where d(Y) is the Heegaard Floer correction term. Second, from Elkies: for any negative-definite unimodular integral lattice V, max over characteristic vectors ξ of ξ^2 + rk(V) is at least 0, with equality if and only if V is diagonalizable over Z.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Neither result is proved in this paper. They are quoted to explain the role of correction terms as obstructions to exotic definite intersection forms bounding a fixed three-manifold. The inequality bounds every characteristic vector in a negative-definite bounding form by 4d(Y), while Elkies' theorem says that, for unimodular negative-definite forms, the maximum characteristic-vector defect ξ^2+rk(V) detects diagonalizability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.5, correction term of +1 surgery on an alternating knot
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K ⊂ S^3 be an alternating knot. Then the Heegaard Floer correction term of +1 surgery on K is d(S^3_1(K)) = 2 min(0, −ceil(−σ(K)/4)), where σ(K) is the signature in the paper's convention.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses the general relation d(S^3_1(K)) = d_{1/2}(S^3_0(K)) − 1/2. When σ(K) ≤ 0, Theorem 1.4 gives the relevant d_{1/2} term for zero-surgery directly. When σ(K)>0, the knot is reflected: if r(K) is the reflection, then d_{1/2}(S^3_0(K)) = −d_{−1/2}(S^3_0(r(K))) = 1/2, again using Theorem 1.4 for the reflected knot. Substituting gives the displayed closed formula.

proof_techniques.name: Checkerboard graph encoding by Kauffman states
proof_techniques.description: The paper converts a decorated planar knot projection into a black Tait graph whose vertices are black regions and whose edges are crossings. Kauffman states, originally assignments of quadrants to crossings avoiding two distinguished regions, become spanning trees of this graph. This replaces a planar local-choice problem by a finite graph-theoretic object with a root or distinguished region.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original object is already a finite tree with a lattice form attached. The Tait-graph viewpoint suggests rooting or distinguishing a vertex or region and translating lattice questions into combinatorics of subtrees, leaves, and local choices.

proof_techniques.name: Local-to-global reduction via transposition connectivity
proof_techniques.description: The Clock Theorem says the space of Kauffman states is connected under elementary transpositions supported along a path between two crossings. Ozsváth and Szabó prove identities for filtration and grading by checking the change under a single transposition, then use connectivity to conclude that the identities hold for all states up to a constant, which is fixed by normalization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a tree lattice, one can try to define a graph of possible decompositions, sign assignments, or support choices and prove it is connected under elementary local moves. If irreducibility or reducibility changes can be controlled locally along an edge or path, a global contradiction may follow from a single normalized case.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical state by finite exhaustion
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.5 constructs a unique canonical Kauffman state by starting from the two distinguished regions and repeatedly attaching an outside region at the last available corner. The proof is a deterministic induction on finitely many regions, maintaining that previously chosen penultimate edges lie in the interior of the exhausted set.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful template for proofs on finite trees: start from a distinguished location, peel off or attach one extremal piece at a time, and maintain a local invariant. The hypothesis that exactly one vertex is bad may be suited to a similar rooted pruning argument.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction of global domains to standard local pieces
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 2.4, transposition domains may have handles and holes, but the Maslov-index calculation ignores irrelevant handles by destabilization and reduces to a square with disks removed. The essential numerical effect of a complicated global move is therefore computed from a standard local model.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a lattice decomposition u=a+b, the support of a and b in a tree may have extraneous branches. A parallel strategy would reduce a putative reducibility configuration to a minimal connected support, often a path or local star, where dot products can be computed explicitly.

proof_techniques.name: Summing local contributions to obtain a global invariant
proof_techniques.description: The alternating-knot calculation assigns local quantities s(x,v) and m(x,v) at each crossing. A local identity relating these quantities to crossing signs and checkerboard colors is summed over all crossings; because a state marks every black region except one, the sum becomes a global count of black regions and positive crossings, then the Gordon-Litherland formula identifies it with the signature.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The tree-lattice condition w(v)<d(v) is a local imbalance between weight and valence. A proof might assign local surplus or deficit terms to vertices and sum them over a support subtree to force a vertex satisfying the desired irreducibility property.

proof_techniques.name: Definite-form constraints through characteristic vectors
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls that Heegaard Floer correction terms bound characteristic vectors in negative-definite four-manifold intersection forms, and pairs this with Elkies' theorem for unimodular definite lattices. This is a standard way to convert topology of a boundary three-manifold into arithmetic restrictions on a definite lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After reversing sign, the positive-definite tree lattice in the original problem can be viewed as a negative-definite graph lattice. Characteristic-vector inequalities or Elkies-type extremal arguments may help force structural constraints, such as the existence of a basis vertex that cannot split with nonnegative pairing.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not formulate graph-lattice irreducibility, but it does supply several adjacent tools and references. Kauffman [16] is cited for the Clock Theorem, and Kauffman [17] for the Alexander polynomial state-sum normalization. Gordon-Litherland [15] and Lee [19] are cited for the signature formula of alternating diagrams, which is the likely source for checkerboard-surface intersection forms. Ozsváth-Szabó [28] is cited for correction-term inequalities for definite four-manifolds with boundary, and Elkies [8] for the characteristic-vector characterization of diagonal definite unimodular lattices.
why_relevant: Uses Goeritz matrices of alternating diagrams as definite graph lattices and relates them to sharp four-manifolds. It is a standard source for the definite-lattice viewpoint behind many later graph-lattice irreducibility arguments.

title: On the Ozsváth–Szabó invariant of negative definite plumbed 3-manifolds
overall_summary: The paper studies connected negative definite plumbing trees Γ with lattice L generated by the vertices, diagonal entries e_j, and off-diagonal entries 1 along edges. Its main topological result is a computation of Heegaard Floer homology for almost-rational plumbing graphs via graded roots, but the most transferable material is the combinatorial lattice theory developed en route: anti-nef cycles, canonical characteristic elements, χ-functions, Artin rationality, and Laufer computation sequences. A central theme is that if all but at most one vertex satisfy the rationality-type inequality −e_j ≥ δ_j, then the graph is almost-rational and the rational parts of the graph are controlled by monotone computation sequences. For the original problem, after reversing the sign of the positive definite form, the hypothesis that one vertex has weight less than degree is exactly the paper’s one-bad-vertex condition, so the anti-nef minimization and Laufer-sequence arguments are the most relevant extraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1 dual-basis sign observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected plumbing graph whose bilinear form B on the free abelian group L with basis {b_j}_{j∈J} is negative definite, with B(b_j,b_j)=e_j and B(b_i,b_j)=1 when vertices i and j are adjacent and 0 otherwise. Let L' = Hom_Z(L,Z), identify L' with a sublattice of L⊗Q via B, and let {g_j}_{j∈J} be the dual basis, so B(g_j,b_i)=δ_{ij}. Then −g_j is an effective rational cycle for every j∈J; equivalently, every entry of the inverse matrix B^{-1} is nonpositive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records this as clear from negative definiteness. In the notation later used in the paper, x=−g_j satisfies B(x,b_i)=−δ_{ij}≤0 for every i, so x lies in the anti-nef cone S_Q={y∈L⊗Q : B(y,b_i)≤0 for all i}. Theorem 6.1(a) proves that every element of this cone is coefficientwise nonnegative. Thus −g_j≥0. Matrix-theoretically this is the standard inverse-sign property for a negative definite tree intersection matrix with nonnegative off-diagonal entries.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph with lattice L and let k be any characteristic element, meaning k∈L' and k(x)+B(x,x) is even for every x∈L. Define χ_k:L→Z by χ_k(x)=−(k(x)+B(x,x))/2. For each n∈Z form a one-dimensional simplicial complex L_{k,≤n} whose vertices are lattice points x∈L with χ_k(x)≤n and whose edges join x to x+b_j whenever both endpoints have χ_k≤n. Let R_k have as vertices the connected components of all L_{k,≤n}, with an edge from a component at level n to a containing component at level n+1. Then R_k, graded by the level n, is a graded root: adjacent levels differ by 1, local branching is upward, each level has finitely many vertices, the grading is bounded below, and for n sufficiently large there is exactly one component.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction gives edges only between adjacent levels. The no-downward-branching condition follows because if a component C_u at a lower level lay inside two distinct higher-level components C_v and C_w, then those two higher-level components would intersect in the nonempty connected set C_u, impossible. Negative definiteness makes χ_k proper and bounded below, so each sublevel has finitely many lattice points and the level sets of components are finite. For eventual connectedness, the paper introduces the finite set Y of lattice points x such that χ_k(x)≤χ_k(x±b_j) for every basis element b_j; equivalently, x satisfies finitely many inequalities −χ_k(−b_j)≤B(x,b_j)≤χ_k(b_j). This set is finite by nondegeneracy and negative definiteness. Choose one y_*∈Y and fixed lattice paths from y_* to every other y∈Y, and let n_0 be the maximum χ_k-value along these paths. Any point not in Y has a neighboring point with no larger χ_k, and repeated descent reaches Y; hence every point in L_{k,≤n} for n≥n_0 connects to y_* inside L_{k,≤n}. Thus high sublevels are connected.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph with lattice L and rational extension L_Q=L⊗Q. Define the anti-nef cone S_Q={x∈L_Q : B(x,b_j)≤0 for every vertex j}. For any coset l'+L⊂L_Q, the intersection (l'+L)∩S_Q has a unique coefficientwise minimal element, denoted l'_[k] in the paper when the coset corresponds to a spin-c structure [k].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence comes from choosing a sufficiently large effective integral cycle y with B(y,b_j)<0 for all j, so l'+Ny lies in S_Q for N large. For uniqueness and minimality, the key closure property is that the coordinatewise minimum of two anti-nef representatives in the same coset is again anti-nef. If l'+l_1 and l'+l_2 are in S_Q, set l=min(l_1,l_2) coefficientwise and write x_i=l_i−l≥0. The supports of x_1 and x_2 are disjoint. For any vertex b_j, at least one of x_1,x_2 has zero coefficient at b_j; say b_j is not in the support of x_1. Then B(l'+l,b_j)=B(l'+l_1,b_j)−B(x_1,b_j)≤0, because B(l'+l_1,b_j)≤0 and B(x_1,b_j)≥0 when x_1 is effective and does not contain b_j in its support. Therefore l'+l∈S_Q. Taking repeated coordinatewise minima yields a unique minimal element.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a spin-c class [k] represented as K+2(l'+L), where K is the canonical characteristic element defined by K(b_j)=−e_j−2. Let l'_[k] be the unique minimal element of (l'+L)∩S_Q from Lemma 5.4, and put k_r=K+2l'_[k]. Then: (a) B(l'_[k],b_j)≥b_j^2+1 for every vertex j; (b) k_r(x)≥B(x,x) for every effective integral cycle x≥0; and (c) χ_{k_r}(−x)≥0 for every effective integral cycle x≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), if B(l'_[k],b_{j0})≤b_{j0}^2 for some j0, then l'_[k]−b_{j0} remains in the same coset and is still anti-nef: the j0-pairing becomes B(l'_[k],b_{j0})−b_{j0}^2≤0, and all other pairings do not increase because off-diagonal intersections are nonnegative. This contradicts minimality of l'_[k]. For (b), first (a) implies k_r(b_j)=2B(l'_[k],b_j)−b_j^2−2≥b_j^2 for each basis vector. If some effective x>0 had k_r(x)<B(x,x), choose a coefficientwise minimal such x. Then for each b_j in the support of x, minimality gives k_r(x−b_j)≥B(x−b_j,x−b_j), while k_r(x)<B(x,x). Subtracting yields B(l'_[k]−x,b_j)≤0 for every b_j in the support. For b_j outside the support, B(l'_[k]−x,b_j)≤0 follows from l'_[k]∈S_Q and x≥0. Hence l'_[k]−x∈(l'_[k]+L)∩S_Q, contradicting minimality. Part (c) is the identity 2χ_{k_r}(−x)=k_r(x)−B(x,x), together with (b).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph. For a spin-c class [k], let l'_[k] be the minimal anti-nef representative from Lemma 5.4, let k_r=K+2l'_[k], and define S_[k]={x∈L : B(x+l'_[k],b_j)≤0 for every j}. Let L_{k,≤n} be the χ_k-sublevel complex, and let L^s_{k,≤n} be the subcomplex spanned by L_{k,≤n}∩S_[k]. Then: (a) if x∈S_[k], then x≥0 coefficientwise; (b) the inclusion L^s_{k,≤n}→L_{k,≤n} induces a surjection on connected components for every n; (c) for k=K, the connected component C_0 of L_{K,≤0} containing 0 contains no nonzero effective cycle, and every nonzero cycle in C_0 is coefficientwise negative and has χ=0; (d) for k=K, the complex L_{K,≤n} is connected for every n≥1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), write x=x_1−x_2 with x_1≥0, x_2≥0, and disjoint supports. If b_j is in the support of x_2, then B(l'_[k]−x_2,b_j)≤B(l'_[k]+x,b_j)≤0; if b_j is not in the support of x_2, then B(l'_[k]−x_2,b_j)≤B(l'_[k],b_j)≤0. Thus l'_[k]−x_2 lies in the same coset and in S_Q, so minimality of l'_[k] forces x_2=0. For (b), if x is not in S_[k], choose j with B(x+l'_[k],b_j)≥1. Then χ_k(x+b_j)≤χ_k(x). Repeating this step gives an increasing sequence of cycles with nonincreasing χ_k. Properness from negative definiteness forces termination, and the endpoint lies in S_[k], so each component of L_{k,≤n} meets L^s_{k,≤n}. For (c), first note that b_j is never in L_{K,≤0}, while −b_j may be if e_j=−1. Then induct along paths in C_0 starting from 0: if x<0 and χ(x)=0, and x±b_j also lies in C_0, the case x+b_j with b_j outside the support would give χ(x+b_j)=1+B(−x,b_j)≥1, impossible in L_{K,≤0}. Thus negativity is preserved, and Proposition 5.7(c) forces χ=0. For (d), by (a) and (b) it is enough to connect every effective x with χ(x)≤n to 0 inside L_{K,≤n}. Two reduction facts are proved. If x>0 and χ(x)>0, some b_j in the support satisfies χ(x−b_j)≤χ(x); otherwise B(b_j,x−b_j)>1 for all support vertices, implying B(K+x,x)≥0 and χ(x)≤0, contradiction. If x>0 and χ(x)≤0, some support vertex satisfies χ(x−b_j)≤1; otherwise, writing c=χ(x)≤0 and N=sum of coefficients of x, the inequalities χ(x−b_j)>1 imply B(K+x,x)+cN>0, equivalently c(N−2)>0, impossible. These steps lower coefficients while staying within the sublevel n whenever n≥1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 6.2 Artin rationality criterion and closure facts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a connected negative definite plumbing graph Γ with canonical χ(x)=−(K(x)+B(x,x))/2, the graph is called rational if χ(x)≥1 for every nonzero effective integral cycle x>0. This is Artin’s topological characterization of resolution graphs of rational singularities. The paper also records that any plumbing graph with −e_j≥δ_j for every vertex j is rational, where δ_j is the valency of j, and that the class of rational graphs is closed under taking subgraphs and under decreasing Euler decorations e_j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The rationality criterion is cited from Artin. For the sufficient condition −e_j≥δ_j, the cycle sum_j b_j is anti-nef because B(sum_i b_i,b_j)=e_j+δ_j≤0. It is the Artin fundamental cycle, and a direct computation gives χ of this fundamental cycle equal to 1; by Artin’s criterion this implies rationality. Closure under subgraphs and decreasing Euler numbers is stated in the paper as a standard consequence: deleting vertices or making diagonal entries more negative preserves the inequalities and, more generally, preserves the positivity of χ on nonzero effective cycles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph whose plumbed 3-manifold is a rational homology sphere, let K be the canonical characteristic element, and let (R_can,χ_can) be the graded root associated with χ=χ_K. Then the following conditions are equivalent: Γ is rational; χ_can^{-1}(0) has exactly one element; R_can is the standard ray R_0; R_can is a standard ray R_m for some integer m; for every characteristic element k, R_k is a standard ray R_{m_k}; equivalently H(R_can)=T^+_0, equivalently H(R_can)=T^+_m for some m, equivalently H(R_k)=T^+_{m_k} for all k. Moreover, if Γ is rational and k=K+2l' is any characteristic element, then min_x χ_k(x)=χ(l'_[k])−χ(l')=χ_k(l'_[k]−l')≤0, where l'_[k] is the minimal anti-nef representative of the coset l'+L; in particular, for the distinguished representative k_r=K+2l'_[k], one has min χ_{k_r}=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Γ is rational, then χ(x)≥1 for every effective x>0. By Theorem 6.1(a,b), every component of a χ-sublevel has an effective anti-nef representative, and the only effective point with χ≤0 is 0; Theorem 6.1(c,d) then force the canonical graded root to be exactly R_0. The implications from a standard ray to the corresponding H-module statements are formal from the definition of H(R). Conversely, if R_can is any standard ray, then χ_can^{-1}(0) has only the component containing 0. Thus L_{K,≤0} equals the component C_0 from Theorem 6.1(c), which contains no nonzero effective cycle, so χ(x)>0 for every x>0 and the graph is rational by Artin’s criterion. For the statement about arbitrary k, it is enough, using the shift relation between representatives in a fixed spin-c class, to take k=K+2l'_[k]. For any effective x>0, if no support vertex b_j satisfied χ_k(x−b_j)≤χ_k(x), then B(x+l'_[k],b_j)>1+b_j^2 for every support vertex. Since B(l'_[k],b_j)≤0, this gives B(K+x,b_j)≥0 on the support, hence χ(x)≤0, contradicting rationality. Therefore every effective x can be reduced to 0 through nonincreasing χ_k, and Theorem 6.1(b) handles non-effective points; hence min χ_k=0 for the distinguished representative. The formula for general l' follows from χ_k(x)=χ(l'+x)−χ(l') and the definition of l'_[k].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a connected negative definite plumbing graph Γ, a spin-c class [k], its distinguished representative k_r=K+2l'_[k], and S_[k]={x∈L : B(x+l'_[k],b_j)≤0 for every j}. For every x∈L there exists a unique coefficientwise minimal y=y(x)∈L such that x≤y and y∈S_[k].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence is obtained by choosing an effective cycle y_0>0 with B(y_0,b_j)<0 for every j and taking a large multiple above x; adding enough y_0 makes all pairings B(x+Ny_0+l'_[k],b_j) nonpositive. For uniqueness, use the same coordinatewise-minimum argument as Lemma 5.4: if y_1,y_2∈S_[k], then min(y_1,y_2) is again in S_[k]. Hence the set of all anti-nef cycles above x is closed under coordinatewise minimum and has a unique minimal element.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.4, generalized Laufer algorithm
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With Γ, [k], k_r, l'_[k], and S_[k] as in Lemma 7.3, fix x∈L. Define a sequence x_0,x_1,... as follows: x_0=x; if x_l is not in S_[k], choose a vertex j with B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j)>0 and set x_{l+1}=x_l+b_j; stop when x_l∈S_[k]. Then the algorithm terminates at the minimal element y(x) from Lemma 7.3. Moreover χ_{k_r}(x_{l+1})≤χ_{k_r}(x_l) at every step.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let y(x) be the minimal anti-nef cycle above x. The proof shows inductively that x_l≤y(x) until termination. If x_l≤y(x) and the algorithm chooses b_j, then the coefficient of b_j in x_l must be strictly smaller than in y(x); otherwise b_j is not in the support of y(x)−x_l, and B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j)=B(y(x)+l'_[k],b_j)−B(y(x)−x_l,b_j)≤0, contradicting the choice of j. Thus x_l+b_j≤y(x). Since the sequence is monotone increasing and bounded above by y(x), it terminates, and minimality forces the terminal point to equal y(x). The χ-change is χ_{k_r}(x_l+b_j)−χ_{k_r}(x_l)=1−B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j), which is ≤0 because the chosen pairing is a positive integer.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 7.5, Artin fundamental cycle and rational Laufer sequences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For k=K and starting point x=b_j, the endpoint y(x) of the generalized Laufer algorithm is Artin’s fundamental cycle x_min, the minimal nonzero effective cycle satisfying B(x_min,b_i)≤0 for every i. If Γ is rational, then any computation sequence from a basis element b_j to x_min as in Lemma 7.4 has χ constantly equal to 1; equivalently, at every step x_{l+1}=x_l+b_{j(l)} one has B(x_l,b_{j(l)})=1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first assertion is the definition of the fundamental cycle in this language: start with any vertex and add vertices with positive intersection until all intersections are nonpositive. For a rational graph, Artin’s criterion gives χ(x)>0 for every effective x>0, and in particular χ(b_j)=1 and χ(x_min)=1. Lemma 7.4 gives χ(x_{l+1})≤χ(x_l) along the sequence. Since all intermediate cycles are nonzero effective and the endpoints have χ=1, all inequalities must be equalities. The identity χ(x_l+b_{j(l)})−χ(x_l)=1−B(x_l,b_{j(l)}) then gives B(x_l,b_{j(l)})=1 at every step.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a distinguished vertex b_0 and write J={0}∪J*. For a spin-c class [k] with distinguished representative k_r=K+2l'_[k], and for each integer i≥0, there is a unique cycle x(i)∈L with the following properties: the coefficient m_0(x(i)) of b_0 is i; B(x(i)+l'_[k],b_j)≤0 for every j∈J*; and x(i) is coefficientwise minimal among cycles satisfying these two properties. Moreover x(i)≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence is established by taking a sufficiently large anti-nef cycle above b_0, multiplying it so that rational denominators in l'_[k] disappear, and then subtracting a suitable multiple of b_0 to force the b_0-coefficient to equal i while preserving the nonpositive pairings against vertices in J*. Uniqueness again follows from coordinatewise-minimum closure, now restricted to cycles with fixed b_0-coefficient. To prove x(i)≥0, write x(i)=x_1−x_2 with x_1,x_2≥0 and disjoint supports. Since the b_0-coefficient is i≥0, the negative part x_2 has no b_0 component. For j∈J*, if b_j is outside the support of x_1, then B(l'_[k]−x_2,b_j)≤B(l'_[k]+x(i),b_j)≤0; if b_j is in the support of x_1, then B(l'_[k]−x_2,b_j)≤B(l'_[k],b_j)≤0. The b_0-pairing is also nonpositive, so l'_[k]−x_2∈S_Q. Minimality of l'_[k] forces x_2=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 7.7 and 7.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Lemma 7.6, there is a computation sequence connecting x(i) to x(i+1): start with x_0=x(i), set x_1=x(i)+b_0, and then, while some j∈J* has B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j)>0, set x_{l+1}=x_l+b_j; stop when all pairings against J* are nonpositive. The endpoint is x(i+1). For all steps after the initial addition of b_0, χ_{k_r} is nonincreasing. The initial change is χ_{k_r}(x(i)+b_0)−χ_{k_r}(x(i))=1−B(x(i)+l'_[k],b_0), so χ increases at the initial step exactly when x(i) lies in the full anti-nef set S_[k]. More generally, if x has b_0-coefficient i and x≤x(i), then applying the same algorithm using only vertices in J* terminates at x(i) and χ_{k_r} is nonincreasing throughout.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof repeats Lemma 7.4 with the coefficient of b_0 fixed after the first step. The inductive invariant is that every intermediate cycle stays below the minimal cycle x(i+1), or below x(i) in the generalized version. If the chosen vertex b_j had already reached its coefficient in the target minimal cycle, the nonpositive target pairing and nonnegative off-diagonal intersections would contradict B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j)>0. Hence the addition is allowed and remains below the target. Termination follows from monotonicity and boundedness. The χ formula is again χ(x_l+b_j)−χ(x_l)=1−B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j), and for j∈J* the chosen positive pairing makes this nonpositive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 8.1 and Examples 8.2, almost-rational graphs
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A connected negative definite plumbing tree Γ is called almost-rational, or AR, if there is a vertex j_0 such that replacing its decoration e_{j0} by some smaller or equal integer e'_{j0}≤e_{j0} produces a rational graph. Rational graphs are AR. Weakly elliptic graphs are AR. Every star-shaped negative definite graph is AR. More generally, the paper notes that the graphs considered by Ozsváth and Szabó with at most one vertex at which the inequality −e_j≥δ_j fails are AR: decrease the decoration at that exceptional vertex until all vertices satisfy −e_j≥δ_j, which is a sufficient condition for rationality. The class of AR graphs is closed under taking subgraphs and under decreasing Euler decorations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a graph with at most one bad vertex, choose that vertex as j_0. Decreasing e_{j0} makes −e_{j0} as large as needed, so eventually every vertex satisfies −e_j≥δ_j. Section 6.2 states that this condition implies rationality, hence the original graph is AR. Star-shaped graphs are handled similarly after blowing down irrelevant −1 vertices: take the central vertex and make its decoration sufficiently negative. Closure under subgraphs and decreasing decorations follows because rational graphs have the same closure properties, and the same distinguished vertex or its induced version can be used.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume Γ is an almost-rational graph with distinguished vertex b_0, and fix a spin-c class [k] with distinguished representative k_r=K+2l'_[k]. Let x(i) be the minimal cycles from Lemma 7.6. Then for every i≥0: (a) if x∈L has b_0-coefficient i, then χ_{k_r}(x)≥χ_{k_r}(x(i)); (b) if y>x(i) and y has the same b_0-coefficient i, then there exists a vertex b_j in the support of y−x(i), with j∈J*, such that χ_{k_r}(y−b_j)≤χ_{k_r}(y); (c) in any computation sequence connecting x(i) to x(i+1) as in Lemma 7.7, every step after the initial b_0-step has B(x_l+l'_[k],b_{j(l)})=1, equivalently χ_{k_r}(x_{l+1})=χ_{k_r}(x_l). In particular χ_{k_r}(x(i)+b_0)=χ_{k_r}(x(i+1)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), write x=x(i)−y_1+y_2 where y_1,y_2≥0, neither contains b_0, and their supports are disjoint. Expanding χ gives χ_k(x)=χ_k(x(i)−y_1)+χ(y_2)+B(y_1,y_2)−B(x(i)+l'_[k],y_2). The last two terms are nonnegative: B(y_1,y_2)≥0 by disjoint support and nonnegative edge intersections, and −B(x(i)+l'_[k],y_2)≥0 by the defining inequalities for x(i). The support of y_2 lies in the rational graph obtained after modifying the distinguished vertex, or in a rational subgraph, so χ(y_2)≥0. Lemma 7.8 then gives χ_k(x(i)−y_1)≥χ_k(x(i)). For (b), suppose every b_j in the support of y−x(i) gave χ_k(y−b_j)>χ_k(y). This is equivalent to B(y+l'_[k],b_j)≥2+b_j^2. Since B(x(i)+l'_[k],b_j)≤0, one obtains B(y−x(i),b_j)≥2+b_j^2, or B(y−x(i)+K,b_j)≥0, on the support. Summing over the effective cycle y−x(i) gives χ(y−x(i))≤0, contradicting rationality of the supporting subgraph. For (c), let z_l=x_l−x(i) along the computation sequence. For l>0 the chosen vertex lies in J*, and B(z_l,b_{j(l)})=B(x_l+l'_[k],b_{j(l)})−B(x(i)+l'_[k],b_{j(l)})>0. These intersection numbers are unchanged when the distinguished decoration is decreased to make the graph rational. In the resulting rational graph, the sequence can be completed to a Laufer sequence for the Artin fundamental cycle, so Remark 7.5 forces B(z_l,b_{j(l)})=1. The displayed identity then gives B(x_l+l'_[k],b_{j(l)})=1 and χ equality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.2 and Theorem 9.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be an almost-rational graph with distinguished vertex b_0, fix [k] and k_r=K+2l'_[k], and let (R_k,χ_k) be the graded root associated with χ_k. If v is a local minimum vertex of R_k and C_v is the corresponding connected component of a χ_k-sublevel complex, then C_v has a unique coefficientwise maximal element x_v, this element lies in S_[k], and if i_v is its b_0-coefficient, then the minimal slice cycle x(i_v) lies in C_v. Consequently, if τ(i)=χ_k(x(i)) for i≥0, then τ is eventually nondecreasing, the graded root R_τ built from the sequence τ is well-defined, and (R_k,χ_k) is isomorphic to (R_τ,χ_τ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 9.2, local minimality of v implies that all lattice points in C_v have the same χ-value. If x, x+b_i, and x+b_j lie in C_v with i≠j, then direct computation gives χ_k(x+b_i+b_j)=χ_k(x)−B(b_i,b_j)≤χ_k(x), so local minimality forces x+b_i+b_j also into C_v. This replacement operation rules out two distinct maximal elements and gives uniqueness of x_v. Since χ strictly increases when adding any b_j to x_v, the formula χ_k(x_v+b_j)−χ_k(x_v)=1−B(x_v+l'_[k],b_j) implies B(x_v+l'_[k],b_j)≤0, hence x_v∈S_[k]. The minimality defining x(i_v) gives x(i_v)≤x_v; Lemma 9.1(b) constructs a path from x(i_v) upward to x_v along which χ does not increase, and local minimality forces it to stay in C_v. For Theorem 9.3, local minima of R_k inject into the sequence of slice minimizers x(i), and every strict down-up pattern in τ gives a local minimum. Since R_k has finitely many local minima, τ is eventually nondecreasing. The same argument identifies local minima of R_k and R_τ with the same χ-values. For two local minima with indices i_u<i_v, any path between the corresponding cycles must pass through a cycle with b_0-coefficient i for each intermediate i, and Lemma 9.1(a) shows the maximal χ along such a path is at least max_{i_u≤i≤i_v}χ_k(x(i)); the concatenated computation sequences from Lemma 7.7 and Lemma 9.1(c) realize this bound. Thus the join levels in the two graded roots agree, giving an isomorphism.

proof_techniques.name: Anti-nef cone and coordinatewise-min closure
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly studies the anti-nef cone S_Q={x : B(x,b_j)≤0 for all j}. A fundamental device is that within a fixed coset l'+L, the coordinatewise minimum of two anti-nef representatives is again anti-nef, because off-diagonal intersections are nonnegative and the supports of the two positive differences from the minimum are disjoint. This gives unique minimal anti-nef representatives and unique minimal cycles with prescribed coefficient at a distinguished vertex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After sign reversal, the original positive definite tree has the same matrix pattern as −B. Minimal anti-nef representatives can be used to choose canonical cycles or extremal decompositions, potentially forcing a vertex with an indecomposability property.

proof_techniques.name: Laufer computation sequence
proof_techniques.description: Given a cycle not satisfying anti-nef inequalities, the paper adds a vertex b_j with positive pairing B(x+l',b_j)>0. The key identity χ(x+b_j)−χ(x)=1−B(x+l',b_j) shows that χ does not increase. Negative definiteness prevents infinite descent inside a sublevel, and minimality arguments show that the process stops at the unique minimal anti-nef cycle above the starting point.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a systematic way to turn local intersection inequalities into global combinatorial conclusions. In the original problem, if one assumes all vertices decompose, analogous sequences may help build a forbidden effective cycle or a contradiction to positive definiteness.

proof_techniques.name: Rational graph control via Artin’s criterion
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Artin’s criterion that a negative definite graph is rational exactly when χ(x)≥1 for every nonzero effective cycle x. The easy sufficient condition −e_j≥δ_j at every vertex makes the sum of all vertices anti-nef and gives a rational graph. On rational graphs, Laufer sequences from a vertex to the fundamental cycle have χ identically 1, forcing every added vertex to have intersection exactly 1 with the current cycle.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original graph has a unique bad vertex, then deleting or isolating that vertex leaves pieces satisfying the rationality inequality after sign reversal. The exact-intersection property on those pieces is a strong combinatorial constraint likely useful for locating an irreducible vertex.

proof_techniques.name: One-bad-vertex isolation through almost-rational graphs
proof_techniques.description: An almost-rational graph is one that becomes rational after decreasing the decoration at a single distinguished vertex. The paper observes that graphs with at most one failure of −e_j≥δ_j are almost-rational, because making the exceptional decoration sufficiently negative forces the sufficient rationality inequalities everywhere. This isolates all nonrational behavior at one vertex and lets the rational complement control computation sequences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis, under the sign convention B=−(original form), is exactly a one-bad-vertex hypothesis. Thus the proof can try to distinguish that vertex and exploit rationality of the rest rather than analyze the whole tree uniformly.

proof_techniques.name: Fixed-coefficient slice minimization
proof_techniques.description: With a distinguished vertex b_0, the paper defines x(i) as the minimal cycle whose b_0-coefficient is i and whose pairings with all other vertices are nonpositive. In almost-rational graphs, x(i) minimizes χ among all cycles with that b_0-coefficient. Any larger cycle in the same slice can be reduced by subtracting a non-distinguished vertex without increasing χ. This converts a multidimensional lattice problem into a one-dimensional sequence indexed by i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a tree with one exceptional vertex, fixing the coefficient of that vertex may reduce potential decompositions or extremal cycles to a controlled family on rational slices. This can bypass the difficulty created by the single bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Sublevel-set and graded-root viewpoint
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes the topology of χ-sublevel sets L_{k,≤n} into a graded root. Negative definiteness ensures finite sublevels and eventual connectedness; local minima of the root correspond to special lattice cycles. In the almost-rational case, all relevant local minima are represented by the slice minimizers x(i), and the whole graded root is recovered from the sequence χ(x(i)).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Even if Heegaard Floer theory is not used, the idea of studying connected components of quadratic-form sublevel sets may reveal extremal vertices or cycles. An irreducible vertex could be sought among cycles representing local minima or unavoidable transitions in such a filtration.

proof_techniques.name: Dual-basis sign and inverse-matrix positivity after sign reversal
proof_techniques.description: For the negative definite plumbing form, the dual basis satisfies −g_j≥0. This is an inverse-matrix sign theorem for the intersection matrix. It is used implicitly to control rational cycles and anti-nef representatives.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original positive definite form with off-diagonal −1 along edges, the inverse matrix should have nonnegative entries. Such positivity can be useful for choosing extremal vertices or proving that certain coefficient patterns in a putative decomposition cannot occur.

other_useful_info: The paper works in the negative definite plumbing convention: diagonal entries are e_j and edge intersections are +1, and the bad-vertex inequality is the failure of −e_j≥δ_j. The original problem’s lattice is the sign-reversal of this convention. The paper itself does not discuss irreducible lattice elements, but its one-bad-vertex framework, Artin rationality criterion, and Laufer computation sequences are directly aligned with the original hypothesis. Relevant cited sources for the combinatorial singularity-theory tools are Artin [1,2] for rational graphs and Laufer [5,6] for computation sequences and elliptic/rational cycle algorithms.
why_relevant: Develops graded-root and lattice methods for negative-definite plumbed trees, including the bad-vertex framework. The Laufer-sequence style arguments are useful for extracting combinatorial consequences from definiteness.

title: Knots with unknotting number one and Heegaard Floer homology
overall_summary: The paper develops Heegaard Floer correction-term obstructions to a knot having unknotting number one, especially for alternating knots where the obstruction can be expressed entirely in terms of the negative-definite Goeritz lattice of an alternating diagram. The core technique combines Montesinos' description of the branched double cover of an unknotting-number-one knot as half-integral surgery, Ozsváth-Szabó correction-term inequalities for negative-definite four-manifolds, and explicit computation of correction terms from definite graph lattices via characteristic covectors. The paper also proves and uses sharpness results for negative-definite four-manifolds bounding L-spaces, allowing Goeritz/plumbing forms to compute correction terms exactly. For the original weighted-tree lattice problem, the most relevant extracted content is the paper's use of definite graph/plumbing lattices, characteristic-vector maximization, coordinate-improving moves in definite lattices, and the sharpness/Goeritz-lattice framework; the paper does not itself formulate irreducibility of lattice vertices, but it supplies closely related lattice tools and conventions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition: lattice correction term M_Q
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be a free abelian group of finite rank equipped with a negative-definite integral symmetric bilinear form Q: V ⊗ V → Z. Let q: V → V* = Hom(V,Z) be the homomorphism q(x)(y)=Q(x,y), and suppose Coker(q) is finite of odd order. A covector K ∈ V* is characteristic if K(x) ≡ Q(x,x) mod 2 for every x ∈ V. Choose v0 ∈ V such that q(v0) is characteristic. For ξ ∈ Coker(q), define M_Q(ξ) by 4 M_Q(ξ) = max over all v ∈ V* with [v]=ξ in Coker(q) of Q*(q(v0)+2v, q(v0)+2v) + rank(V), where Q* is the induced rational-valued form on V* obtained by inverting Q over Q. Since Q is negative-definite, the maximum exists; since Coker(q) has odd order, the function is independent of the choice of v0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper gives this as a definition with the two key justifications. Negative-definiteness implies that within any affine coset of q(V) in V*, the rational square Q*(K,K) tends to −∞ as the covector goes to infinity, so a maximum exists. The odd-order hypothesis implies that the mod-2 obstruction is invertible enough to choose v0 with q(v0) characteristic and that changing v0 only translates the set of covectors being maximized over; hence the maximum attached to a class ξ is unchanged. This construction is the purely lattice-theoretic quantity later identified with Heegaard Floer correction terms for Goeritz and plumbing lattices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.5, Montesinos trick
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If K ⊂ S^3 is a knot with unknotting number u(K)=1 and determinant D, then the double cover Σ(K) of S^3 branched over K is diffeomorphic to S^3_{±D/2}(C) for some knot C ⊂ S^3. Here S^3_{r}(C) denotes Dehn surgery on C with slope r, and the sign depends on the crossing-change convention and orientation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this classical result of Montesinos. The idea is to view a crossing change that unknots K as replacing a rational tangle in a 3-ball. Taking the double branched cover of the tangle decomposition converts the local crossing-change operation into Dehn surgery on the lift of the unknotting arc. Since the target knot after the crossing change is the unknot, whose branched double cover is S^3, the original branched double cover is obtained by half-integral surgery on a knot C in S^3. The numerator of the surgery coefficient is the determinant D = |H_1(Σ(K);Z)|.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1, correction-term bound for negative-definite fillings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Y be a rational homology 3-sphere, and let W be a smooth compact oriented 4-manifold with boundary Y whose intersection form is negative-definite. For every Spin^c structure s on W, with restriction s|Y to Y, the Heegaard Floer correction term d(Y,s|Y) satisfies c1(s)^2 + b2(W) ≡ 4 d(Y,s|Y) mod 2 and c1(s)^2 + b2(W) ≤ 4 d(Y,s|Y).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is recalled from Ozsváth-Szabó's general correction-term theory. When b1(W)=0, remove a 4-ball from W and regard the result as a cobordism from S^3 to Y. For a negative-definite cobordism between rational homology spheres with b1=0, the induced map on HF^∞ in each Spin^c structure is an isomorphism. The absolute grading shift of the cobordism map is Δ = (c1(s)^2 − 2χ − 3σ)/4; for a negative-definite filling this grading shift becomes (c1(s)^2+b2(W))/4 up to the normalization d(S^3)=0. Since d(Y,t) is the minimal grading in the image of HF^∞ → HF^+, this gives the inequality. The congruence is the compatibility of the absolute Q-grading with the mod-2 grading. If b1(W)>0, the paper notes that one can perform surgery on W to reduce to the b1=0 case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2, Heegaard Floer surgery exact triangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a compact 3-manifold with torus boundary. Let γ0, γ1, γ2 be three simple closed curves on ∂M with algebraic intersection numbers #(γ0∩γ1)=#(γ1∩γ2)=#(γ2∩γ0)=−1, where ∂M is oriented as the boundary of M. Let Yi be the closed 3-manifold obtained by Dehn filling M along γi. Then the natural two-handle cobordisms between these fillings induce a long exact sequence ... → HF^+(Y0) → HF^+(Y1) → HF^+(Y2) → ... .
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard Ozsváth-Szabó surgery long exact sequence. In this paper it is used as a black-box structural input. The curves γ0,γ1,γ2 form a surgery triad; attaching a two-handle changing one filling to the next gives cobordisms Wi: Yi → Yi+1, and the sum over Spin^c structures on each cobordism gives the maps in the exact triangle. This exact triangle is the main inductive mechanism for proving that branched double covers of alternating links are L-spaces and that their Goeritz four-manifolds are sharp.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4, L-spaces from an exact triangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose Y0, Y1, and Y2 are rational homology 3-spheres related by a surgery exact triangle as in Theorem 2.2. Suppose the number of elements in H1(Y1;Z) is greater than the number of elements in H1(Y0;Z) and greater than the number of elements in H1(Y2;Z). If Y0 and Y2 are L-spaces, meaning that for every Spin^c structure t on Yi the map HF^∞(Yi,t) → HF^+(Yi,t) is surjective, then Y1 is also an L-space.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is described as a straightforward application of the exact triangle and the standard structure HF^∞(Y,t) ≅ F[U,U^{-1}] for rational homology spheres. For rational homology spheres, the Euler characteristic and tower contributions are controlled by |H1(Y;Z)|. If the two outer terms Y0 and Y2 have no reduced Floer homology, exactness forces the middle term to have the minimal possible Floer rank/tower structure compatible with its first homology order. Therefore HF^+ for Y1 is also generated entirely by the images of HF^∞ in each Spin^c structure, so Y1 is an L-space.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.5 and Proposition 2.6, sharp four-manifolds and exact-triangle propagation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Y be an L-space. A smooth compact 4-manifold X bounding −Y is called sharp if b1(X)=0, the intersection form on H2(X,Y;Z) is negative-definite, and for every Spin^c structure t on Y there is an extension s over X such that c1(s)^2 + b2(X) = −4 d(Y,t).
