Now suppose Y0,Y1,Y2 are rational homology 3-spheres related by a surgery exact triangle, with natural two-handle cobordisms Wi: Yi → Yi+1. Suppose Y0 and Y2 are L-spaces. If X2 is sharp for Y2 and X0 = W0 ∪ W1 ∪ X2 is sharp for Y0, then X1 = W1 ∪ X2 is sharp for Y1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof introduces, for a negative-definite X bounding −Y, an F[U]-module H^+(Y;X) consisting of functions φ: Spin^c(X) → HF^+(S^3) satisfying the relation U^{(⟨c1(s),v⟩+v·v)/2} φ(s+PD(v)) = φ(s) whenever v ∈ H2(X;Z) and the exponent is nonnegative. The cobordism X−B^4 induces a map T_X^+: HF^+(Y) → H^+(Y;X). Sharpness is equivalent to T_X^+ being an isomorphism. Lemma 2.7 identifies H^+(Y;X), as an ungraded F[U]-module, with a direct sum of towers (F[U,U^{-1}]/F[U])^n, where n is the number of boundary Spin^c structures extending over X; this is proved by choosing maximal-square extensions and using the defining U-relations to recover all values. Lemma 2.8 proves that the map H^+(Y0;W0∪W1∪X2) → H^+(Y1;W1∪X2) induced by W0 is injective: for a nonzero function φ, multiply by a power of U so Uφ=0, choose a Spin^c structure with nonzero value, adjust along the kernel generated by the relevant two-handle classes and a square −1 class so that exactly one term survives in the sum defining the induced map. With these lemmas, one forms a commutative diagram whose top row is the Floer exact sequence and whose bottom row is the corresponding sequence of H^+-modules. The vertical maps for X0 and X2 are isomorphisms by sharpness. A diagram chase gives injectivity of T_X1^+, and the tower-rank comparison from Lemma 2.7 promotes injectivity to an isomorphism. Hence X1 is sharp.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1, correction-term obstruction for arbitrary unknotting-number-one knots
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a knot with unknotting number one and determinant D. Let γ_D: Z/DZ → Q be the function M_R for the negative-definite rank-two form R represented, when D=2n−1, by the matrix [[−n,1],[1,−2]], with the paper's fixed identification of Coker(R) with Z/DZ. Then there exists an isomorphism φ: Z/DZ → H^2(Σ(K);Z) such that one of the following two alternatives holds for every i ∈ Z/DZ: either γ_D(i) ≡ d(Σ(K),φ(i)) mod 2 and γ_D(i) ≤ d(Σ(K),φ(i)), or γ_D(i) ≡ −d(Σ(K),φ(i)) mod 2 and γ_D(i) ≤ −d(Σ(K),φ(i)). Here d denotes the Heegaard Floer correction term, and Spin^c structures on Σ(K) are identified with H^2(Σ(K);Z) using c1/2, possible because |H^2(Σ(K);Z)|=D is odd.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Montesinos' lemma, Σ(K) is diffeomorphic to either +S^3_{D/2}(C) or −S^3_{D/2}(C) for some knot C. Equivalently, one of ±Σ(K) bounds a negative-definite 4-manifold W whose intersection form is the rank-two form R = [[−n,1],[1,−2]]. The boundary cohomology is cyclic of order D, giving the isomorphism φ. Applying Theorem 2.1 to W gives, for every Spin^c extension, the mod-2 congruence and the inequality between c1(s)^2+b2(W) and the correction term of the boundary. Maximizing c1(s)^2+b2(W) over extensions in a fixed boundary Spin^c structure is exactly the lattice quantity γ_D. If the relevant boundary orientation is Σ(K), one obtains the first set of inequalities; if it is −Σ(K), the sign change d(−Y,t)=−d(Y,t) gives the second.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2, alternating branched double covers and Goeritz correction terms
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an alternating knot, and let Q be the negative-definite Goeritz form associated to a regular alternating projection of K with the paper's checkerboard coloring convention. Let q be the homomorphism induced by Q. Then Σ(K), the double cover of S^3 branched over K, is an L-space. Moreover, there is an affine identification φ: Coker(q) → Spin^c(Σ(K)) taking 0 to the unique spin structure such that M_Q(ξ) = d(Σ(K),φ(ξ)) for every ξ ∈ Coker(q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is sketched in the paper. From a connected alternating projection of a link L, construct a 4-manifold X_L bounding Σ(L): for all but one vertex of the black graph add a one-handle, and for each crossing add a −1-framed two-handle linking the relevant dotted unknots. After handleslides corresponding to circuits in the white graph, the intersection form of X_L is the Goeritz form Q. Choose a crossing such that both resolutions L0 and L1 remain connected. The branched double covers −Σ(L0), −Σ(L), and −Σ(L1) fit into the surgery exact triangle. Induction on the number of crossings, starting from the unknot, and Proposition 2.4 show that Σ(L) is an L-space. The same induction, using the relation W0∪W1∪X_{L1}=X_{L0}#CPbar^2 and Proposition 2.6, shows that the Goeritz 4-manifold is sharp. Sharpness says that for each boundary Spin^c structure the correction term is obtained by maximizing c1(s)^2+b2 over all extensions, which is precisely the lattice maximization defining M_Q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1, alternating unknotting-number-one obstruction from Goeritz lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be an alternating knot with determinant D, and let Q be the negative-definite Goeritz form of a regular alternating projection. Let q be the map induced by Q. For D=2n−1, let γ_D be the function M_R associated to the rank-two form R = [[−n,1],[1,−2]], using the paper's fixed identification Z/DZ ≅ Coker(R). Given an isomorphism φ: Z/DZ → Coker(q) and a sign ε ∈ {±1}, define T_{φ,ε}: Z/DZ → Q by T_{φ,ε}(i)=−ε M_Q(φ(i))−γ_D(i). If u(K)=1, then there are φ and ε such that for every i ∈ Z/DZ, T_{φ,ε}(i) ≡ 0 mod 2 and T_{φ,ε}(i) ≥ 0. Moreover, if |M_Q(0)| ≤ 1/2 and D=4k−1 or D=4k+1, then φ and ε can be chosen so that T_{φ,ε}(i)=T_{φ,ε}(2k−i) for 1≤i<k when D=4k−1, and for 0≤i<k when D=4k+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first two conditions are obtained by combining Theorem 3.1 with Proposition 3.2. Theorem 3.1 gives correction-term inequalities for Σ(K) or −Σ(K) in terms of γ_D, while Proposition 3.2 identifies d(Σ(K),·) with the Goeritz-lattice function M_Q. The sign ε records whether the relevant orientation is Σ(K) or its reverse; reflecting K reverses the sign of correction terms. For the symmetry, write Σ(K)=S^3_{−D/2}(C) after choosing orientation/reflection. Since K is alternating, Σ(K) is an L-space by Proposition 3.2. The paper observes that γ_D(i)=d(S^3_{−D/2}(O),i), where O is the unknot. The hypothesis |M_Q(0)|≤1/2 ensures equality of the spin correction terms for S^3_{−D/2}(C) and S^3_{−D/2}(O), which is the hypothesis needed in Theorem 4.1. Theorem 4.1 then gives the stated symmetry for the difference of the correction terms, which is exactly T_{φ,ε}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1, symmetry for L-space half-integer surgeries
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let O be the unknot, and let C ⊂ S^3 be a knot. Suppose n>1, Y_C=S^3_{−(2n−1)/2}(C) is an L-space, and the spin correction terms agree: d(Y_C,0)=d(S^3_{−(2n−1)/2}(O),0), where 0 denotes the spin Spin^c structure under the identification by c1/2. Choose an orientation of C, giving a natural isomorphism φ: Z/(2n−1)Z → H^2(Y_C;Z) from the surgery cobordism. Write n=2k or n=2k+1. Then the correction-term difference δ(i)=d(S^3_{−(2n−1)/2}(C),φ(i))−d(S^3_{−(2n−1)/2}(O),φ(i)) satisfies δ(i)=δ(2k−i) for i=1,...,k when n=2k, and for i=0,...,k when n=2k+1, with the ranges as stated in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof factors the negative half-integer surgery cobordism through the negative integer surgery S^3_{−n}(C). The full cobordism has intersection form [[−n,1],[1,−2]]. The paper writes explicit lists of characteristic covectors κ_i for this rank-two form; their restrictions are Spin^c structures w_i on S^3_{−(2n−1)/2}(C) and v_i on S^3_{−n}(C). The key intermediate result, Theorem 4.2, states that under the spin equality hypothesis, for every i the half-integer correction-term difference at w_i equals the integer-surgery correction-term difference at v_i. Theorem 4.2 is proved by first obtaining inequalities 0 ≤ d(S^3_{−n}(C),v_i)−d(S^3_{−n}(O),v_i) ≤ d(S^3_{−(2n−1)/2}(C),w_i)−d(S^3_{−(2n−1)/2}(O),w_i) from the negative-definite cobordism inequality and sharpness for the unknot; then using the fact that half-integer L-space surgery implies integer L-space surgery; and finally summing the inequalities and applying an Euler-characteristic identity for total correction terms to force equality term-by-term. Once the difference is transferred to integer surgery, the relevant pairs v_i and v_{2k−i} are conjugate Spin^c structures, and correction terms are invariant under conjugation. This gives the asserted symmetry.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 6 finite maximizer bound for Goeritz matrices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be an m×m negative-definite matrix representing a Goeritz form Q in a chosen basis, so V* is identified with Z^m and Q* is represented by G^{-1}. In computing M_Q, two dual vectors v1,v2 ∈ Z^m represent the same element of Coker(q) exactly when G^{-1}(v1−v2) ∈ Z^m. A characteristic vector K=(K1,...,Km) satisfies Ki ≡ Gii mod 2 for each i. The paper uses the following finite-search bound: any characteristic vector that maximizes Q*(K,K) within its equivalence class can be chosen from the finite set of K with |Ki| ≤ |Gii| and Ki ≡ Gii mod 2 for all i. If a characteristic vector lies outside this set, there is an equivalent characteristic vector with strictly larger Q*-square.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Although stated briefly in the paper as straightforward, the coordinate-improvement argument is explicit. Equivalent characteristic covectors differ by adding or subtracting 2q(e_i), where e_i is a basis element and q(e_i) is the ith row/column covector of G. If Ki > −Gii, then K' = K + 2q(e_i) is equivalent and characteristic, and Q*(K',K')−Q*(K,K)=4(Ki+Gii)>0 because Gii<0. If Ki < Gii, then K' = K − 2q(e_i) gives Q*(K',K')−Q*(K,K)=4(−Ki+Gii)>0. Thus no maximizing representative can have Ki outside the interval [Gii,−Gii], i.e. |Ki|≤|Gii|. This produces a finite list of characteristic covectors sufficient for computing all values of M_Q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 7.1 recalled plumbing criterion from Ozsváth-Szabó [24]
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a Seifert fibered rational homology 3-sphere Y presented by a negative-definite plumbing diagram, let V be the lattice generated by the vertices with preferred vertex basis, let Q be the plumbing intersection form, and let q:V→V* be induced by Q. Section 7.1 recalls an algorithm from Ozsváth-Szabó [24]: consider characteristic covectors in V* modulo the standard length-preserving vertex moves at preferred basis vectors when the evaluation on that vertex is extremal; equivalently, one follows finite equivalence classes of characteristic vectors with vertex-wise evaluations bounded by the magnitudes of the negative self-intersections. Let X be the set of such bounded equivalence classes. The number of elements of X is the rank of Ker(U) in HF^+(−Y). Since |Coker(q)| is |H^2(Y;Z)|, if |X|=|Coker(q)|, then Y is an L-space. Under this same circumstance, the map M_Q: Coker(q)→Q agrees, under a suitable identification of Coker(q) with Spin^c(Y), with the correction terms of −Y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reprove the plumbing algorithm; it cites [24] and applies it in examples. The underlying mechanism is combinatorial: characteristic covectors are transformed by vertex moves that preserve their rational square Q*(K,K), and the allowed full paths terminate in bounded covectors precisely when they contribute to Ker(U) in the plumbed-manifold Floer complex. If the number of terminal/bounded equivalence classes is as small as possible, namely one for each boundary Spin^c structure, then the reduced Floer homology vanishes and the manifold is an L-space. In that case, the same maximization over characteristic covectors in each Coker(q)-class gives the correction term, matching the definition of M_Q.

proof_techniques.name: Characteristic-covector maximization in definite lattices
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly converts Floer correction-term questions into finite maximization problems in a negative-definite lattice. Given a definite integral form Q, one studies characteristic covectors K and maximizes Q*(K,K)+rank(Q) in each Coker(q)-class. In concrete Goeritz computations, the coordinate-improving move K ↦ K ± 2q(e_i) shows that a maximizing characteristic vector must satisfy a coordinate bound |Ki|≤|Gii|, turning an a priori infinite search into a finite one.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is about a definite graph lattice. Similar coordinate-improving moves along vertex rows may help prove structural statements about vertices by showing that certain hypothetical decompositions or extremal configurations can be improved unless a special vertex exists.

proof_techniques.name: Graph forms as intersection forms of plumbed or Goeritz four-manifolds
proof_techniques.description: For an alternating diagram, the paper constructs a 4-manifold bounding the branched double cover whose intersection form is the Goeritz form of the checkerboard graph. More generally, in the plumbing discussion, vertices of a graph give a preferred basis for a lattice, diagonal entries are self-intersections, and edges record pairings. This translates graph-lattice statements into the language of negative-definite 4-manifold intersection forms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The weighted tree lattice in the original problem is exactly a graph-defined definite form, up to sign conventions. Treating it as a plumbing lattice may allow use of known moves on vertices, blowups/blowdowns, or induction on tree operations.

proof_techniques.name: Sharpness as equality in all correction-term inequalities
proof_techniques.description: The correction-term inequality c1(s)^2+b2≤4d(Y,t) becomes an exact formula when the bounding negative-definite 4-manifold is sharp. The paper proves sharpness for alternating Goeritz four-manifolds by induction using the surgery exact triangle and then identifies d-invariants with the lattice function M_Q. Sharpness is therefore a bridge from topology back to exact lattice maximization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the tree lattice can be realized as a sharp plumbing or compared to one, equality cases in correction-term inequalities may impose strong combinatorial restrictions on how basis vertices can decompose.

proof_techniques.name: Induction through exact triangles and graph resolutions
proof_techniques.description: The proof that alternating branched double covers are L-spaces and that their Goeritz forms are sharp proceeds by resolving a crossing, relating the three branched double covers by a surgery exact triangle, and using induction on the number of crossings. The corresponding four-manifolds differ by adding two-handle cobordisms and, in one branch, by a blowup.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, an analogous induction might remove a leaf, contract an edge, or alter a vertex weight while tracking definiteness and the bad-vertex condition. The paper's strategy suggests proving a lattice property by showing it survives elementary graph modifications.

proof_techniques.name: Length-preserving vertex moves in plumbing lattices
proof_techniques.description: In the recalled plumbing algorithm from [24], characteristic covectors are organized by vertex moves that preserve their square and are triggered when the evaluation on a vertex reaches an extremal value. The bounded terminal classes govern Ker(U) and, in L-space cases, correction terms. This is a combinatorial procedure entirely in the vertex lattice once the plumbing graph is fixed.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis w(v)<d(v) is a graph-theoretic imbalance reminiscent of bad vertices in negative-definite plumbing theory. Vertex-move or full-path arguments may provide a way to exploit the unique bad vertex to force existence of an irreducible vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Using conjugation symmetry to force lattice-function symmetries
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the conjugation symmetry d(Y,s)=d(Y,conj(s)) of Heegaard Floer correction terms, together with explicit identifications of Spin^c structures through surgery cobordisms, to derive symmetries of difference functions such as T_{φ,ε}. Although topological in origin, once correction terms are identified with M_Q, the result becomes a symmetry statement for lattice maximization functions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a tree lattice admits an involution or a natural pairing of characteristic classes, conjugation-type symmetry could be translated into constraints on vertex configurations or decompositions.

proof_techniques.name: Donaldson-style obstruction philosophy via definite forms
proof_techniques.description: Although Donaldson's theorem is not explicitly the central theorem in this paper, the method has the same flavor: realize a 3-manifold as the boundary of two definite 4-manifolds, compare their intersection forms through correction-term constraints, and derive arithmetic obstructions from the resulting lattice data. The rank-two form [[−n,1],[1,−2]] plays the role of a model lattice forced by half-integral surgery.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is a purely lattice-theoretic component of the broader definite-form obstruction framework. One possible route is to argue by contradiction that if all vertices were reducible, the tree lattice would fail a definiteness or embedding constraint analogous to those used in the paper.

other_useful_info: Section 6 gives practical matrix conventions that may be useful independently of knots: for a Goeritz matrix G, the dual form is v^T G^{-1} w, and v1,v2 ∈ Z^m represent the same Coker(q)-class exactly when G^{-1}(v1−v2) is integral. The paper's most directly relevant cited source for weighted plumbed trees and bad-vertex algorithms is Ozsváth-Szabó, On the Floer homology of plumbed three-manifolds [24]; the present paper only recalls the needed plumbing criterion in Section 7.1. Proposition 3.2 and the surrounding proof explain how definite graph lattices arise naturally from checkerboard graphs and how sharpness turns correction-term calculations into exact lattice maximization. The paper uses negative-definite conventions, whereas the original problem uses a positive-definite tree with off-diagonal −1; sign reversal will be necessary when comparing conventions.
why_relevant: Develops the original Heegaard-Floer obstruction using definite Goeritz forms and lattice correction terms. It is useful background for the Donaldson-plus-lattice embedding techniques used in changemaker proofs.
